context: Look in Hebrews chapter 12, if you will. And it's a familiar passage of scripture here.
point_summary: Divine chastening is a mark of God's love, not his anger — those he loves, he chastens and scourges, making correction a reason for rejoicing rather than despair.

context: In verse number four, the Bible says this, you have not yet resisted unto blood's trials, against sin
point_summary: God's chastening of believers is presented as a birthmark and evidence of salvation, distinguishing true sons from those who are not His.

context: and you've forgotten the exhortation which speaketh unto you as unto children. My son, despise not thou the chastening of the Lord, nor faint when thou art rebuked of him
point_summary: Divine chastening is presented as a birthmark of genuine salvation — evidence that God deals with believers as sons rather than leaving them to their own path.

context: For whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth and scourgeth every son whom he receiveth
point_summary: Divine chastening is presented as a birthmark of genuine salvation — evidence that God deals with believers as sons rather than leaving them to their own path.

context: If ye endure chastening, God dealeth with you as with sons. For what son is he whom the Father chasteneth not?
point_summary: Enduring God's chastening is a birthmark and evidence of genuine salvation, since the Father disciplines only those who are truly his sons.

context: Shall we not much rather be in subjection unto the father of spirits and live?
point_summary: Earthly fathers who corrected their children and received reverence illustrate why believers should submit even more readily to the Father of spirits when He chastens them for their profit.

context: For they verily for a few days chastened us after their own pleasure. But notice this phrase, he for our profit, that we might be partakers of his holiness.
point_summary: God's chastening of believers is purposeful rather than punitive, designed specifically for their profit and to make them partakers of his holiness.

context: Now, no chastening for the present seemeth to be joyous. Listen, if you've ever experienced the chastening hand of God, you will agree wholeheartedly with verse number 11. It's not joyous.
point_summary: Chastening from God is not joyous in the moment but yields the peaceable fruit of righteousness afterward, confirming that divine discipline is purposeful and profitable for those who receive it.

context: in Hebrews 7 in verse number 14 when the Bible's speaking about the priesthood, it says that we know that Jesus, He didn't come from Levi. No, He came from Judah, from the tribe of Judah.
point_summary: Hebrews 7:14 confirms that Jesus descended from Judah, not Levi, establishing his identity as the royal fulfillment of Jacob's blessing over the tribe of Judah.

context: for as much then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, he also himself took part of the same. That through death, notice what he did, he might destroy him that had the power of death, that is, the devil
point_summary: Christ's incarnation—taking on flesh and blood—was purposeful: through his death he destroyed the devil's power over death and freed those enslaved by the fear of it.

context: for verily, he took not on him the nature of angels, why not? Because he couldn't die and provide propitiation for our sins if he was in the form of an angel. He had to come in the likeness of men. So it tells us at the end of verse number 16, but he took on him the seed of Abraham.
point_summary: Christ took on human flesh rather than angelic nature precisely because only as a man could he die and serve as the propitiation for sin.

context: Wherefore in all things it behooved him to be made like unto his brethren, why? That he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people.
point_summary: Christ had to become fully human—not angelic—so that he could serve as merciful high priest and make propitiation for sins, the very act in which God's love is revealed.

context: For in that he himself hath suffered being tempted, he is able to succor them that are tempted.
point_summary: Because Christ became flesh and suffered temptation, Hebrews 2:18 is cited to show he qualifies as a merciful high priest able to help those who are tempted, grounding his propitiatory work in genuine human experience.

context: let your conversation be without covetousness and be content with such things as ye have. For he hath said, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee
point_summary: God's promise never to leave nor forsake his people produces bold confidence — "the Lord is my helper, I will not fear what man shall do unto me" — which directly answers the enemies' taunt that there is no help for David in God.

context: so that we may boldly say, the Lord is my helper and I will not fear what man shall do unto me
point_summary: God's promise never to leave or forsake His people produces bold, fearless confidence — "the Lord is my helper; I will not fear what man shall do unto me" — which directly answers the enemies' taunt that there is no help for David in God.

context: you said that you'd never leave us nor forsake us
point_summary: God's promise never to leave nor forsake his people—cited from Hebrews 13:5—is offered as one anchor among several divine assurances that displace fear and ground the believer's rest.

context: the Bible tells us as it appointed unto man once to die but after this the judgment.
point_summary: Death is universal and certain — appointed once for every person — and what follows it is judgment, as God's authoritative Word reveals.

context: The Bible says, as I quoted just a moment ago in Hebrews 9 and verse number 27, as it is appointed unto man once to die, after this the judgment.
point_summary: Death is appointed once for every person, making readiness for that certain moment — beginning with assurance of salvation — an urgent, non-negotiable priority.

context: Look at Hebrews 12 in closing. Verses that we're familiar with, no doubt.
point_summary: Hebrews 11's "hall of faith" is read as a record of individuals fulfilling God's unique plan for their lives, setting the stage for Hebrews 12's call to finish one's own God-given course.

context: In Hebrews chapter 12, it's interesting for in chapter number 11, if you're familiar with Hebrews 11, we often will refer to the chapter as being the hall of faith chapter.
point_summary: Hebrews 11's "hall of faith" is presented not merely as a record of heroic deeds but as a gallery of individuals each fulfilling the specific plan God had for their lives.

context: wherefore, seeing we also are compassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses, let us lay aside every weight, and the sin which does so easily beset us, notice what he says, and let us run with patience the race that is before us.
point_summary: The "cloud of witnesses" in Hebrews 12:1 represents saints of old who fulfilled God's individual plan for their lives, and their example calls believers to lay aside every weight and run their own appointed race.

context: looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith, who for the joy that was set before Him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God.
point_summary: Fixing one's gaze on Jesus as "author and finisher" is presented as the essential posture for enduring the race and fulfilling the specific plan God has assigned to each believer.

context: For consider Him that endured such contradiction of sinners against Himself, lest ye be wearied and faint in your minds.
point_summary: Hebrews 12:3 is cited as the warning against growing weary, with Jesus' endurance of sinners' contradiction held up as the fixed focus that enables believers to finish God's plan for their lives.

context: Just like he said in Hebrews 11 and verse number 13, we are strangers and we are pilgrims.
point_summary: Believers' identity as strangers and pilgrims (Heb. 11:13) grounds the conviction that this world system is not their home, but that they are heading toward one.

context: Consider Hebrews 13. Turn over there if you will and consider Hebrews 13 and what the word of God says with regard to our praise for our God.
point_summary: Hebrews 13:15 is cited to show that the believer's fitting response to the gift of salvation is a continual, Spirit-enabled sacrifice of praise and thanksgiving to God.

context: And here in Hebrews 13 and verse number 15, the Bible speaks here about the praise of his people. By him, therefore, let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God. How often? Continually. That is the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name.
point_summary: Hebrews 13:15's call to offer "the sacrifice of praise continually" is cited as the fitting response to God's unearned gift of eternal life — praise belonging solely to him, not to any human merit.

context: And so if we know the book of Hebrews in the 13th chapter says, the Lord is my helper.
point_summary: God's designation as "my helper" in Hebrews 13:6 grounds the sermon's call to seek divine aid rather than turning to earthly powers like Egypt.

context: for in the time that you ought to be teachers, you have need that one teach you
point_summary: Hebrews 5:12 is invoked as a negative example — believers who should have matured into teachers still needed basic instruction — to underscore that spiritual growth is imperative, not optional, for the church.

context: But we must be disciplined to keep our eyes on Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith.
point_summary: Keeping eyes fixed on Jesus amid adversity is grounded in Hebrews 12:2's description of Christ as "the author and finisher of our faith."

context: Hebrews 11 says this, that we're pilgrims and we're strangers
point_summary: Believers are pilgrims and strangers in this world, a truth invoked to frame John's exile on Patmos as the condition of one whose true citizenship is in heaven.

context: According to Hebrews seven and verse 25, he ever liveth to make intercession for us.
point_summary: Christ's priestly garment in the vision is connected to His ongoing intercessory work, with Hebrews 7:25 cited to affirm that He ever lives to make intercession for believers as their great high priest.

context: we find that in Hebrews chapter four and verse number 12, it says, for the Word of God is quick and powerful and sharper than a two-edged sword, piercing even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit and of the joints and marrow and as a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart.
point_summary: The sharp two-edged sword proceeding from Christ's mouth in Revelation 1:16 is identified as the Word of God—living, powerful, and penetrating—on the authority of Hebrews 4:12.

reference_text: He said he would never forsake us
context: He said he would never forsake us. And we understand this evening that he knows us.
point_summary: God's promise never to forsake His people (Hebrews 13:5) is the assurance that Joseph, though forgotten by the butler, was never forgotten by God.

context: I mentioned that this morning from Hebrews chapter number two
point_summary: Hebrews 2's teaching on the fear of death identifies a universal human anxiety that believers, equipped with Scripture, are uniquely positioned to address through the gospel.

context: first five verses in Hebrews chapter number eight
point_summary: Christ's superiority over all things—established throughout Hebrews 1–7—is the theological foundation on which chapter 8 opens, making verse 1 the capstone of that cumulative argument.

context: encouragement that we receive in Hebrews chapter number 12
point_summary: Hebrews 12:1's call to run the race with eyes fixed on Jesus serves as the year's guiding theme, grounding the entire series in Christ's superiority established throughout the preceding chapters.

context: The Bible says this now the things which we have spoken. This is the sum We have such an high priest who is set on the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens
point_summary: Hebrews 8:1 is presented as the summation of the preceding argument: Christ, as our high priest, is seated at the right hand of the Majesty in heaven, demonstrating his superiority over the Levitical priesthood.

context: a minister of the sanctuary and of the true Tabernacle, which the Lord pitched and not man
point_summary: Christ's priesthood is superior because he ministers in the true heavenly tabernacle—the one God himself pitched—of which the earthly Levitical sanctuary was only a shadow and pattern.

context: For every high priest is ordained to offer gifts and sacrifices Wherefore it is of necessity That this man have somewhat also to offer
point_summary: Every high priest must have an offering to present, establishing that Christ's priesthood requires him to have something to offer—a necessity the sermon identifies as fulfilled in his own sacrifice.

context: for if he Jesus were on earth He should not be a priest seeing that there are priests that offer gifts
point_summary: Christ's earthly ministry disqualified him from the Levitical priesthood, which still functioned at the time of writing, demonstrating that his priesthood operates on an entirely different, heavenly order.

context: who serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things, as Moses was admonished of God when he was about to make the tabernacle: for, See, saith he, that thou make all things according to the pattern shewed to thee in the mount.
point_summary: The Mosaic tabernacle and its priestly service were constructed according to a heavenly pattern, making them a shadow pointing to the superior, true sanctuary where Christ now ministers.

context: You notice in verse number 1 of chapter 8. It's where I've adopted the title this morning He says we have such and high priest
point_summary: The declaration "we have such a high priest" is presented as the sermon's controlling theme, assuring Jewish believers that Christ fulfills and surpasses the entire Old Testament priestly system they already understood.

context: and then again in Hebrews 9 as some of those things Are dealt with
point_summary: The tabernacle's physical details — including its single entrance — are treated as typological pointers to Christ, with Hebrews 9 named as the passage where those correspondences will be developed.

context: In fact the priests they daily ministered we read of that in Hebrews chapter number 10
point_summary: The daily priestly sacrifices described in Hebrews 10 are cited as background to the tabernacle's sacrificial system, which the sermon treats as a type pointing forward to Christ as the singular way to God.

context: as we study these first five verses of Hebrews chapter number eight
point_summary: The phrase "we have such a high priest" anchors the sermon's central claim that Jewish believers, who understood priestly ministry and sacrifice, now possess in Christ the perfect fulfillment of everything the tabernacle's system foreshadowed.

context: At the end of chapter number seven, you find that in verse number 27, it talks about the fact that they had to first offer for their own sins.
point_summary: Unlike the Levitical priests who had to offer sacrifices first for their own sins, Jesus as the sinless Son of God needed no such offering for himself before atoning for others.

context: wherefore in all things that behooved him to be made like unto his brethren that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God to make reconciliation for the sins of the people.
point_summary: Christ's incarnation—being made like his brethren in all things—qualified him to serve as a merciful and faithful high priest who makes reconciliation for sin and aids the tempted.

context: For in that he himself hath suffered being tempted, he is able to succor them that are tempted.
point_summary: Because Christ himself suffered temptation, Hebrews 2:18 establishes that he is able to come to the aid of those who are tempted—a mercy grounded in his incarnate experience.

context: wherefore holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling, consider the apostle
point_summary: Hebrews 3:1 is cited as one of several early references in the letter establishing that Jesus is the high priest of our profession, anticipating the fuller treatment of his priesthood in chapters 5–10.

context: seeing then that we have a great high priest that is passed into the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold fast our profession
point_summary: Hebrews 4:14 is cited as one of several anticipatory references in chapters 2–4 establishing Jesus as great high priest before the subject receives full treatment in chapters 5–10.

context: for we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities, but was in all points tempted like as we are yet without sin
point_summary: Christ's qualification as high priest rests on his having been tempted in all points as we are, yet remaining without sin, making him both sympathetic and perfectly suited to intercede.

context: our high priest ministers in a better place
point_summary: Christ's high priestly ministry is superior because he is seated at the right hand of God's throne in heaven itself—a better place than any earthly sanctuary.

context: now of the things which we have spoken, this is the sum, we have such an high priest who is set on the right hand of the throne of the majesty in the heavens
point_summary: Christ's present session at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens establishes that He ministers in a better place than any earthly temple or tabernacle.

context: who serve unto the example and shadow of heavenly things. Notice what the Bible continues to say here, as Moses was admonished of God when he was about to make the tabernacle. For see, saith he, that's God, that thou, that's Moses, make all things according to the pattern showed to thee in the mount.
point_summary: The Mosaic tabernacle was a divinely prescribed shadow and pattern of the true heavenly sanctuary where Christ now ministers, and its exact specifications were required precisely because of what it typified.

context: Hebrews 4 and verse number 14, the Bible says, seeing then that we have a great high priest that is passed into the heavens
point_summary: Christ's passing "into the heavens" as great High Priest surpasses the Levitical high priest, who could only pass through the earthly veil into the holy of holies once a year.

reference_text: chapter number 7, we studied this a few weeks ago, but in verse number 25
context: chapter number 7, we studied this a few weeks ago, but in verse number 25, the Bible says at the end of that verse
point_summary: Christ's present, unceasing intercession as the living High Priest in heaven—not an earthly tabernacle—grounds the believer's confidence in access to God.

context: for every high priest is ordained to offer gifts and sacrifices
point_summary: Every high priest is ordained to offer gifts and sacrifices, establishing the category that frames Christ's own offering as the superior fulfillment of that priestly requirement.

context: for every high priest taken from among men is ordained for men in things pertaining to God, that he may offer both gifts and sacrifices for sins
point_summary: Every Levitical high priest was ordained to offer gifts and sacrifices for sin, establishing the priestly pattern that Christ fulfills with a better offering.

context: how they would first offer for their own sins, then for the people's
point_summary: Unlike the Levitical priests who first offered sacrifices for their own sins before the people's, Jesus presented a single, unrepeated offering as the sinless high priest.

context: for the law having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things, can never with those sacrifices which they offered, notice this, year by year continually, make the comers thereunto perfect.
point_summary: The Old Testament sacrifices, repeated year by year and never able to make worshipers perfect, were only a shadow pointing forward to the one sufficient offering of Christ.

context: but in those sacrifices, there is a remembrance again made of sins every year
point_summary: The repeated annual sacrifices of the old covenant could never remove sins but served as a yearly reminder of sin's ongoing guilt, pointing forward to the Messiah who alone could accomplish what they could not.

context: for it is not possible the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins.
point_summary: The repeated animal sacrifices under the old covenant could never permanently remove sin but served as annual reminders pointing forward to the Messiah who alone would accomplish what they could not.

context: Wherefore, when he cometh into the world, he sayeth, sacrifice and offering thou wouldest not, but a body hast thou prepared for me.
point_summary: The repeated animal sacrifices of the Levitical system could never remove sins but served as annual reminders pointing forward to the Messiah, whose prepared body would be the one sufficient offering.

context: and every priest standeth daily ministering and offering, notice what it says, oftentimes the same sacrifices which can never take away sins.
point_summary: The daily, repetitive sacrifices of the Levitical priests, which could never take away sins, pointed forward to the Messiah who would one day come as the fulfillment they foreshadowed.

context: for every high priest is ordained to offer gifts and sacrifices. Wherefore, it is of necessity that this man have somewhat also to offer.
point_summary: Because every high priest must have an offering to present, Christ's role as our high priest made it an absolute necessity—not merely fitting—that He too would have something to offer.

context: for if He were on earth, He should not be a priest,
point_summary: Because Christ belonged to the tribe of Judah rather than Levi, an earthly priesthood was impossible for Him, making His heavenly, once-for-all offering a theological necessity.

context: for it is evident that our Lord sprang out of Judah, of which tribe Moses spake nothing concerning priesthood.
point_summary: Jesus' descent from Judah, not Levi, disqualified Him from an earthly Levitical priesthood, necessitating a different order under which He could serve as both king and priest.

context: if He were on earth, He should not be a priest, seeing that there are priests that offer gifts according to the law
point_summary: Because Jesus was from the tribe of Judah rather than Levi, He could not serve as an earthly priest under the Mosaic law, but fulfills priesthood after the order of Melchizedek.

context: who needeth not daily as those high priests to offer up sacrifice first for his own sins and then for the people's notice the latter part for this he Christ did once when he offered up himself
point_summary: Unlike the Levitical priests who offered daily sacrifices for their own sins and the people's, Christ's priesthood after the order of Melchizedek required only one self-offering, sufficient and unrepeatable.

context: but Christ being come in high priest of good things to come by a greater and more perfect tabernacle not made with hands that is to say not of this building
point_summary: Christ's priesthood surpasses the Levitical order because He entered the true, heavenly tabernacle not made with hands, offering His own blood rather than that of animals.

context: let me read verses 11 through 14
point_summary: Christ's self-offering as high priest after the order of Melchizedek surpasses the repeated Levitical sacrifices because He entered the holy place once by His own blood, obtaining eternal redemption.

context: but by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place having obtained eternal redemption for us
point_summary: Christ's singular, once-for-all sacrifice for sins—contrasted with the repeated daily offerings of the Levitical priests—is what secured eternal redemption and allowed him to sit down at the right hand of God.

context: for if the blood of bulls and of goats and the ashes on a heifer sprinkling the unclean sanctified through the purifying of the flesh
point_summary: The blood of bulls, goats, and a heifer could only purify the flesh externally, establishing a contrast that magnifies how much more Christ's own blood accomplishes for the conscience.

context: how much more shall the blood of Christ who through the eternal spirit offered himself without spot to God purge your conscience from dead works to serve the living God
point_summary: Christ's blood, unlike the repeated animal sacrifices of the Levitical priesthood, was offered once and accomplishes what those sacrifices could never do—purging the conscience from dead works to serve the living God.

context: for then must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world but now once in the end of the world have he appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all appearance to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself stands in deliberate contrast to the repeated, never-sufficient daily offerings of the Levitical priesthood.

context: so Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many and unto them that look for him shall he appear the second time without sin unto salvation
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all offering to bear the sins of many stands in deliberate contrast to the repeated, never-sufficient sacrifices of the Levitical priests, securing eternal redemption and anticipating his second appearing unto salvation.

context: about the priest standing and ministering daily those same sacrifices which could never take away sin
point_summary: Daily Levitical priests stood because their repeated sacrifices never finally removed sin, whereas Christ sat down—his one sacrifice accomplishing what theirs never could.

context: but you look at verse number 12 and the Bible says but this
point_summary: Christ's singular, finished sacrifice for sins—contrasted with the Levitical priests' endless daily offerings—is confirmed by his sitting down at God's right hand, signaling completed, unrepeatable atonement.

context: the Bible tells us as we read that passage of Scripture in Hebrews 9 in verse number 12 that what did that what did that sinless precious blood get eternal redemption for us
point_summary: Christ's shed blood, as the sinless and sufficient payment for sin, secured eternal redemption—satisfying sin's demands in a way no corruptible thing like gold or silver ever could.

context: we find that he continueth ever in Hebrews 7 in verse number 24 the Bible says but this man speaking again of Jesus because he continueth ever hath an unchangeable priesthood
point_summary: Christ's unending priesthood rests on his resurrection: because he conquered sin, death, and the grave, he continues forever, holding an unchangeable priesthood unlike the mortal Aaronic priests who died.

context: see as we think about the truth of Hebrews chapter 5
point_summary: Christ's eternal, unchangeable priesthood—grounded in His resurrection victory over sin, death, and the grave—is the fulfillment toward which the entire Aaronic sacrificial system pointed.

context: ...expounded upon in Hebrews 9 and Hebrews 10...
point_summary: Hebrews 9 and 10 are identified as the expository development of the two truths introduced in Hebrews 8—that Christ ministers in a better place and presented a better offering.

context: ...expounded upon in Hebrews 9 and Hebrews 10...
point_summary: Hebrews 9 and 10 are identified as the extended exposition of the two truths introduced in Hebrews 8—that Christ ministers in a better place and presented a better offering.

context: regard to fellowship and so on and so forth, and as we think about the truth of Hebrews chapter number 5 that we have in high priest that ministers in a better place, that presented a better offering,
point_summary: Hebrews 5 is invoked as background for the twin truths—Christ ministers in a better place and presented a better offering—which are then connected to 1 John 2's portrait of Jesus as our advocate with the Father.

context: Take your Bible, if you will, the book of Hebrews and we'll begin in the 12th chapter
point_summary: Hebrews 12 is introduced as the launching point for a sermon-wide journey through the book, framed under the theme of God's greatness as unsearchable yet revealed in Scripture.

context: Wherefore, seeing we also are compassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses, let us lay aside every weight and the sin which doth so easily beset us, and let us run with patience the race that is set before us,
point_summary: Hebrews 12:1 is read as the opening of a passage whose cumulative call—to lay aside every weight and run with patience—frames the sermon's broader theme of living in light of God's greatness.

context: looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith, who for the joy that was set before Him endured the cross, despising the shame,
point_summary: Hebrews 12:2 is read as the foundation for the call to "consider Christ," presenting Jesus as the one who endured the cross and now reigns, whose greatness is meant to impact how believers live.

context: For consider Him that endured such contradiction of sinners against Himself, lest ye be wearied and faint in your own minds.
point_summary: Hebrews 12:3's command to "consider Him" calls believers to deliberate, sustained meditation on Christ so that weariness and fainting of mind are overcome by refocusing attention on Him.

context: consider Christ. We need to consider Him. We need to think over, ponder, meditate, muse upon just how wonderful our Savior truly is. No doubt as we think about this admonition that is given here in Hebrews chapter 12
point_summary: Deliberate, sustained meditation on Christ—pondering His endurance of sinners' contradiction—is the God-given antidote to the weariness and mental fainting that competing distractions produce in the believer's life.

context: God, who at sundry times and in diverse manners, spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets
point_summary: God's progressive revelation through the prophets, cited here as the opening movement of Hebrews, establishes the backdrop against which Christ's superior and final word is about to be set.

context: God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets,
point_summary: God's progressive revelation through the prophets finds its culmination and surpassing fulfillment in the Son, who is creator, heir of all things, and the express image of the Father's glory.

context: Hath in these last days spoken unto us by his Son, whom he hath appointed heir of all things, by whom also he made the worlds;
point_summary: God's final and supreme revelation has come not through prophets but through His Son, who is appointed heir of all things and the very creator of the worlds.

context: Who being the brightness of his glory, and the express image of his person, and upholding all things by the word of his power, when he had by himself purged our sins,
point_summary: Christ is presented in Hebrews 1:3 as the brightness of God's glory, the express image of His person, the upholder of all things, the purger of sins, and the one now seated at the right hand of majesty—a compressed portrait of His full person and work.

context: upholding all things by the word of his power
point_summary: Hebrews 1:3 presents Christ as simultaneously Creator, Sustainer, and Redeemer — upholding all things by His word while also purging sins by Himself.

context: when He had by Himself purged our sins, notice, sat down on the right hand of the Majesty on high
point_summary: Hebrews 1:3 presents Christ as simultaneously Creator, Sustainer, and Redeemer—the last established by His once-for-all purging of sins and subsequent session at the Father's right hand.

context: but we see Jesus, who was made a little lower than the angels for the suffering of death, crowned with glory and honor, that He, by the grace of God, should taste death for every man.
point_summary: Christ's incarnation and suffering unto death — tasting death for every man — is presented as the ground of substitutionary atonement, confirming His role as Redeemer introduced in Hebrews 1.

context: For it became Him for whom are all things and by whom are all things in bringing many sons unto glory to make the captain of their salvation perfect through sufferings.
point_summary: Christ's suffering was the fitting and necessary path by which God perfected the "captain of salvation" and brought many sons to glory.

context: For both He that sanctifieth and they who are sanctified are all of one, for which cause He is not ashamed to call them brethren,
point_summary: Christ's sanctifying work in Hebrews 2:11 establishes His solidarity with believers—He who sanctifies and those sanctified are "all of one," making Him unashamed to call them brethren.

context: saying, I will declare thy name unto my brethren. In the midst of the church will I sing praise unto thee,
point_summary: Christ's declaration in Hebrews 2:12 — singing praise among His brethren — is read as evidence that the incarnate, victorious Redeemer identifies with the sanctified as family, not strangers.

context: and again, I will put my trust in Him. And again, behold, I and the children which God hath given me.
point_summary: Christ's incarnation and solidarity with "the children which God hath given me" is read as proof that He took on flesh and blood specifically to destroy the devil's power of death and deliver those in bondage to its fear.

context: For as much then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, He also Himself likewise took part of the same. Notice that through death, He might destroy him that had the power of death, that is the devil.
point_summary: Christ's incarnation and death are presented as the means by which He destroyed the devil's power over death, freeing those who had lived in bondage to the fear of dying.

context: Notice what He does, He delivers in verse number 15, them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage.
point_summary: Christ's death liberates believers from the lifelong bondage of fearing death, a bondage the preacher illustrates personally by recalling his own pre-conversion dread of dying without salvation.

context: He delivered them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage
point_summary: Christ's victory over death liberates believers from the lifelong bondage of death-fear, a bondage the preacher testifies he personally experienced before trusting Christ as Savior.

context: for verily He took not on Him the nature of angels, but He took on Him the seed of Abraham.
point_summary: Christ had to take on human flesh — not angelic nature — specifically because only a man could die as a substitute in the place of sinners.

context: wherefore, in all things that behooved Him to be made like unto His brethren, that He might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God to make reconciliation for the sins of the people
point_summary: Christ's incarnation was necessary so that, as a merciful and faithful high priest who himself suffered temptation, he could make reconciliation for sin and come alongside the tempted as their helper.

context: for in that He Himself hath suffered being tempted, He is able to succor them that are tempted.
point_summary: Because Christ Himself suffered and was tempted in human flesh, He is uniquely able to "succor"—to come alongside and help—those who are tempted.

context: seeing then that we have a great high priest that is passed into the heavens, Jesus, the Son of God, let us hold fast our profession

context: and being made perfect, he, the Lord Jesus Christ, he became the author of eternal salvation unto all them that obey him.

context: And then we come to our text this morning, and the writer says in verse number 32, and what shall I say more, or what shall I more say? For the time would fail me to tell of Gideon, of Barak, of Samson, of Jephthah, of David also, and Samuel of the prophets.

context: they wandered in deserts and in mountains and in dens and caves of the earth.

context: We know that they were delivered by God, but they didn't accept deliverance from Nebuchadnezzar. We find that these others that were stoned, that were sawn asunder, the folks that dealt with these adversities, that they might obtain a better resurrection,

context: but notice the statement in verse 38. Only to begin it, it's in parenthesis, of whom the world was not worthy.

context: but all the way back to the beginning of the 11th chapter of the book of Hebrews, we begin to see all, remember every era of human history, remember not every name is mentioned because it says of Samuel and of the prophets, and so we begin to think about all of these, the entire body of people represented by what is given to us in Hebrews chapter number 11, these all having obtained a good report through faith.

context: He said in Hebrews 13, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee.
point_summary: Christ's promise never to leave nor forsake his people (Hebrews 13) is cited as the ground for the peace Jesus offers in John 16:33, even amid tribulation.

context: The Bible says in Hebrews chapter 12, looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith.
point_summary: Fixing one's gaze on Jesus rather than on circumstances—illustrated by Peter walking on water—is grounded in Hebrews 12's call to look unto Jesus as the author and finisher of faith.

context: Without faith, it's impossible to please him, for he that cometh to God must believe that he is, and then he's a rewarder of them that diligently seek him.
point_summary: Faith is identified as essential to pleasing God, grounded in Hebrews 11:6's declaration that without it one cannot please Him and that God rewards those who diligently seek Him.

context: let your conversation be without covetousness and be content with such things as you have, for he hath said, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee
point_summary: Hebrews 13:5 is cited to show that the command against covetousness and the call to contentment form the full verse, not merely the promise of God's presence that is commonly quoted in isolation.

context: the book of Hebrews tells us in the 11th chapter that he believed that even if his son were to die there upon the altar that God could raise him up
point_summary: Hebrews 11 is cited to show that Abraham's willingness to slay Isaac rested on faith that God could raise the dead, making his obedience a moment-by-moment trust rather than mere foreknowledge of the outcome.

context: We find in Hebrews, you don't need to turn there, but Hebrews 13 and verse number 21, the Bible says, I'll read verse 20, now the God of peace that brought again from the dead our Lord
point_summary: God works within believers to produce what is well-pleasing in His sight, making the pursuit of His approval not merely a human striving but a divine operation through Jesus Christ.

context: Jesus, that great Shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the everlasting covenant
point_summary: God's working in believers toward what is well-pleasing in His sight is not automatic but requires the believer's willing surrender and availability to Him.

context: make you perfect in every good work to do His will, working in you that which is well-pleasing in His sight
point_summary: God works in believers to produce what is well-pleasing in His sight, but that divine working requires the believer's willing surrender and availability to Him.

context: whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth
point_summary: Divine chastening of those he loves (Hebrews 12:6; cf. Proverbs) grounds the warning that God will bring sudden, certain judgment on the church at Sardis if it refuses to heed his counsel.

context: Hebrews 7 and verse number 25, that He is able to save to the uttermost of all men that come to God.
point_summary: Christ's ability to save "to the uttermost" is cited as the gospel assurance underlying the Noahic covenant's warning: all who come to God can receive full forgiveness and salvation through Jesus.

context: Remember Hebrews 13, when the Word of God tells us, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee.
point_summary: God's promise "I will never leave thee nor forsake thee" in Hebrews 13:5 is presented as the New Testament counterpart to the repeated Old Testament assurance "I will be with thee" given to Isaac, Jacob, Joshua, and Jeremiah.

context: for in the time that you ought to be teachers, you have a need that one teach you
point_summary: Prolonged failure to grow in biblical knowledge is a mark of shame, not neutrality — by the time believers should be teaching others, remaining in need of basic instruction dishonors God's design for spiritual maturity.

context: as it is appointed unto man once to die, after this the judgment
point_summary: Hebrews 9:27 grounds the sermon's opening premise that death is a universal, appointed reality every person faces, shaping the believer's distinct perspective on mortality.

context: for as much then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, he also himself likewise took part of the same, that through death he might destroy him that had the power of death. That is the devil.
point_summary: Christ's incarnation and death destroyed the devil's power over death, freeing believers from the lifelong bondage of fearing death.

context: and deliver them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage.
point_summary: Christ's conquest of death through the Incarnation liberates believers from the lifelong bondage of fearing death, freeing them to approach mortality from a distinctly different perspective than the unredeemed.

context: We find him in the book of Hebrews in the 11th chapter repeatedly being referenced.
point_summary: Hebrews 11 is cited to establish Abraham's towering reputation as a man of faith, against which his raw, tearful grief over Sarah's death reveals his full humanity.

context: And here we find that Hebrews speaks of Sarah. And as soon as it finishes speaking of Sarah in Hebrews 11, it says in verse number 13, and these all died in faith.
point_summary: Dying "in faith," as Hebrews 11:13 declares of Sarah and the patriarchs, is what distinguishes Christian grief as sorrow with hope rather than the hopeless mourning of those without Christ.

context: In Hebrews 11 and verse 13, the Bible says these all died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off and were persuaded of them and embrace them and confess that they were what? Strangers and pilgrims on the earth.
point_summary: Abraham's self-description as "a stranger and a sojourner" in Genesis 23:4 is illuminated by Hebrews 11:13, which identifies the patriarchs as pilgrims who died in faith confessing this world was not their home.

context: But the writer of Hebrews said that our God is a consuming fire, is a fearful thing to fall in the hands of a living God, an angry God.
point_summary: God's consuming fire and fearful judgment, attested in both Testaments, stands as the necessary backdrop against which His grace and mercy in Jonah 4 must be understood.

context: But the writer of Hebrews said that our God is a consuming fire, is a fearful thing to fall in the hands of a living God, an angry God.
point_summary: Falling into the hands of a living, angry God is a fearful thing, invoked here to establish that divine judgment—seen across both Testaments—is consistent with God's unchanging nature.

context: God said of Himself, I'm the same yesterday, today, and forever.
point_summary: God's self-declared unchanging nature — "the same yesterday, today, and forever" — means His justice and judgment witnessed across both Testaments reflect the same consistent character readers must reckon with when approaching any passage.

context: as is appointed unto man, wants to die and after this, the judgment.
point_summary: Death's certainty and the brevity of earthly life ground the call to live fully for Christ now, since one's remaining time is unknown and judgment follows.

context: ...to look at a single verse of scripture in the book of Hebrews in the 11th chapter...
point_summary: Faith that pleases God—illustrated by Enoch's testimony in Hebrews 11—is framed as the animating challenge behind this sermon on living for Jesus.

context: He had this testimony, he pleased God.
point_summary: Enoch's testimony in Hebrews 11:5 — that he pleased God — is held up as the defining ambition for a life lived for Jesus.

context: in the book of Hebrews, in Hebrews chapter 11, the Bible says this, he went out not knowing whither he went.
point_summary: Abraham's obedience to God's call—going out without knowing his destination—is presented as the foundational act that set in motion 25 years of God's faithfulness leading to Isaac's birth.

context: Hebrews 10 and verse 37, if we get a little while, and he that shall come will come and will not tarry.
point_summary: Hebrews 10:37's promise that "he that shall come will come and will not tarry" is cited as assurance that God's timing is exact—He is never late.

context: The analogy is used throughout the scriptures. We find it in Hebrews chapter number 12, particularly where the Bible talks about looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher
point_summary: Fixing the eyes on Jesus as "author and finisher of our faith" is presented as the sustaining motivation that keeps believers from growing weary while running the long-distance race of the Christian life.

context: looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Fixing eyes on Jesus as the one for whom the race is run, Hebrews 12:2 is invoked to show that Paul's exaltation of Christ in 1 Timothy 6 serves the same motivating purpose: Christ is the finish line.

context: let us run with patience the race that is set before us
point_summary: Finishing the Christian race with patience is grounded in keeping eyes fixed on Jesus, the one for whom believers run and who is both author and finisher of faith.

context: He did not have to make a sacrifice first for his own sin.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Hebrews chapter number five this evening, and be looking at verses 11 through 14 tonight
point_summary: Knowing what we ought to do and actually doing it are two different things, a tension the passage addresses by calling believers to spiritual growth they already understand is required.

context: Hebrews chapter number five this evening, and be looking at verses 11 through 14 tonight
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Hebrews chapter number five this evening, and be looking at verses 11 through 14 tonight
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Hebrews chapter number five this evening, and be looking at verses 11 through 14 tonight
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: seeing ye are dull of hearing
point_summary: Spiritual dullness—not the difficulty of the subject matter—is identified as the true obstacle to deeper teaching, making the hearers' condition the barrier to growth.

context: For when for the time ye ought to be teachers, ye have need that one teach you again, which be the first principles of the oracles of God
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity — not the difficulty of the subject matter — is what made deep teaching hard to deliver; the hearers' dullness, not the content, was the obstacle.

context: For everyone that useth milk is unskillful in the word of righteousness, for he is a babe
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity — being a "babe" unskilled in the word of righteousness — renders a believer incapable of receiving the deeper, meatier truths of Scripture.

context: But strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil
point_summary: Spiritual maturity, described in verse 14 as belonging to those whose senses are exercised through use, is presented as God's normative expectation for every believer after salvation.

context: the passage in Hebrews chapter number five, I believe that it confronts every believer and then begs us to evaluate our own lives to consider our spiritual growth.
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11–14 confronts every believer with a call to evaluate personal spiritual growth, recognizing that Christians are each at different stages in their Christian journey.

context: The Bible says, of whom we have many things to say and hard to be uttered. Seeing ye are dull of hearing.
point_summary: Spiritual dullness of hearing, not length of time in the faith, marks the condition being corrected in Hebrews 5:11, since even long-standing believers can fail to grow.

context: these individuals that are being addressed in Hebrews chapter five
point_summary: Prolonged spiritual infancy is the indictment of Hebrews 5:12—believers who, given sufficient time, should be teaching others are still requiring instruction in the most elementary principles of God's Word.

context: these that are being addressed in Hebrews chapter five
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity in Hebrews 5 is traced not to carnality, as in 1 Corinthians 3, but specifically to dullness of hearing—an unwillingness to receive God's Word.

context: an indictment against these believers that are being addressed in Hebrews chapter five, that their lack of spiritual growth can be directly traced to the fact that they were dull of hearing.
point_summary: Spiritual growth is not optional but expected by God, who has supplied everything necessary for it, making the dullness of hearing indicted in Hebrews 5 without excuse.

context: but strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil.
point_summary: Spiritual maturity, measured by the capacity to discern good and evil, stands in contrast to spiritual infancy, and the widespread inability to make that distinction marks a crisis of growth in the contemporary church.

context: Hebrews six and verse number one, we read in Hebrews five, you don't need to turn there, but in verse 11 through 14, in verse number one of chapter six, the Bible says this, let us go on unto perfection.
point_summary: Spiritual maturity—rendered here as the goal of "going on unto perfection"—is presented as the ongoing, never-completed pursuit every believer should actively desire.

context: God says that he will never leave us nor forsake us.
point_summary: God's promise of his presence—"I will never leave thee nor forsake thee"—is cited as the New Testament counterpart to God's assurance to Moses in Exodus 3:12, showing that divine accompaniment extends to all believers today.

context: I will never leave thee nor forsake thee
point_summary: God's promise "I will never leave thee nor forsake thee" (Heb. 13:5) grounds the assurance that, because God is with us and for us, we need not fear what man can do.

context: Hebrews chapter number 11 does tell us that there's pleasure in sin
point_summary: Moses's choice to refuse Egypt's pleasures and suffer with God's people illustrates that sin's pleasures are only temporary, contrasted with living for God.

context: Moses had that experience and it speaks of it in Hebrews chapter number 11, and we find that he refused that and he was willing to suffer affliction with the people of God.
point_summary: Hebrews 11 is cited to illustrate that even Moses found temporary pleasure in sin before choosing to suffer with God's people rather than live for worldly gain.

context: like as we are, yet without sin, according to Hebrews chapter number 4
point_summary: Because Christ as high priest was tempted in all points yet without sin, he is able to succor the feeble-minded, demonstrating that God knows our frame and provides compassionate comfort in discouragement.

context: The Bible plainly tells us in Hebrews chapter nine, we understand that all of the Old Testament just pictures that God gave us.
point_summary: Hebrews 9's declaration that without the shedding of blood there is no remission grounds the entire Old Testament sacrificial pattern as pointing forward to Christ's atoning blood.

context: Hebrews chapter 12 and verse number 1 talks about laying aside every weight and the sin
point_summary: Returning to sin already forsaken re-entangles the believer in the very bondage from which they were delivered, illustrating why Hebrews 12:1 calls for laying aside every besetting weight.

context: Notice in the book of Hebrews, if you will, Hebrews chapter number one... But notice what it says in verse number 6, and again, when He bringeth in the first begotten of the world, He saith, and let all the angels of God worship Him.
point_summary: The command for all angels to worship Christ demonstrates His deity, since angels are not to be worshipped — only God is.

context: but unto the Son He saith, thy throne, O God, is forever and ever. A scepter of righteousness is the scepter of thy kingdom.
point_summary: God the Father's direct address to the Son as "O God" in Hebrews 1:8 is cited as scriptural proof that Jesus is God, distinguishing Him from angels who are never worshipped but only worship Him.

reference_text: Hebrews chapter number four and verse number 16
context: We're familiar with the truth of Hebrews chapter number four and verse number 16 when he says that we're to come boldly under the throne of grace.
point_summary: Bold access to God through Christ as our great high priest means believers can approach the throne of grace with confidence, obtaining mercy and grace precisely when they are in need.

context: He ever liveth to make intercession for us
point_summary: Christ's perpetual intercession—"He ever liveth to make intercession for us"—grounds the believer's confidence in direct access to God through the one mediator, Jesus Christ.

context: as of the point of unto man wants to die, but after this the judgment
point_summary: Death's certainty and the coming judgment—grounded in Hebrews 9:27—are the preacher's warrant for urgently asking every person whether they are sure of heaven.

context: as is appointed unto man who wants to die
point_summary: Death's universality — grounded in Hebrews 9:27's "appointed unto man once to die" — serves as the sobering premise from which the gospel's good news of Christ's victory over death is proclaimed.

context: The Bible tells us in Hebrews 2 and verse number 15, and deliver them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage.
point_summary: Christ's victory over death liberates believers from the lifelong bondage of death-fear, so that they can face mortality—including a pandemic—without that enslaving dread.

context: we understand that Hebrews 13 tells us and we can rejoice in the truth that he had said, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee
point_summary: God's promise never to leave nor forsake His people, cited from Hebrews 13, is set alongside parallel divine assurances given to Moses, Joshua, and Jeremiah as evidence of His unchanging faithfulness to those He calls.

context: we understand from Hebrews 9 and verse 27, and as it's appointed unto man once to die, after this the judgment
point_summary: Death is the universal, divinely appointed boundary for every person, invoked here as the theological frame for understanding Elisha's approaching death in 2 Kings 13.

context: the Word of God is powerful, sharper than any two-edged sword, able to divide and sunder both bone and marrow
point_summary: God's Word carries inherent power regardless of the preacher's delivery style, as illustrated by Jonathan Edwards reading metronomically yet igniting revival through Scripture alone.

context: this wonderful promise from the book of Hebrews in the 13th chapter, where the Bible says that God will never leave us nor forsake us.
point_summary: God's promise never to leave nor forsake His people is not merely a future guarantee but a present reality, illustrated by the Lord's repeated presence with Joseph in both prosperity and prison.

context: for yet a little while, and He that shall come will come and will not tarry
point_summary: Hebrews 10:37 is cited to affirm that Christ's return is divinely certain and precisely timed — He "will come and will not tarry" — with no person or circumstance able to delay His arrival.

context: Abraham finds himself in Hebrews chapter 11 is the great hall of faith
point_summary: Abraham's inclusion in Hebrews 11's hall of faith stands in ironic contrast to his failure in Genesis 20, where the pagan Abimelech outshines him in demonstrated obedience.

reference_text: The just shall live by faith
context: But we've been called to live by faith. The just shall live by faith.
point_summary: Faith's call in Hebrews 10:38—"the just shall live by faith"—is invoked as the governing principle that believers must simply take God at His word rather than demanding to understand how outcomes will unfold.

context: let us, therefore, come boldly unto the throne of grace that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need.
point_summary: Hebrews 4:16 is cited as one of Scripture's many prayer promises, grounding the call to bold, believing prayer in the assurance that God hears and answers those who come to His throne.

context: Notice with me over in Hebrews chapter number 12, and this is really what we seek to guard against as we would understand the importance of forgiveness in our lives, and we'd understand the potential for bitterness.
point_summary: Hebrews 12:15 is cited as a warning that unresolved offenses can produce a root of bitterness that troubles and defiles many within the people of God.

context: where the Bible says, looking diligently, lest any man fail of the grace of God, lest any root of bitterness springing up trouble you, and thereby many be defiled.
point_summary: Unresolved bitterness—warned against in Hebrews 12:15 as a root that defiles many—is presented as a real and active danger even within the church, destroying the one who harbors it more than its target.

context: ...and for this cause He is the mediator of the New Testament...
point_summary: Christ's death as mediator of the New Testament secured redemption from transgressions under the first testament so that the called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance.

context: ...there must also of necessity be the death of the testator...
point_summary: A testament becomes legally operative only through the death of the one who made it, establishing why Christ's death was a necessity, not merely an option, for the New Covenant to take effect.

context: ...For a testament is of force after men are dead...
point_summary: A testament carries legal force only upon the death of the one who made it, establishing why Christ's death was a necessary condition for the New Covenant's promises to take effect.

context: Looking unto Jesus. Our theme for this year from Hebrews 12.
point_summary: Hebrews 12:2's call to look to Jesus is the congregation's annual theme, grounding the exhortation to run the race by fixing eyes on a superior Savior amid opposition.

context: ...the truth of what the passage that we're dealing with in Hebrews 9 is dealing with...
point_summary: Hebrews 9 is expounded to show that Christ's atoning death fulfilled Old Testament typology as a better priest offering His own blood in the true heavenly sanctuary.

context: Neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption for us.
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all entry into the heavenly tabernacle with His own blood—not animal blood—secured eternal redemption, a better sacrifice yielding a better gift than the Levitical system could provide.

context: The Bible says that He entered in once.
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all entry into the heavenly holy place with His own blood—contrasted with the high priest's repeated Day of Atonement entries—secured eternal redemption where animal sacrifices could not.

context: but now once in the end of the world hath He appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of Himself.
point_summary: Christ's single appearance to put away sin by the sacrifice of Himself contrasts with the high priest's repeated Day of Atonement entries, because Jesus, being sinless, needed only one offering.

context: Pick it up with me in verse 18. The Bible says, "...whereupon neither the First Testament was dedicated without blood.
point_summary: Blood atonement is confirmed as the covenantal pattern from the First Testament onward, as even the Mosaic covenant's dedication required the sprinkling of blood.

context: he took the blood of calves and of goats with water and scarlet wool and hyssop and sprinkled both the book and all the people
point_summary: Moses' sprinkling of blood on the book and the people at Sinai establishes that the old covenant itself was inaugurated only through blood, grounding the sermon's argument that Christ's atoning death was absolutely necessary.

context: saying, this is the blood of the Testament which God hath enjoined unto you
point_summary: Blood atonement was absolutely necessary, as Moses' sprinkling of blood to dedicate the first covenant (v. 20) illustrates that without the shedding of blood there is no remission of sin.

context: Moreover, he sprinkled with blood both the tabernacle and all the vessels of the ministry
point_summary: Blood atonement is necessary for purification, as Moses' sprinkling of blood over the tabernacle and its vessels established the pattern that without shedding of blood there is no remission.

context: And almost all things are by the law purged with blood, and without shedding of blood is no remission
point_summary: Without the shedding of blood there is no remission, making Christ's death absolutely necessary as the blood atonement that secures eternal redemption for those who are called.

context: for where a Testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator
point_summary: A testament's promised inheritance cannot be received until the testator dies, making Christ's death an absolute necessity for the eternal redemption He secured.

context: they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance
point_summary: Christ's death was absolutely necessary so that those called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance, fulfilling what His shed blood secured as the new covenant's mediator.

context: And you go through the whole of the Old Testament, and you see that God has designed it such that without shedding of blood is no remission.
point_summary: Without the shedding of blood there is no remission, and Christ's death was therefore absolutely necessary to activate the new covenant and secure what God had promised from Genesis onward.

context: the atonement of Christ there in Hebrews 9 and verses 12-26
point_summary: Christ's atoning death, as developed across Hebrews 9:12–26, accomplished eternal redemption—making His superiority over every competing loyalty the ground for believers to forsake sin that hinders fellowship.

context: the atonement of Christ there in Hebrews 9 and verses 12-26
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all atoning death, referenced across Hebrews 9:12–26, accomplished eternal redemption and stands as the ground why sin in the believer's life is not worth pursuing—because Christ is better.

context: Oh, but what we miss out on. The joy of what we have on this earth with our fellowship with God, that we willingly surrender that for something far inferior. For some momentary pleasure. Hebrews 11 says that there is pleasure in sin for a season.
point_summary: Sin offers only temporary pleasure, making Christ's superiority the compelling reason to forsake it rather than presume on grace.

context: the believers in Hebrews 9, they're being instructed with regard to the necessity of Christ's death
point_summary: Christ's death was necessary to make the new covenant promises effective, as his blood — sufficient and accepted — is the sole basis on which those promises come into force.

context: Remember, all the way back in Hebrews 2, in verse 14, the Bible says for as much then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, He also Himself likewise took part of the same, that through death... he might destroy him that had the power of death. That is the devil.
point_summary: Christ's incarnation — his taking on flesh and blood — was the necessary precondition for his death, through which he destroyed the devil's power over death.

context: as you look back in Hebrews 9 we read through verse 24
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all atonement provides daily benefit to believers, enabling confession, forgiveness, and the pleading of his blood against the devil's accusations.

context: at verse 25 nor yet that he should offer himself often actually read this verse 2 but that's okay and verse 25 as the high priest enters in the holy place every year with blood of others
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all sacrifice, contrasted with the repeated offerings of the Levitical high priest, is presented as the basis for daily victory over sin's power, not merely forgiveness of sin's penalty.

context: notice 26 for then must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world but now once in the end of the world hath he appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all sacrifice not only secured freedom from sin's penalty at salvation but provides daily victory over sin's power for the believer.

context: Hebrews 9 in verse 27 it's a verse that we often quote the Bible says
point_summary: Death is not the end: the appointment every person keeps is followed by judgment, not annihilation.

context: as it and as it is appointed unto men wants to die but after this the judgment
point_summary: Death is appointed to all people because of sin, issuing in both a physical and a spiritual death, with the second death awaiting those who are not born again.

context: The Bible says, no point unto man once to die, after this the judgment.
point_summary: Appointed once to die and then face judgment, the verse frames human mortality as the backdrop against which the new birth and Christ's atonement deliver believers from the great white throne judgment.

context: Hebrews 9 and verse 28, so Christ was once offered...
point_summary: Christ's singular, sufficient atonement at His first coming guarantees that His second appearing will be without sin—bringing final salvation to those who eagerly await Him.

context: the verse that we've looked at, but I want you to see in chapter 10, what the Bible says about the truth of Christ's return.
point_summary: Christ's promised return is certain—Hebrews 9:28 is cited alongside a chain of New Testament texts to establish that every divine promise of the Second Coming will be fulfilled.

context: for yet a little while and he that shall come will come and will not tarry.
point_summary: Hebrews 10:37 is cited as one of several converging scriptural witnesses affirming that Christ's return is certain and without delay.

context: we consider the appearing of Christ
point_summary: Hebrews 9's closing promise of Christ's appearing is presented as one anchor in a chain of New Testament texts establishing that his return is certain, not conditional.

context: This morning, we look at Hebrews 9. It could have been three separate messages, but I tried to put it all together. The atonement, the appointment, and the appearing.
point_summary: Hebrews 9 is presented as a unified passage encompassing three interlocking themes—the atonement, the appointment, and the appearing—all centering on Christ's return and readiness before him.

reference_text: the book of Hebrews in the fourth chapter
context: We're to come boldly into the throne of grace, is what the Scriptures tell us in the book of Hebrews in the fourth chapter.
point_summary: Prayer is a unique privilege reserved for believers, who—on the basis of belonging to Christ—may come boldly into the throne of grace (Heb. 4).

context: the bible tells us in the book of hebrews in the 11th chapter that we're strangers and pilgrims
point_summary: Hebrews 11 is cited to establish that believers are strangers and pilgrims in this world, grounding the ambassador's identity in a heavenly citizenship rather than earthly belonging.

context: ...hardened not your hearts in the day of provocation, in the day of temptation, in the wilderness,
point_summary: Hebrews 3:8's warning against hardening the heart introduces the sermon's controlling theme: sin deceives its practitioners into believing they retain control over both their behavior and its consequences.

context: ...take heed, brethren, lest there be any of you an evil heart of unbelief and departing from the living God.
point_summary: Hebrews 3:12 is cited to establish that sin operates through deceit, luring people into sinful behavior with a false sense of control over both the sin itself and its consequences.

context: This morning we'll be in Hebrews 12, verses 4 through 17
point_summary: Hebrews 12:4 opens a passage on divine chastisement that is rarely discussed yet carries significant importance, establishing that God's discipline is both part of His divine plan and has a definite purpose.

context: This morning we'll be in Hebrews 12, verses 4 through 17
point_summary: Hebrews 12:17 falls within a passage (vv. 4–17) treated as a unified unit on divine chastisement, presented as part of God's intentional plan with a definite purpose for believers.

context: you have not yet resisted unto blood, striving against sin
point_summary: Hebrews 12:4 serves as a transitional marker establishing that the first-century recipients had faced real suffering but had not yet been called to die for their faith.

context: ye have forgotten the exhortation which speaketh unto you as unto children. My son, despise not thou the chastening of the Lord, nor faint when thou art rebuked of him
point_summary: Believers who have forgotten that God's chastening is an expression of His love need to be reminded that divine discipline and divine affection are inseparable, not contradictory.

context: For whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth, and scourgeth every son whom he receiveth
point_summary: Divine chastening is not a contradiction of God's love but a direct expression of it, correcting the misunderstanding that love permits unchecked behavior.

context: whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth
point_summary: Divine chastening, defined here as instruction aimed at increasing virtue and correction through severity, is inseparable from God's love — not a contradiction of it.

reference_text: whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth
context: whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth
point_summary: Divine chastening, defined here as instruction aimed at increasing virtue and correction through measured, appropriate punishment, is the very expression of God's love for his children.

context: rebuke us. And it proves to us that he loves us. Not only does it prove that he loves us, but it proves to us back in Hebrews chapter number 12, it proves to us that we belong to him.
point_summary: Divine chastening, according to Hebrews 12, confirms both God's love for believers and their genuine belonging to him as sons.

context: my son despise not thou the chastening of the Lord
point_summary: God's chastening of believers, signaled by the address "my son" in verse 5, serves as proof both of his love and of the believer's genuine adoption into his family.

context: whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth and scourgeth
point_summary: Divine chastening, far from contradicting God's love, confirms both his love for the believer and the believer's genuine sonship within the family of God.

context: if ye endure chastening, God dealeth with you as with sons
point_summary: Enduring God's chastening is presented as evidence of sonship, since the Father's discipline of every son confirms that the one being corrected truly belongs to him.

context: what son is he whom the Father chasteneth not?
point_summary: Enduring chastening is evidence of sonship, and the absence of God's corrective discipline raises serious questions about whether one truly belongs to the Father.

context: fathers of our flesh which corrected us and we gave them reverence. Shall we not much rather be in subjection to the Father of Spirits and live?
point_summary: Earthly fathers disciplined only their own children, not neighbors' kids — and so God's chastening likewise proves that those he corrects truly belong to him as his children.

context: we're of all our partakers in verse number eight.
point_summary: The absence of divine chastisement, the sermon argues, is not a blessing but a mark of illegitimacy—proof that one does not truly belong to God as His child.

context: At the end of verse number seven, for what son is he whom the father chasteneth not?
point_summary: Divine chastening proves genuine sonship: a father disciplines his own children, not the neighbor's, so God's correction confirms that the one being disciplined truly belongs to him.

context: despise not thou the chastening of the Lord, nor faint when thou art rebuked of Him
point_summary: Chastening is a birthmark of genuine sonship—God disciplines those who belong to Him, and its absence marks one as illegitimate rather than a true child of God.

context: endure chastening, if ye endure chastening
point_summary: Enduring chastisement is the command of Hebrews 12:7, and the sermon grounds it in the truth that divine discipline is the birthmark proving a believer belongs to God.

context: We find that it says that we might be partakers of His holiness. Continue with me back in Hebrews 12 and notice in verse number 11, great truth is revealed to us here again with regard to the subject matter of chastening. It says, now, no chastening for the present seemeth to be joyous. How many of you can say amen? I agree, right? It's not enjoyable when you're dealing with the chastisement, but He's helping us by showing us what it proves and what it produces. He's helping us to understand that we're not going to faint, that we will despise not thou the chastening of the Lord, that we will endure chastening because we understand what it proves and what it produces. And yes, it's not joyous. It's not an enjoyable time. It's grievous as it says here in verse number 11. Notice what it says though. Nevertheless, afterward, it yieldeth the peaceable fruit of righteousness unto them which are exercised thereby. Yieldeth the peaceable fruit of righteousness.
point_summary: Though chastening is grievous in the moment, its designed purpose is to yield the peaceable fruit of righteousness—transforming unrighteous behavior into righteous behavior pleasing to God.

context: We find that it yieldeth the peaceable fruit of righteousness unto them which are exercised thereby. As we look at the passage of Scripture, we continue looking at verses 12 through 17 this morning.
point_summary: God's chastisement is measured and progressive, moving from the quiet pricking of Scripture to more severe discipline when earlier warnings are ignored.

context: follow peace with all men and holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord
point_summary: Hebrews 12:14's call to follow holiness and peace is read within the context of chastening as a warning against the bitterness that troubles and defiles when discipline is received with anger rather than submission.

context: looking diligently, lest any man fail of the grace of God, lest any root of bitterness springing up trouble you, and thereby many be defiled
point_summary: Bitterness toward God, warned against in Hebrews 12:15, is presented here as a destructive response to divine chastening that troubles the one who harbors it and defiles many.

context: In fact, he says in verse number 17, for you know, the writer of Hebrews has the expectation
point_summary: Esau's example in Hebrews 12:17 is cited as a warning that bitterness and failure to repent—understood as a change of mind producing a change of direction—are the wrong responses to God's chastening discipline.

context: When he would have inherited the blessing, he was rejected, for he found no place of repentance, though he sought it carefully with tears.
point_summary: Esau's tears were for the lost blessing, not for repentance, illustrating that a person can desperately want God's favor while remaining indifferent to the character and conduct that chastisement is meant to produce.

reference_text: Whom the Lord loveth, He chasteneth
context: Our God loves us, and because He loves us, He chasteneth us. Whom the Lord loveth, He chasteneth
point_summary: Divine chastisement proves God's love, as the sermon closes by grounding the entire discussion of discipline's purpose and severity in the truth that God chastens those He loves.

context: I'm told as one of my responsibilities in Hebrews that I watch for your souls.

context: obey them that have the rule over you and submit yourselves for they watch for your souls, notice this, as they must give account that they may do it, notice what it says, with joy and not with grief. But then it says this, for that is unprofitable for you.
