context: Look over to the book of Romans if you, or excuse me, Hebrews if you will, chapter number 9. Look at Hebrews chapter number 9.
point_summary: Sin's payment can only be made through blood, as Hebrews 9:22 declares that without the shedding of blood there is no remission — establishing why Christ's sacrifice was the sole acceptable remedy.

context: and almost all things are by the law purged with blood. The Bible actually says, and without shedding of blood is no remission.
point_summary: Blood is the non-negotiable currency of remission, establishing why Christ's suffering was necessary rather than merely tragic.

context: for the law having a shadow of good things to come.
point_summary: The Old Testament law and its repeated sacrifices were only a shadow of good things to come, unable to make worshippers perfect—pointing forward to the one sufficient sacrifice Christ would fulfill.

context: those sacrifices could never make the comers thereunto perfect.
point_summary: The repeated animal sacrifices of the Old Testament were only a shadow of good things to come, unable to perfect worshipers or remove sin, but pointing forward to the coming Messiah who would.

context: for then would they not have ceased to be offered?
point_summary: The repeated Old Testament sacrifices could never perfect the worshiper precisely because their ongoing necessity proved their insufficiency, pointing forward to a coming Messiah who would accomplish what they could not.

context: but in those sacrifices there is a remembrance again made of sins every year.
point_summary: Annual sacrifices under the old covenant could never permanently remove sin but instead served as a yearly reminder of it, pointing forward to the Messiah who would.

context: for it is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins.
point_summary: Animal blood sacrifices could never permanently remove sin but only foreshadowed the coming Messiah who would accomplish what they could not.

context: and every priest standeth daily ministering and offering oftentimes
point_summary: The repeated, never-finished priestly sacrifices under the old covenant could never take away sins, serving only as a continual reminder of sin and of the need for a coming Messiah.

context: And then we read in verse number 12, the shadow passing away in the very image of the Lord Jesus Christ. Here in verse number 12 it says, but this man, the Lord Jesus Christ, see the book of Hebrews is all about exalting Christ and how Christ is better, how Christ is superior.
point_summary: Christ's single, unrepeatable sacrifice for sins — contrasted with the endless, ineffective offerings of the Levitical priests — is presented as the fulfillment and termination of the entire sacrificial shadow system.

context: But Jesus, after he had made that one sacrifice for sins forever, after he rose conquering sin, death, and the grave, hallelujah, he sat, the Bible says, on the right hand of God.
point_summary: Christ's single, unrepeated sacrifice—unlike the endless Levitical offerings—was accepted by God as permanently sufficient, after which he sat down at God's right hand, his atoning work complete.

context: The Bible tells us that he ever lives to make intercession for us.
point_summary: Christ's one sacrifice for sins, accepted forever by God as perfect and sinless, is contrasted with the repeated, ineffective Levitical offerings, establishing his superiority as the interceding, enthroned High Priest.

context: looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith. And then notice these words, who for the joy that was set before Him endured the cross

context: The Bible tells us that he ever liveth to make intercession for us in Hebrews chapter 7

context: Whom the Lord loveth, He chasteneth.

context: it is appointed unto man once to die, but after this, the judgment.

context: He's the same yesterday and today and forever.

context: we spent time in Hebrews 1 and 2 dealing with
point_summary: Christ's acceptance of worship, contrasted with the angel's refusal of John's worship, demonstrates from Hebrews 1–2 that Jesus is God and therefore uniquely worthy of adoration.

context: we spent time in Hebrews 1 and 2 dealing with

context: In Hebrews chapter four, if you take a moment and turn over to the book of Hebrews, we'll actually look at a couple of passages in the book of Hebrews. context: The Bible teaches in the book of Hebrews the danger of a root of bitterness.

context: as we come to chapter number twelve in verse number twenty-five, that the Bible, in line with these other warnings, it says, see that you refuse not him that speaketh.

context: Wherefore we, receiving a kingdom which cannot be moved.

context: we are receiving a kingdom which cannot be moved

context: we receive a kingdom which cannot be moved

context: for our God as a consuming fire

context: On Sunday morning, as we were studying through the book of Hebrews, in Hebrews chapter number 10, I dealt with the subject matter of a new and living way.

context: He offered Himself willingly, once and for all, according to the book of Hebrews.

Hebrews 12:1–3 is introduced as the culmination of a book-long argument that Christ is superior to everything, addressed to first-century believers tempted to return to Judaism under opposition.

The congregation's 2020 theme of "looking unto Jesus" is drawn directly from Hebrews 12:2, set within the book's sustained argument that Christ is superior to everything that might draw believers back from him.

context: believers in the region of Galatia, and we look at our passage this morning here in Hebrews chapter number 12, verses 1 through 3, if you're taking notes, I would submit to you number 1 this morning, that you must be fit for the race.
point_summary: Endurance in the Christian life is framed as a marathon requiring deliberate preparation, with Hebrews 12:2 anchoring the call to be spiritually fit for a race meant to be finished, not merely started.

context: believers in the region of Galatia, and we look at our passage this morning here in Hebrews chapter number 12, verses 1 through 3, if you're taking notes, I would submit to you number 1 this morning, that you must be fit for the race.
point_summary: Endurance in the Christian life requires preparation and fitness for a long race—not a sprint—that every believer is already running by virtue of being saved.

context: we're foreseeing we also are encompassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses

context: instruction to the believers here in Hebrews chapter number 12, when they're being given this call, this challenge to run the race

context: looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith

context: He endured the cross despising the shame. Hebrews 12 and verse number 2 notice and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God.

context: Really the reference here in Hebrews 12 and verse number 2 at the end when it says that He sat down at the right hand of the throne of God
point_summary: Christ's session at the right hand of God, referenced in Hebrews 12:2, is connected to Hebrews 1 as confirmation that the risen, ascended Savior reigns in exalted majesty after enduring the cross for our salvation.

context: it really reminds us all the way back to the beginning of Hebrews chapter number 1 when the Bible says, God who at sundry times
point_summary: Christ's session at the Father's right hand, introduced in Hebrews 1 as the completion of his sin-purging work, is recalled here as the fulfillment foreshadowed by Hebrews 12:2's declaration that he "sat down."

context: and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets
point_summary: Hebrews 1:1–3 is cited to anchor Christ's present session at God's right hand in the Son's identity as the climactic, final word of divine revelation and the one who purged sins before sitting down in exaltation.

context: hath in these last days spoken unto us by His Son, whom He hath appointed heir of all things, by whom also He hath made the worlds
point_summary: Christ's session at the Father's right hand, introduced in Hebrews 1:2, is cited as the fulfillment of His completed purification for sins and the ground for His anticipated return.

context: who being the brightness of His glory and the expression of the image of His person, upholding all things by the word of His power, when He had by Himself purged our sins, sat down on the right hand of the majesty on high
point_summary: Christ's session at the Father's right hand, introduced in Hebrews 1:3, is shown to be the same exalted position referenced in Hebrews 12:2, confirming the continuity of His completed redemptive work and present reign.

context: being made so much better than the angels, as He hath by inheritance obtained a more excellent name than they
point_summary: Christ's session at the Father's right hand, introduced in Hebrews 1:4 as the inheritance of a name more excellent than the angels, is here connected to His completed work of purging sins and His coming return.

context: And here as we come to the end of verse number 2 of Hebrews 12, it says, and has sat down at the right hand of the throne of God
point_summary: Christ's session at the right hand of God, connecting Hebrews 12:2 to Hebrews 1's portrait of the exalted Son, points forward to His promised return.

context: Notice as we look at the text of Scripture in Hebrews chapter number 12, notice verse number three. The Bible says for consider him.
point_summary: Fixing attention on Christ who "endured such contradiction of sinners" supplies the endurance needed to finish the race, because the command to "consider him" directs the believer's mind specifically to his suffering, not merely his person in general.

context: Notice it says for consider him that endured such contradiction of sinners against himself.
point_summary: Hebrews 12:3's command to "consider him" is expounded as a specific, contextually bounded charge—not general meditation on Christ, but focused contemplation of the contradiction of sinners he endured, offered as the model for finishing the race.

context: the writer in Hebrews tells us here in Hebrews 12 and verse number 3, what it is that we should consider about the life and ministry of the Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Considering Christ's endurance of great opposition until his work was complete is presented as the model and motivation for believers to resist distraction and finish the race God has assigned them.

context: unto perfection, as it says in Hebrews 6.
point_summary: Hebrews 6's call to "go on unto perfection" frames the entire book as divine encouragement for first-century believers—and present ones—to press forward by faith and finish their race.

context: challenge is given to us in these three verses of Scripture in Hebrews 12
point_summary: Hebrews 12's call to fix eyes on Jesus and run with endurance frames the urgent question of whether believers will finish their race with joy and please their Savior.

context: of whom we have many things to say
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11 is read alongside John 16:12 to show that spiritual immaturity — dullness of hearing — limits how much divine truth a believer can receive and retain.

context: Moses was a man that, uh, was, was faithful in all his house.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Strong me, in Hebrews chapter number 5, belonging to them who are full age.
point_summary: Spiritual maturity — belonging to those of "full age" — is tied directly to consistent, disciplined Bible reading, without which believers remain in need of basic instruction rather than becoming teachers.

context: In the time, he says in the same passage, that you ought to be teachers, you have need that one teach you,
point_summary: Prolonged failure to feed on Scripture leaves believers perpetually needing basic instruction rather than advancing to the maturity that equips them to teach others.

context: I'm mindful of what the book of Hebrews teaches us that we are strangers and pilgrims. What a great terminology that is used there in Hebrews 11. If you want to jot the reference down, you can look at v. 13-16 there in Hebrews 11, and you can find how that there as we would consider the great hall of faith chapter that in summary in those few verses he speaks about the fact that we are strangers and pilgrims.
point_summary: Believers are described in Hebrews 11:13–16 as strangers and pilgrims, illustrating that Christians are not yet home but are heading there—a reality that defines their identity in the world.

context: keep our eyes on Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Keeping eyes on Jesus as "the author and finisher of our faith" is presented as the necessary anchor that sustains believers against giving up amid pressure and temptation.

context: the Lord, the Bible says that he said he would never leave us nor forsake us
point_summary: God's promise never to leave or forsake his people (Heb. 13:5) grounds the believer's freedom from fear of what man can do, undergirding the provision Paul describes in 2 Timothy 1:7.

context: we can come boldly to the throne of grace
point_summary: The believer's privilege of bold, unhindered access to God's throne of grace is grounded in Christ's death, burial, and resurrection, which secured direct entry into heaven's throne room at any time and for any reason.

context: Because the word of God is quick and powerful and sharper than the two-edged sword.
point_summary: The Word of God is cited as inherently powerful and beneficial in itself — "quick and powerful and sharper than a two-edged sword" — as grounds for urging believers to read Scripture for their own spiritual good.

context: Hebrews 10 speaks about those that would stand
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: those priests that would minister daily, offering the same sacrifices that could never take away sin.
point_summary: The repeated, ineffectual daily sacrifices of the Levitical priests stand in deliberate contrast to Christ's single, sufficient sacrifice, which accomplished what no animal offering ever could.

context: But this man, after he made one sacrifice for sin forever, sat at the right hand of the throne of God.
point_summary: Christ's single, unrepeated sacrifice for sin—contrasted with the daily, ineffective offerings of Levitical priests—secured redemption permanently, as Peter understood when he bore witness to the sufferings.

context: In Hebrews chapter number 12, we'll make application and be done this morning. In Hebrews chapter number 12, Hebrews 12. Beginning in verse number one. Hebrews 12 and verse number one, the Bible says, we're foreseeing we also are encompassed about
point_summary: Hebrews 12:1–2 is cited as a closing application, calling believers who have been saved through Christ's imputed righteousness to lay aside sin and run with endurance, looking to Jesus.

context: with so great a cloud of witnesses. Let us lay aside every weight and the sin which doth so easily beset us and let us run with patience the race that is set before us.
point_summary: Though the word "suffer" does not appear in Hebrews 12:1–3, Christ's endurance of the cross and contradiction of sinners demonstrates that his suffering was purposeful—motivated by the joy set before him and inseparable from the glory that followed.

context: Looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith, notice this, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God.
point_summary: Though the word "suffer" does not appear in Hebrews 12:2, Christ's endurance of the cross and despising of the shame demonstrate that he bore suffering willingly because he understood suffering precedes glory.

context: For consider him that endured such contradiction of sinners against himself, lest you be wearied and faint in your own minds.
point_summary: Hebrews 12:3's description of Christ enduring contradiction from sinners illustrates that his suffering was purposeful—borne for the joy set before him—confirming that suffering precedes glory.

context: looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross
point_summary: Christ endured the cross and its shame not despite the suffering but through it, motivated by the joy set before him, because suffering precedes glory.

context: He came after the seed of Abraham, according to Hebrews chapter number 2, in order to redeem fallen man.
point_summary: Christ's incarnation required him to take on human flesh and come after the seed of Abraham, according to Hebrews 2, specifically in order to redeem fallen humanity.

context: I will never leave thee nor forsake thee
point_summary: Knowing God's promise never to leave or forsake His people produces bold, fearless confidence that the Lord is one's helper against any human threat.

context: the Lord is my helper, and I will not fear what man shall do unto me
point_summary: Knowing what God has promised — that He will never leave nor forsake — is the ground on which a believer can boldly declare freedom from the fear of man.

reference_text: whom the Lord loveth, He chasteneth
context: We understand that the Bible teaches us, whom the Lord loveth, He chasteneth, and they saw the chastisement, they saw the judgment.
point_summary: Divine chastisement of those who rebel — illustrated by the swallowing of Dathan and Abiram — is grounded in the biblical principle that God disciplines those He loves.

context: 'For whom, Lord, lovely chasteneth, that we should not be condemned by the world.'
point_summary: God's chastening of believers—cited from Hebrews 12:6—is presented as confirmation of salvation, since its absence would indicate one is not truly saved.

reference_text: His Word is quick and powerful and sharper than a two-edged sword
context: He said that His Word is quick and powerful and sharper than a two-edged sword.
point_summary: God's Word is living, powerful, and sharper than a two-edged sword—a brief affirmation of Scripture's active, effectual work in believers, grounding the promises that follow.

context: wherefore He is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by Him, seeing He ever liveth to make intercession for them
point_summary: Christ's ability to save "to the uttermost" is grounded in His unceasing intercessory life, confirming that His saving promise is not merely initial but ongoing and complete.

reference_text: looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith
context: attention upon my God, when I am taking to heart the truth looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith, how my heart can rejoice in the midst of turmoil
point_summary: Fixing one's attention on Jesus as "the author and finisher of our faith" is presented as the means by which the heart can rejoice amid turmoil, chaos, and confusion.

context: who being the brightness of his glory and the express image of his person, it sounds similar to what we're studying in here in Colossians 1, upholding all things by the word of his power. When he had by himself purged our sins, sat down on the right hand of the majesty on high.
point_summary: Hebrews 1:3's declaration that Christ upholds all things by the word of his power confirms that he is not merely Creator but the ongoing Sustainer who holds the entire universe together.

context: He's immutable, that He never changes, He is the same yesterday and today and forever
point_summary: God's immutability — that He is the same yesterday, today, and forever — grounds praise not in changing circumstances but in who God unchangingly is.

context: Paul says this in Hebrews 10 in verse number 38, for yet a little while, and he that shall come will come and will not tarry.
point_summary: Hebrews 10:38 is cited to affirm that Christ's return is certain and precisely timed — he will come and will not tarry, arriving neither late nor early.

context: The Bible tells us that strong meat belongeth to them that are full age.
point_summary: Spiritual maturity and faithfulness through trials require more than minimal Scripture intake — solid food belongs to those who are fully developed, demanding sustained feasting on God's Word.

context: looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We mentioned this morning from Hebrews chapter number 10
point_summary: Hebrews 10's call to hold fast without wavering is grounded in God's own faithfulness to His promises, as illustrated by the unconditional "I will" declarations to Abram.

context: Remember, as we looked at Hebrews chapter number 6 many weeks ago, and referenced it this morning...
point_summary: The aspect of the Abrahamic promise foregrounded in Hebrews 6 is the promise of a seed, not the promise of land, a distinction carried forward into Galatians 3's identification of that seed with Christ.

context: In verse number 8 of Hebrews 11, the Bible says, By faith Abraham, when he was called to go out into a place which he should after receive for an inheritance, what's it say? Obeyed.
point_summary: Abram's obedience to God's call was an act of faith exercised without a known destination, illustrating that genuine faith moves forward in obedience even amid uncertainty.

context: By faith, notice verse number 9 of Hebrews 11, he sojourned in the land of promise.
point_summary: Abraham's sojourning in Canaan as a stranger dwelling in tents illustrates that his obedience to God's call was ongoing and costly, not a single act but a sustained pilgrimage of faith.

context: Hebrews 11 six without faith, it is impossible to please him for he that cometh to God must believe that he is and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him without faith.
point_summary: Obedience to God's straightforward commands requires faith, and without that faith — as Hebrews 11:6 declares — it is impossible to please Him.

context: Remember, without faith, it's impossible to please Him.
point_summary: Living by faith and obedience is the standard by which God's people are tested, since without faith it is impossible to please Him.

context: In Hebrews 6 in verse 10 the Bible says for God is not unrighteous to forget notice your work and labor of love, which you have showed toward His name
point_summary: God's righteousness guarantees that acts of love expressed through ministering to the saints are never forgotten or overlooked by Him.

context: looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Keeping eyes fixed on Jesus as the author and finisher of faith is the believer's only safeguard against the distractions that derail the race set before them.

context: We need to keep our eyes on Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith.
point_summary: Fixing one's gaze on Jesus as "author and finisher of our faith" is the only safeguard against the distractions that cause a soldier—or a runner—to lose the race.

context: keep our eyes on Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Fixing one's eyes on Jesus as "the author and finisher of our faith" is invoked as the antidote to life's distractions and the means of finishing one's course with joy before God.

context: as it is appointed unto man once to die, but after this, the judgment
point_summary: The appointed death and subsequent judgment of every person (Heb. 9:27) frames the stakes of evangelism as eternal, making the lost condition of souls a weightier matter than any temporal destruction.

context: not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together as the matter of some is, but exhorting one another and so much the more as you see the day approaching
point_summary: The rare Greek word translated "gathering together" in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 appears only one other time in the New Testament—Hebrews 10:25—linking the church's present assembling to the future eschatological gathering unto Christ.

context: Turn over to Hebrews 10, if you will, and I want you to see here just a couple of things as we think about the current gathering.
point_summary: The Greek word translated "gathering together" in 2 Thessalonians 2:1 appears only twice in the New Testament, with Hebrews 10:25 providing its present-tense application: the local church's commanded, regular assembling together.

context: And let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works.
point_summary: Gathering together in the local church serves the positive purpose of mutually "provoking" one another—calling each other into action—toward love and good works.

context: Hebrews 10, verses 24 and 25, that uses this word with regard to this gathering
point_summary: Gathering together in corporate worship serves the purpose of provoking one another to love—the same love Christ commanded in John 13:34, by which all men recognize his disciples.

context: Hebrews 10, verses 24 and 25, that uses this word with regard to this gathering
point_summary: Gathering together, as described in Hebrews 10:24–25, serves the purpose of provoking one another to love—the same love Christ commanded in John 13:34, by which all men recognize his disciples.

context: as that word is used in Hebrews 10 in verse number 25
point_summary: The assembling of believers together—the current, ongoing gathering of the saints—is the present-tense counterpart to the future eschatological gathering described in 2 Thessalonians 2:1, with Hebrews 10:25 grounding the exhortation not to forsake it.

context: we look at the preceding verse in verse number 24
point_summary: The current gathering of believers—assembling together to provoke one another toward love and good works—is presented as the earthly counterpart to the future eschatological gathering described in 2 Thessalonians 2:1.

context: not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as the manner of some is
point_summary: Forsaking regular assembly is no modern problem; the text itself acknowledges that even in the first century some were already neglecting the gathering, making faithful attendance a privilege rather than a burden.

context: the Bible says and so much the more as you see the day approaching
point_summary: Forsaking assembly is directly contradicted by the command to gather *more* as the day of Christ's return approaches, making increased faithfulness in corporate worship the expected response to prophetic urgency.

reference_text: as an appointed unto man wants to die, but after this, the judgment
context: The Bible tells us as an appointed unto man wants to die, but after this, the judgment.
point_summary: Death is appointed for every person, but what follows—judgment—makes eternal life through Christ the supreme concern above physical existence.

context: Hebrews chapter 13 in verse number six reminds us that we can have that confidence that God, he is our helper.
point_summary: Amid Paul's hardships, Hebrews 13:6 grounds the believer's confidence that God is our helper, making endurance possible.

context: at the end of verse five, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee
point_summary: God's promise never to leave or forsake his people (Heb. 13:5–6) is the direct answer to the persecution and abandonment Paul describes in 2 Corinthians 4:9—hardship is real, but so is divine help.

context: so that we might boldly say, the Lord is my helper. I will not fear what man shall do unto me
point_summary: God's promise never to leave or forsake his people (Heb. 13:5–6) grounds the bold confession that the Lord is our helper, so that persecution and loneliness need not become abandonment or despair.

context: wherefore in all things that behooved Him to be made like unto His brethren. Notice this, that He might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God to make reconciliation for the sins of the people.
point_summary: Christ's incarnation was necessary so that He could serve as a merciful and faithful high priest, making reconciliation for sins through the shedding of blood.

context: though you won't see the word
point_summary: Without using the word "reconciliation," Hebrews 9:22 establishes the necessary mechanism behind it: blood must be shed, for without it there is no remission of sins.

context: and almost all things are by the law purged with blood, and without shedding of blood is no remission.
point_summary: Without shedding of blood there is no remission, establishing that the miracle of reconciliation described in 2 Corinthians 5 is grounded in the necessity of Christ's atoning blood.

context: as of the point of end to man, wants to die. But after this, the judgment.
point_summary: Death's universality — "it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment" — frames the pandemic's mortality statistics as a reminder that every person faces not only physical death but divine reckoning.

context: and deliver them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage
point_summary: Reconciliation with Christ frees believers from the lifelong bondage of fearing death, because he has conquered death on their behalf.

context: Perhaps the ones that you could go to and read of their lives in Hebrews chapter number 11
point_summary: Heroes of faith in Hebrews 11 are held up as examples of people who understood who God is and what He is able to do.

context: He is the same yesterday and today and forever.
point_summary: God's immutability — that He is the same yesterday, today, and forever — means the instruction and truth Elisha gave his servant remains equally valid and necessary for believers today.

context: In Hebrews chapter 13, and it's interesting, it's the same, in the same vein of Scripture where we find that the truth is given of the immutability of God in Hebrews 13 and verse number 8, where it says, Jesus Christ, the same yesterday and today and forever.
point_summary: God's immutability—that Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever—grounds the assurance of His unfailing presence and the believer's freedom from fear.

context: let your conversation be without covetousness and be content with such things as you have. For He has said, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee
point_summary: God's promise never to leave or forsake His people (Heb. 13:5) grounds Elisha's fearless "fear not" and supplies the believer's bold confession that the Lord is helper against any human threat.

context: so that we may boldly say, the Lord is my helper and I will not fear what man shall do unto me
point_summary: Hebrews 13:6 grounds the call to "fear not" in God's promise never to leave nor forsake His people, so believers can boldly declare the Lord as their helper against any human threat.

context: In verse number two, it says this, excuse me, verse number, yeah, I'll use verse number two, who for the joy that was set before Him endured the cross.
point_summary: Christ's willing endurance of the cross is grounded in the joy set before Him, demonstrating He sought not escape from Calvary but the accomplishment of substitutionary atonement.

context: Hebrews chapter 3 will repeat that same testimony of Moses of being faithful
point_summary: Hebrews 3:2 is cited as New Testament confirmation of the same divine testimony found in Numbers 12:7—that Moses was faithful in God's house—underscoring his faithfulness as a defining characteristic of his ministry.

context: That chastening that we speak about as a believer. Whom the Lord loveth, He chasteneth.
point_summary: God's escalating covenant judgments in Leviticus 26 are framed as chastening love, with Hebrews 12:6 confirming that divine discipline is an Old and New Testament principle directed toward restoration.

reference_text: Whom the Lord loveth, He chasteneth
context: And we understand that not only in Proverbs, that's an Old Testament and New Testament principle. Whom the Lord loveth, He chasteneth.
point_summary: Divine chastening—cited from both Proverbs and Hebrews—is presented as an Old and New Testament principle: God's escalating judgments are purposeful acts of love intended to bring His people back to Himself.

context: We often quote Hebrews 13 in verse number five, that portion of that verse of Scripture that says, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee.
point_summary: God's promise never to leave nor forsake his people, cited from Hebrews 13:5, is offered as the premier example of a divine promise believers can claim and rejoice in as they trace God's faithfulness across their lives.

context: we're foreseeing we also are encompassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses. Let us lay aside every weight and the sin which doth so easily beset us, and let us run with patience the race that is set before us.
point_summary: Running with patience the race set before us (Heb. 12:1) is presented as evidence of spiritual maturity, paralleling the steadfast endurance Paul calls for in Colossians 1:11.

context: looking unto Jesus
point_summary: Endurance in the Christian race (Heb. 12:1) frames the call to patience in Col. 1:11, with Heb. 12:2's "looking unto Jesus" serving as the congregation's stated theme verse for the prior year.

context: The Bible tells us in Hebrews chapter 11 that there's pleasure in sin for a season.
point_summary: Hebrews 11's acknowledgment that sin holds genuine but temporary pleasure is set against the certainty of judgment, exposing why "lovers of pleasure" ultimately choose sin over God.

context: Hebrews 11 reminds us that without faith, it is impossible to please Him, for he that cometh to God must believe that he is, and that he's a rewarder of them that diligently seek him.
point_summary: Faith is defined here as simply taking God at His Word—believing He will do what He said—and is cited as the indispensable condition for pleasing God at all.

context: Hebrews chapter number 7 looking at verses 1 through 28
point_summary: Hebrews 7 develops what chapter 5 previewed: that Jesus Christ is the fulfillment of Psalm 110:4's prophecy of a superior priesthood after the order of Melchizedek.

context: verse number 6 of chapter 5 Thou art a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek
point_summary: Hebrews 5:6, quoting Psalm 110:4, introduced Christ's Melchizedekian priesthood as messianic prophecy whose full exposition is reserved for chapter 7.

context: it was in chapter number 5 that he said this the writer says in verse number 10 called of God and high priest after the order of Melchizedek
point_summary: Hebrews 5:10 introduced Christ's Melchizedekian priesthood as a preview that chapter 7 would fully develop, with verses 11–6:20 serving as a parenthetical warning before the subject resumes.

context: then in verse number 11 of whom we have many things to say
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11 marks where the writer previewed Christ's Melchizedekian priesthood but paused for warning, with chapter 7 now delivering the full exposition those "many things" promised.

context: Whether the forerunner is for us entered even Jesus
point_summary: Hebrews 6:20 functions as a structural hinge, closing the parenthetical warning of chapters 5–6 and returning the argument to Christ as high priest after the order of Melchizedek, fully developed in chapter 7.

context: For this Melchizedek chapter 7 verse number 1 King of Salem priest of the Most High God
point_summary: Hebrews 7:1 opens the sermon's exposition of Melchizedek as the Old Testament type whose description in verses 1–10 was previewed in chapter 5 and now fully developed to establish Christ's unchangeable priesthood.

context: to whom also Abraham gave a tenth part of all
point_summary: Melchizedek's name, interpreted as "king of righteousness" and "king of Salem" (king of peace), introduces the description of this priestly figure whose attributes foreshadow Christ's unchangeable priesthood.

context: Without father without mother without descent having neither beginning of days nor end of life
point_summary: Melchizedek's description in verses 1–10 — including his being without recorded father, mother, or genealogy — serves as the textual foundation for establishing Christ's eternal, unchangeable priesthood.

context: but this man speaking of Jesus because he continueth ever
point_summary: Because Christ "continueth ever," his priesthood is unchangeable — a permanence that becomes the sermon's controlling theme drawn directly from the language of verse 24.

context: half an unchangeable priesthood
point_summary: Christ's eternal, unending life is the ground of his unchangeable priesthood and his consequent ability to save to the uttermost through perpetual intercession.

context: Wherefore he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him Seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them.
point_summary: Christ's unchangeable priesthood, grounded in his endless life and continuous intercession, is the sermon's controlling theme, drawn directly from the contrast between Melchizedek's typological permanence and the Levitical priesthood's limitations.

context: We'll look at the entirety of the chapter if you're taking notes I have adopted the title from the end of verse number 24 an Unchangeable priesthood an Unchangeable priesthood.
point_summary: Christ's priesthood is called "unchangeable" in verse 24 because, unlike the Levitical priests who died, Jesus continues forever—a point the sermon takes as its governing title and theme.

context: In fact, if Hebrews did not give us the truth that is provided here in chapter 5 7 8 and in dealing with The order of Melchizedek.
point_summary: Hebrews 7's extended treatment of Melchizedek demonstrates that Christ holds an unchangeable priesthood, superior to the Levitical order, grounded in the brief but typologically loaded Genesis 14 account.

context: In fact, if Hebrews did not give us the truth that is provided here in chapter 5 7 8 and in dealing with The order of Melchizedek.
point_summary: Hebrews 7 establishes Christ's superiority over the Levitical priesthood by drawing on the brief Genesis 14 account of Melchizedek to demonstrate that Jesus holds an unchangeable, eternal priesthood able to save completely.

context: In fact, if Hebrews did not give us the truth that is provided here in chapter 5 7 8 and in dealing with The order of Melchizedek.
point_summary: Hebrews 7–8's treatment of Melchizedek demonstrates that the brief Genesis 14 account functions as a divinely intended type proving the superiority and unchangeable nature of Christ's priesthood.

context: we mentioned this as we studied the end of chapter number 6 these Jewish believers that are being written to in the book of Hebrews
point_summary: Hebrews 6 is referenced as the passage where the writer first introduces Abraham and the Melchizedek theme that chapter 7 then develops to demonstrate Christ's superior priesthood.

context: consider how great this man was unto whom, notice this phrase, even the patriarch Abraham gave the tenth of the spoils
point_summary: Melchizedek's greatness surpasses even Abraham's, demonstrated by the fact that Abraham — the revered patriarch — paid tithes to him, establishing Melchizedek's superior rank in the minds of Jewish believers.

context: have a commandment to take tithes of the people according to the law. That is, of their brethren, though they come out of the loins of Abraham.
point_summary: The Levitical priesthood's authority to collect tithes rested on Mosaic law, but Melchizedek received tithes from Abraham himself, establishing a greater priestly order by contrast.

context: And here, men that die receive tithes, but there he receiveth them of whom it is witnessed that he liveth.
point_summary: Melchizedek's reception of tithes from Abraham—while Levi was still in Abraham's loins—establishes Melchizedek's superiority over the Levitical priesthood, grounding the argument for Christ's superior priesthood.

context: And as I may so say, Levi also who receiveth tithes, notice this, paid tithes in Abraham.
point_summary: Levi's representative payment of tithes through Abraham to Melchizedek establishes Melchizedek's superiority over the Levitical priesthood, laying the groundwork for Christ's superior priesthood.

context: For he, speaking of Levi, was yet in the loins of his father when Melchizedek met him.
point_summary: Levi's representative payment of tithes through Abraham's loins establishes Melchizedek's superiority over the Levitical priesthood, laying the foundation for the argument that Christ's Melchizedekan priesthood surpasses Aaron's.

context: See, the Bible tells us in verse number 10 that he, speaking of Levi, was yet in the loins of his father.
point_summary: Levi's descent through Abraham's loins means that when Abraham paid tithes to Melchizedek, Levi himself—ancestor of the Aaronic priesthood—did so representatively, establishing Melchizedek's superiority over the Levitical order.

context: Remember, as we dealt with the latter part of chapter number six about the promises, Abraham received a promise, but he didn't obtain the promise until he endured with patience.
point_summary: Hebrews 6's promise-and-patience motif is invoked to press Jewish believers to persevere in Christ rather than revert to Judaism, since Abraham himself waited to obtain what God had promised.

context: But he whose descent is not counted from them received tithes of Abraham.
point_summary: Melchizedek's reception of tithes from Abraham and his blessing of the patriarch demonstrate that his priesthood stands outside and above the Levitical line, proving him greater even than Abraham himself.

context: And without all contradiction the less is blessed of the better.
point_summary: Because Melchizedek blessed Abraham—who held God's promises—the axiom of Hebrews 7:7 establishes Melchizedek as greater than Abraham, a mind-challenging claim used to argue Christ's priesthood surpasses the Levitical order.

context: Notice, the priesthood required the law, we see in verse number 11, it says for under it the people received the law
point_summary: The insufficiency of the Levitical priesthood is proven by the very existence of the Melchizedekian prophecy, since a perfect priesthood would have required no successor.

context: the Bible gives us here about the law in verse number 19, excuse me, the Bible tells us this, for the law made nothing perfect
point_summary: The law, like the Levitical priesthood, could make nothing perfect, making a better hope—by which we draw near to God—absolutely essential.

context: the insufficiency of the Levitical priesthood
point_summary: Just as the Levitical priesthood could not bring perfection, the law likewise could not give life but only condemn—serving instead as a guardian to lead sinners to Christ.

context: For the priesthood being changed, there is made of necessity a change also of the law.
point_summary: A change in priesthood necessarily entails a change in the law, establishing that Christ's Melchizedekian priesthood supersedes the Levitical order entirely.

context: For he of whom these things are spoken pertaineth to another tribe of which no man gave attendance at the altar.
point_summary: Christ's priesthood surpasses the Levitical order precisely because he belongs to Judah, a tribe with no Mosaic provision for priesthood, signaling a fundamentally different and higher priestly order.

context: for it is evident that our Lord sprang out of Judah, of which tribe Moses spake nothing concerning priesthood
point_summary: Christ's descent from Judah rather than Levi demonstrates that his priesthood operates on an entirely different order than the Levitical system, making him uniquely both king and priest after the order of Melchizedek.

context: for he testifieth, thou art a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek
point_summary: Christ's priesthood is validated not by Levitical lineage or legal commandment but by the power of an endless life, fulfilling the Melchizedekian order prophesied in Psalm 110.

context: for there is barely a disannulling of the commandment going before
point_summary: The Levitical law's weakness and unprofitableness rendered it unable to make anything perfect, necessitating its setting aside in favor of the better hope through which believers draw near to God.

context: seeing then that we have a great high priest, notice this, that has passed into the heavens. Jesus, the Son of God, let us hold fast our profession.
point_summary: Christ's priesthood surpasses the Levitical order because, unlike priests who died and sinned, he has passed permanently into the heavens as the sinless, eternal high priest.

context: We read it in verse 17 of chapter 7, and at the end of verse number 21, thou art a priest, what does it say, forever after the order of Melchizedek.
point_summary: Christ's priesthood is eternal—not temporary like the Levitical order—because, as Psalm 110:4 declares, he is a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek.

context: We read it in verse 17 of chapter 7, and at the end of verse number 21, thou art a priest, what does it say, forever after the order of Melchizedek.
point_summary: Christ's priesthood is eternal—confirmed by the oath of Psalm 110:4, "forever after the order of Melchizedek"—unlike the Levitical priests whose ministry was temporary and burdened by their own sinfulness.

context: We find that it said in verse number 16, the power of an endless life.
point_summary: Christ's priesthood is grounded in "the power of an endless life," contrasting the mortal, sin-bound Levitical priests with a risen Savior who is consecrated forevermore.

context: Notice with me in verse number 24, but this man, because he continueth ever. It says this hath an unchangeable priesthood.
point_summary: Christ's priesthood is superior to the Levitical because, continuing forever, he holds an unchangeable priesthood — unlike mortal priests who died and were replaced.

context: Tells us in verse number 25, wherefore he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him, seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them.
point_summary: Christ's eternal, unending life grounds his ability to save completely and to intercede perpetually—unlike the mortal Levitical priests whose ministry was interrupted by death.

context: Verse 28, the last part of the verse says, who is consecrated forevermore.
point_summary: Christ's priesthood is eternal because he is a risen, living Savior—consecrated forevermore—not a dead priest but one who ever lives to intercede.

context: he hath an unchangeable priesthood
point_summary: Christ's permanent, unchangeable priesthood is grounded in his resurrection and eternal life, distinguishing him from mortal priests whose ministry ended at death.

context: Jesus Christ, the same yesterday and today and forever.
point_summary: Christ's immutability—declared in Hebrews 13:8 as "the same yesterday and today and forever"—is grounded in his unchangeable character and confirmed by his own word in Malachi 3:6.

context: He's made, at the end of v. 26, higher than the heavens.
point_summary: Christ's holiness in Hebrews 7:26—described as harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners, and higher than the heavens—establishes His sinless superiority over the Levitical priesthood He came to fulfill.

context: Notice in v. 27, who needeth not daily as those high priests to offer up sacrifice, notice first for His own sins, and then for the people's.
point_summary: Christ's sinlessness meant He needed no sacrifice for Himself, and so His single self-offering was wholly for sinners, unlike the repeated sacrifices of the Levitical priests.

context: for this He did once when He offered up Himself
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all self-offering stands in deliberate contrast to the repeated daily sacrifices of the Levitical priests, establishing the sufficiency of his substitutionary atonement because he, being sinless, offered himself.

context: for every high priest who is ordained to offer gifts and sacrifices
point_summary: Every high priest must have something to offer, and Christ's singular, necessary offering was himself—the once-for-all sacrifice that fulfilled and surpassed the repeated offerings of the Levitical priesthood.

context: wherefore, he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all self-offering accomplishes what the repeated Levitical sacrifices could not—saving to the uttermost those who come to God through him, as he ever lives to intercede for them.

context: if therefore perfection were by the Levitical priesthood
point_summary: Hebrews 7:11 is cited as a contrast point: the Levitical priesthood could not achieve perfection, whereas Christ's single self-offering accomplishes complete salvation to the uttermost.

context: the law made nothing perfect
point_summary: The law's inability to make anything perfect stands in deliberate contrast to Christ's once-for-all offering, which accomplishes complete salvation and reconciliation for all who come to God through Him.

context: We find that here in the book of Hebrews in chapter number 7, as he began dealing with this subject
point_summary: Unlike the Levitical priesthood, which required repeated offerings that could never perfect the worshiper, Christ saves to the uttermost by living perpetually to intercede as mediator between God and sinners.

context: The blood of bulls and goats can never, the Bible tells us, it can never take away sins
point_summary: The repeated animal sacrifices of the Levitical system were inherently insufficient, serving only as daily reminders that the blood of bulls and goats cannot take away sins.

context: how he ever liveth to make intercession.
point_summary: Christ's eternal priesthood after the order of Melchizedek surpasses the Levitical priesthood precisely because he ever lives to make intercession, unlike mortal priests who died and required successors.

context: what a great presentation in Hebrews chapter number seven
point_summary: Christ's eternal priesthood after the order of Melchizedek, unlike the succession of mortal Levitical priests, means he ever lives to intercede—giving Jewish believers (and all believers) every reason not to abandon what they have in him.

context: hopefully make application through what we're dealing with even here in Hebrews 7 because we serve a God who lives
point_summary: Hebrews 7's declaration that "we serve a God who lives" grounds the practical call to live as salt and light before a sin-cursed world.

context: in Hebrews chapter number seven these Jewish believers were struggling
point_summary: Christ's superiority as the greater high priest after the order of Melchizedek is the argument against reverting to Judaism, since the Levitical priesthood could never accomplish what he accomplished.

context: He is able to save them to the uttermost that come to God
point_summary: Christ's saving power reaches "to the uttermost," distinguishing the gospel's transforming work from mere reformation or self-improvement through human righteousness.

context: In Hebrews chapter number six, so many people love to run to Hebrews chapter number six and say you can lose your salvation.
point_summary: Hebrews 6, often cited to argue that salvation can be lost, must be read in its surrounding context, where the same letter affirms God as the author of eternal salvation—making that interpretation self-contradictory.

context: it's quick and it's powerful and it's sharper than a two-edged sword
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labor of love, which you have showed toward his name, in that ye have ministered to the saints and do minister.
point_summary: God's recognition of believers' "labor of love" in Hebrews 6:10 is tied specifically to ongoing ministry to the saints—both past and present—as the visible evidence of loving one another.

context: ...by Him, therefore, let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually, that is, the...
point_summary: Hebrews 13:15 grounds the sermon's Thanksgiving theme by identifying praise offered "continually" through the lips as a sacrifice that pleases God.

context: remember them which have the rule over you, who have spoken unto you the word of God, whose faith follow, considering
point_summary: Hebrews 13:7 is presented as the first of ten reasons for believers to rejoice, grounding that rejoicing in the call to remember, follow the faith of, and consider the lives of spiritual leaders.

context: remember them which have the rule over you, who have spoken unto you the word of God, and then it says, whose faith follow, considering the end of their conversation
point_summary: Hebrews 13:7 grounds the first of ten reasons to rejoice in Hebrews 13:7–25: gratitude for spiritual leaders who spoke God's Word and whose faith-shaped lives are worthy of imitation.

context: obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves. Notice what it says, for they watch for your souls, as they that must give account
point_summary: Spiritual leaders bear the solemn accountability of watching for souls and must one day give account for that stewardship, a weight the sermon calls heavy.

context: as I began to meditate upon this truth in Hebrews 13 and verse number 7
point_summary: Hebrews 13:7 grounds the call to give thanks for spiritual leaders as a God-ordained reason for rejoicing, illustrated by the preacher's own formative experience of pastoral influence.

context: in Hebrews 13 and verse number 7 and verse number 17
point_summary: Hebrews 13:17, read alongside verse 7, grounds the first reason for thanksgiving in Hebrews 13: God has ordained spiritual leaders for the church, and believers owe gratitude for them.

context: and I would say this morning that as we look at Hebrews 13 and begin looking at these reasons to rejoice, that it begins with identifying spiritual leadership and thanking God for spiritual leaders.
point_summary: Hebrews 13 opens a series of reasons to rejoice, the first being gratitude for spiritual leaders who faithfully feed the flock through prayer and the ministry of the Word.

context: Number two, if you're taking notes back in Hebrews 13, look in verse number eight, the Bible says this, Jesus Christ, the same yesterday and today and forever.
point_summary: Christ's immutability — his being the same yesterday, today, and forever — is presented as a distinct and certain reason for rejoicing amid a world of rapid, unwelcome change.

context: Jesus Christ, the same yesterday and today and forever.
point_summary: Hebrews 13:8 grounds a second reason to rejoice: Christ's immutability — that He is the same yesterday, today, and forever — means the God believers serve never changes, even as everything around them does.

context: And I hope this morning that as you begin to think this week as we celebrate Thanksgiving and we think about the principle of giving thanks and praising God, that we would find even here in Hebrews 13, some real good reasons for which we should rejoice.
point_summary: Hebrews 13:8 grounds the doctrine of Christ's immutability—that he is the same yesterday, today, and forever—as a cause for Thanksgiving rejoicing, confirmed by Malachi 3:6's declaration that God does not change.

context: 'Be not carried about with divers in strange doctrines, 'for it is a good thing that the heart 'be established with grace, not with meats 'which have not profited them 'that have been occupied therein.'
point_summary: Grounding the heart in grace through sound doctrine is the gift the Word of God provides, without which believers would be swept away by the diverse and strange teachings that proliferate wherever Scripture is absent.

context: The Bible says in verse number 10, we have an altar whereof they have no right to eat which serves the tabernacle.
point_summary: Hebrews 13:10's reference to the altar from which tabernacle servants had no right to eat sets up a contrast resolved in verse 12: Christ's substitutionary suffering outside the gate to sanctify his people.

context: For the bodies of those beasts whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin are burned without the camp.
point_summary: Hebrews 13:11 grounds the fourth reason for thanksgiving by anchoring Christ's substitutionary suffering outside the gate in the Old Testament pattern of sin offerings burned outside the camp.

context: But let's read verse 10 through 12.
point_summary: Hebrews 13:12 grounds the fourth reason for thanksgiving: Christ's suffering outside the gate, paralleling the Old Testament sin offering, accomplished substitutionary atonement for His people.

context: wherefore Jesus also that he might sanctify the people with his own blood suffered without the gate
point_summary: Christ's suffering outside the gate to sanctify his people with his own blood is the ground of substitutionary atonement that calls believers to continual, not merely seasonal, praise.

context: let us go forth therefore into him without the camp bearing his reproach
point_summary: Bearing Christ's reproach outside the camp is the posture from which believers continually offer the sacrifice of praise, grounded in the substitutionary atonement that gives them every reason to give thanks.

context: for here we have no continuing city but we seek one to come
point_summary: Because this world is not our permanent home, believers are called to continual, not merely seasonal, thanksgiving and praise to God.

context: by him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually that is the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name
point_summary: Continual praise—not seasonal thanksgiving—is the fitting response to Christ's substitutionary atonement, with the word "continually" in verse 15 marking this sacrifice of praise as an ongoing, daily obligation.

context: but to do good and to communicate forget not for with such sacrifices God is well pleased
point_summary: Gratitude for the substitutionary atonement is not a once-a-year Thanksgiving observance but a continual, daily sacrifice of praise that pleases God.

context: For here, we have no continuing city, but we seek one to come.
point_summary: Because this present world holds no permanent city for believers, they are to rejoice in the heavenly home God has prepared, living as strangers and pilgrims whose true country lies ahead.

context: These all died in faith, not having received the promises, but having seen them afar off and were persuaded of them and embraced them and confessed that they were strangers and pilgrims on the earth.
point_summary: Faith's heroes died without receiving the promised inheritance, yet confessed themselves strangers and pilgrims on earth, declaring by that confession that their true home was a heavenly city still to come.

context: The Bible talks about the fact that they were looking for a better city, and it says in verse number 14, for they that say such things declare plainly that they seek a country...
point_summary: Believers' confession that they seek a heavenly country—echoing Hebrews 11:13–16—declares plainly that this world is not their home, grounding present rejoicing in the certainty of heaven.

context: ...for truly, if they had been mindful of that country from whence they came out, they might have had opportunity to have returned...
point_summary: Hebrews 11:15's reminder that the patriarchs could have returned to their former country grounds the sermon's point that believers are strangers and pilgrims whose true, reserved home is heaven.

context: ...but now they desire a better country that is in heavenly, wherefore God is not ashamed to be called their God, for he hath prepared for them a city.
point_summary: God's preparation of a heavenly city for the faithful—as declared in Hebrews 11:16—grounds the believer's identity as a stranger and pilgrim whose true, unchanging home lies ahead.

context: Here in Hebrews 13, in verse number 14, it says for here we have no continuing city, but we seek one to come.
point_summary: Earthly life offers no permanent city, and believers are therefore pilgrims whose true and settled home awaits them in heaven, a certainty that grounds present rejoicing.

context: ...as we finished chapter number 12 a couple of Sunday mornings ago and looked at verses 18 through 29...
point_summary: Hebrews 12:18–29's contrast between Mount Sinai and Mount Zion grounds the believer's certain, immutable hope of heaven as a settled finish line that sustains rejoicing amid present difficulty.

context: ...as we finished chapter number 12 a couple of Sunday mornings ago and looked at verses 18 through 29...
point_summary: God's consuming fire in Hebrews 12:29 anchors a prior sermon's contrast between Mount Sinai and Mount Zion, establishing heaven's certainty as the believer's settled, unchangeable finish line.

context: as we look at Hebrews chapter number 13, and examine the first five of these reasons to rejoice
point_summary: Hebrews 13 supplies five reasons for thanksgiving—spiritual leaders, Christ's immutability, and the Word among them—set against the backdrop of a changing, chaotic world.

context: I'm thankful as we look at Hebrews 13, not only for spiritual leaders, but I'm thankful for the immutability of Christ. Oh, all the things around us are changing, but our God's the same, yesterday and today and forever.
point_summary: Amid a world of constant change, Christ's immutability — he is the same yesterday, today, and forever — stands as a distinct reason for Thanksgiving-season rejoicing.

context: we have the Word of God. We've mentioned this morning that that's one of the reasons that is given here in Hebrews 13 for which we should rejoice
point_summary: Hebrews 13 supplies settled, unchanging reasons for rejoicing—including the immutability of Christ, the Word of God, substitutionary atonement, and the believer's heavenly home.

context: But after this, the judgment is what the Bible says in the book of Hebrews in the ninth chapter.
point_summary: Hebrews 9 is cited only to anchor the warning that after physical death comes judgment, supporting the call to be born again before that moment arrives.

context: But Jesus, after he made one sacrifice for sin forever, the Bible tells us, on verse number 12, he sat down on the right hand of God.
point_summary: Christ's single, finished sacrifice—contrasted with the law's repeated offerings—secured permanent forgiveness, so that God remembers the believer's sins no more.

context: Look down at verse 17, it says, and their sins and iniquities will I remember no more.
point_summary: God's promise that He will remember the sins of the saved "no more" grounds the assurance that believers will not face condemnation for sin already covered by Christ's one sufficient sacrifice.

context: Hebrews 6 and verse number 10 says, for God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labor of love which you have showed toward his name and that you have ministered to the saints and do minister.
point_summary: God's perfect memory of every work and labor of love done in His name grounds the certainty that nothing done for Christ will be overlooked at the judgment seat.

context: it's a fearful thing to fall into the hands of an angry God
point_summary: Falling into the hands of an angry God is cited as the fearful reality behind Paul's phrase "the terror of the Lord," motivating evangelism because the lost face eternal judgment.

context: Our God, here's what the Bible says, is a consuming fire
point_summary: God's consuming fire nature (Heb. 12:29) grounds the "terror of the Lord" in 2 Cor. 5:11, explaining why accountability before Him motivates evangelism.

context: Hebrews chapter number 12 says, looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Amid life's continuing journey of adversity and varied relationships, Hebrews 12's call to look unto Jesus as the author and finisher of faith provides the orienting instruction believers need to press forward.

context: And the Bible tells us in Hebrews chapter number two that we no longer have to have the fear of death.
point_summary: Hebrews 2 is cited to affirm that Christ's conquest of death frees believers from the fear of death.

context: without the shedding of blood, there's no remission
point_summary: Without the shedding of blood there is no remission, grounding Christ's washing of believers from their sins in his own blood in the necessity of blood for forgiveness.

context: Hebrews 13 in verse 15 talks about bringing continually the sacrifice of the praise of our lips
point_summary: Believers, made kings and priests, have the privilege of continually offering the sacrifice of praise to God as an expression of that priestly role.

context: 'Having therefore, brethren, boldness to enter 'into the holiest by the blood of Jesus, 'by a new and living way, 'which he hath consecrated for us through the veil, 'that is to say, his flesh, 'and having an high priest over the house of God, 'let us draw near with a true heart 'in full assurance of faith, 'having our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience 'and our bodies washed with pure water. 'Let us hold fast the profession of our faith 'without wavering, for he is faithful, that promised. 'And let us consider one another 'to provoke and to love and to good works, 'not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together
point_summary: The blood of Jesus has opened a bold, new, and living way of access into the holiest, a truth the sermon frames as central to Hebrews' overarching theme of Christ's superiority.

context: having therefore brethren, notice this, boldness to enter into the holiest, notice what it says, by the blood of Jesus
point_summary: The address "brethren" in verse 19 signals that the exhortations now following are directed to the already-saved, those born again, as the writer turns doctrinal exposition toward its practical impact on believers' lives.

context: because of what the Lord Jesus Christ did, because of his sacrifice, when he offered himself his sacrifice for sin, his death, burial and glorious resurrection, that one thing that we have received according to verses 19 and 20 is access into the holiest of all
point_summary: Access into the holiest of all is received by believers through Christ's blood, replacing the once-yearly, blood-required entry of the Levitical high priest into the holy of holies.

context: we have boldness to enter into the holiest by the blood of Jesus
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all sacrifice has opened permanent, bold access into the holiest place for all believers—access the high priest could only briefly and conditionally enter once a year by blood.

context: by a new and living way, which he hath consecrated for us through the veil, that is to say, his flesh
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all sacrifice has opened permanent, bold access into the holiest of all—the way the high priest could only enter once a year with blood, now freely available to all through the blood of Jesus.

context: it could never with those sacrifices which they offered year by year continually make the comers thereunto perfect
point_summary: The repeated Old Testament sacrifices, unable to perfect the worshiper or take away sins, stand in contrast to Christ's once-for-all offering of His own flesh, which opened a new and living way into the holiest.

context: every priest standeth daily ministering and offering oftentimes the same sacrifices, notice, which can never take away sins
point_summary: The repetitive daily offerings of the Old Testament priesthood, which could never take away sins, stand in contrast to Christ's singular sacrifice that opened a new and living way into God's presence.

context: a new and living way, which He in verse number 20 hath consecrated for us through the veil
point_summary: Christ's torn flesh, like the torn temple veil, consecrated a new and living way into God's presence, replacing the repeated, ineffective animal sacrifices that could never permanently take away sin.

context: we now have access into the holiest in verses 19 and 20
point_summary: Access into the holiest of all is now available to believers because Christ's flesh, offered once for all, accomplished what the repeated animal sacrifices of the Old Testament could never do: permanently take away sins.

context: according to verse 21. Notice what he says. "...and having a high priest over the house of God."
point_summary: Christ's superiority as the great High Priest over the house of God is the culminating truth toward which the preceding chapters of Hebrews have been building, establishing his supremacy over the Old Testament priesthood.

context: ...seeing then that we have a great High Priest that is passed into the heavens. Jesus, the Son of God, let us hold fast our profession...
point_summary: Christ's superiority over the Levitical priesthood is grounded in His having passed into the heavens as the great High Priest, fully sympathetic to human weakness yet sinless.

context: And you think about what He says at the beginning of verse number one in Hebrews 10, the law having a shadow of good things to come.
point_summary: The Old Testament sacrificial system, ordained by God, was only a shadow of good things to come—not the real thing—pointing forward to what Christ would perfectly accomplish.

context: having our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience and our bodies washed with pure water
point_summary: Because Christ has opened the way, the proper response to having access to God is not passive acknowledgment but active drawing near, with hearts sprinkled clean and bodies washed.

context: this truth of Hebrews 10 that we're dealing with this morning
point_summary: Hebrews 10:22's call to "draw near" is grounded in the believer's unique access to God—made possible by heart-sprinkling from an evil conscience, bodily washing, justification, and imputed righteousness unavailable to the unbeliever.

context: We find that in Hebrews 4
point_summary: Hebrews 4:16's call to "come boldly" is presented as the direct counterpart to Hebrews 10:22, both grounded in Christ's high-priestly work that grants justified believers confident access to God.

context: how wonderful the connection is here between Hebrews 10 and verse 22
point_summary: The cleansed conscience and imputed righteousness described in Hebrews 10:22 are the grounds on which believers—unlike the lost—have direct, bold access to God's throne of grace.

context: Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need.
point_summary: Because believers have been justified and their hearts sprinkled clean, they may approach God's throne boldly and in full assurance—access the lost do not yet possess.

context: He tells these believers to hold fast the profession of our faith without wavering.
point_summary: Hebrews 10:23 calls believers to hold fast their confession of faith without wavering, grounding that steadfastness not in wishful thinking but in certain expectation based on revealed truth.

context: hold fast the profession of our faith without wavering, for He is faithful, that promised
point_summary: Holding fast the confession without wavering is grounded not in human resolve but in God's own faithfulness to His promises, as the verse's closing phrase—"he is faithful that promised"—makes explicit.

context: when God made promise to Abraham
point_summary: God's oath to Abraham—sworn by himself because no greater existed—grounds the certainty of his promises and explains why believers can hold fast their confession without wavering.

context: because he could swear by no greater, he swore by himself.
point_summary: God's oath to Abraham — swearing by himself because no greater existed — grounds the certainty of his promises and the unshakeable hope that anchors the believer's soul.

context: and so after he had patiently endured, he obtained the promise.
point_summary: Abraham's patient endurance until he obtained the promise of the seed serves as the Old Testament anchor for the believer's own unwavering faith in God's certain, immutable promises.

context: Notice in verse number 17 how the writer again is speaking about this Old Testament truth,
point_summary: God's oath to Abraham in Hebrews 6:17 demonstrates the immutability of His counsel, assuring present-day heirs of promise that His word cannot change and their hope is anchored.

context: that by two immutable things in which it was impossible for God to lie, we might have a strong consolation who have fled for refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us
point_summary: God's two immutable things—his promise and his oath—make it impossible for him to lie, giving believers strong consolation and a sure anchor for their hope.

context: which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and steadfast, and which entereth into that within the veil
point_summary: Hope described as an anchor of the soul is grounded in God's two immutable guarantees—his oath and his promise—making it impossible for God to lie and certain that he will fulfill what he has said.

context: let us hold fast the profession of our faith without wavering, why? Because he is faithful that promised
point_summary: God's faithfulness to His promises—not human certainty—is the ground for holding fast the confession without wavering, since He who promised cannot lie.

context: let us consider one another and as we are attentively perceiving considering one another were to provoke were to incite them number one to love and number two to good works
point_summary: Hebrews 10:24's call to "consider one another" is expounded as a charge to actively provoke fellow believers toward love—empowered by the indwelling Holy Spirit—and toward good works, which are distinguished from any saving merit.

context: provoke one another unto love and to good works
point_summary: Mutual consideration among believers, expressed as provoking one another unto love and good works, is the corporate duty that makes the assembling of the church the necessary context for using spiritual gifts.

context: not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together
point_summary: Forsaking the assembly undermines the very context where spiritual gifts are exercised and believers exhort one another unto love and good works.

context: I know that Hebrews 10 in verse number 25 has been a verse that has been used often in 2020
point_summary: Assembling together is God's designed context for believers to exercise their spiritual gifts and fulfill the mutual obligations of considering, provoking, and exhorting one another.

context: he said I will never leave thee nor forsake thing
point_summary: God's promise in Hebrews 13:5 — "I will never leave thee nor forsake thee" — is cited as the ground of comfort that flows from his omnipresence for every child of God.

context: Hebrews 13 and verse number 8 says, Jesus Christ, the same yesterday and today and forever.
point_summary: Christ's immutability—unlike people whose attitudes shift daily—grounds confidence in Scripture's plan of redemption, since the same Jesus who saved in Paul's day saves identically today.

context: The Bible tells us in Hebrews 13, verse 20 and 21, I want you to see these verses as we close. The Bible says, now the God of peace that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great shepherd of the sheep through the blood of the everlasting covenant.
point_summary: Faithful service is possible because God himself is working in believers to accomplish his will, making the call to run for an audience of one both a command and a divine enablement.

context: The Bible tells us in Hebrews 13, verse 20 and 21, I want you to see these verses as we close. The Bible says, now the God of peace that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great shepherd of the sheep through the blood of the everlasting covenant. Notice where it says, make you perfect in every what? Good work. To do His what? To do His will. Working in you, aren't you thankful He's working in us?
point_summary: God equips believers to do His will and works within them toward what is well-pleasing in His sight—the basis for faithful service rendered to an audience of One regardless of visible results.

context: we're strangers and pilgrims, read that in Hebrews 11.
point_summary: Hebrews 11 is cited as the scriptural basis for the identity of believers as strangers and pilgrims, reinforcing that this world is not their home.

context: And in Hebrews chapter number five, and beginning in verse number 11, notice what the Bible says. Of whom we have many
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity renders believers "dull of hearing," making deep biblical truth hard to communicate—a condition Paul identifies in Corinth through the envying, strife, and divisions among them.

context: And so we find that he says there in Hebrews 5, there's some things that I want to tell you. They're hard to be uttered. Why? Because you're dull of hearing.
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11 is cited as a parallel to 1 Corinthians 3 showing that spiritual immaturity—"dull of hearing"—requires a teacher to meet learners where they are rather than where they should be.

context: think about what the book of Hebrews says in Hebrews 4 and verse number 16
point_summary: Access to God in prayer is a privilege granted to His children, who—like a child approaching a father—may come boldly to the throne of grace because of the relationship made possible through Christ's sacrifice.

point_summary: No man assumes the high priestly office by self-appointment; it requires a divine call, just as Aaron's was, and Christ's priesthood is no exception.

context: So also Christ glorified not himself to be made an high priest, but he that said unto him, thou art my son, this today have I begotten thee.
point_summary: Christ did not appoint himself High Priest but was designated by the Father, as confirmed by the divine declaration "Thou art my Son," establishing his priestly office by divine call rather than self-assertion.

point_summary: Christ's eternal priesthood is grounded not in self-appointment but in divine calling, as Psalm 110:4 ("Thou art a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek") is cited to confirm that God himself designated Jesus to this office.

context: for verily he took not on him the nature of angels, but he took on him the seed of Abraham
point_summary: Christ's incarnation as the seed of Abraham, not as an angel, was necessary precisely because only a human being could die as a substitutionary sacrifice for the sins of mankind.

context: he was tempted in all points yet without sin, but we have not a high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of
