context: where the Bible says that we have not, and a high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities, but was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin.
point_summary: Christ's being "touched with the feeling of our infirmities" establishes that, unlike flawed Old Testament priests, He perfectly embodies the compassionate demeanor God always intended the high-priestly role to reflect.

context: No, no man taketh this honor unto himself, but he that is called
point_summary: The divine appointment of Aaron — confirmed by the budding rod after Korah's rebellion — illustrates that the priestly office cannot be self-assumed but must be conferred by God's explicit call, as the cases of Korah and Uzziah demonstrate through judgment.

context: so also Christ glorified not himself to be made in high priest
point_summary: Christ's appointment as High Priest is grounded in the Father's sovereign call, not self-glorification, with Psalm 2:7 cited as Old Testament prophecy now properly fulfilled in Jesus.

context: unto which of the angels said he at any time, thou art my son, this day have I begotten thee
point_summary: Hebrews 1:5's quotation of Psalm 2:7 ("Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee") is shown to refer not to the incarnation but to the resurrection of Christ.

context: Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee.
point_summary: The "Thou art my Son" quotation in Hebrews 5:5, drawn from Psalm 2:7, refers not to Christ's incarnation but to His resurrection, as confirmed by Paul's parallel use of the same psalm in Acts 13:33.

context: we come to Psalm 2 in verse number 7, that's referenced here in Hebrews 5 in verse number 5
point_summary: The writer of Hebrews applies Psalm 2:7 to Christ's resurrection—not his birth—as Old Testament prophecy appropriately fulfilled in Jesus as the appointed High Priest.

context: the truth of Psalm 110 in verse number 4 is being applied here in Hebrews 5, 6
point_summary: Hebrews 5:6 cites Psalm 110:4, a psalm universally recognized by Jewish leaders as messianic, confirming that its priesthood prophecy applies directly to Jesus as the promised Messiah.

context: In fact, the references in Hebrews give us far more information
point_summary: Christ's priesthood after the order of Melchizedek fulfills Old Testament prophecy (Ps. 110) and transcends the Levitical pattern precisely because, unlike Israel's kings, Jesus is simultaneously priest and king.

reference_text: though He were a son in verse number eight, yet learned the obedience by the things which He suffered
context: though He were a son in verse number eight, yet learned the obedience by the things which He suffered
point_summary: Though Christ possessed divine omniscience, He experientially learned obedience through human suffering, gaining firsthand knowledge that equips Him to help and sympathize with those He intercedes for.

context: chapter number four and verses 14 and 15 in understanding the way in which He's able to help us
point_summary: Christ's experiential learning through human suffering—including Gethsemane's spiritual battle—is what qualifies Him, as declared in Hebrews 4:14–15, to sympathize with and succor those He intercedes for as great High Priest.

context: chapter number four and verses 14 and 15 in understanding the way in which He's able to help us
point_summary: Christ's experiential learning through human suffering—not merely divine omniscience—is what qualifies Him to be touched with the feeling of our infirmities and to succor those who are tempted.

reference_text: chapter number two, as we mentioned in verses 17
context: chapter number two, as we mentioned in verses 17 and 18, He came, it behooved Him to be made like unto His brethren
point_summary: Christ's incarnation was necessary not only to make reconciliation for sins but also so that, having suffered temptation in human flesh, He could experientially succor those who are tempted.

reference_text: chapter number two, as we mentioned in verses 18
context: chapter number two, as we mentioned in verses 17 and 18, He came, it behooved Him to be made like unto His brethren
point_summary: Christ's suffering in human flesh gave Him experiential knowledge of temptation, enabling Him to succor those who are tempted in a way His divine omniscience alone could not.

context: He is touched with the feeling of our infirmities.
point_summary: Christ's experiential learning of obedience through suffering in human flesh—distinct from His omniscient divine knowledge—is what qualifies Him to be touched with the feeling of our infirmities and to succor the tempted.

context: being made perfect, He became the author of eternal salvation unto all them that obey Him.
point_summary: "Made perfect" in Hebrews 5:9 means completion of Christ's mission, not moral improvement, so that He became the author of eternal salvation for all who obey Him.

context: seeing then that we have a great high priest
point_summary: Christ's title "great high priest" in Hebrews 4:14 is unique — no Old Testament high priest ever bore that designation — and his passing through the heavens, not merely the earthly veil, marks his priesthood as categorically superior.

context: where the Bible tells us there with regard
point_summary: Christ's heavenly session—seated at God's right hand after one final sacrifice—demonstrates that He passed through the heavens themselves, not merely the earthly veil into the Holy of Holies.

context: but this man, after he'd offered one sacrifice for sins forever, sat down on the right hand of God
point_summary: Christ's singular, completed sacrifice—offered once for sins forever—positioned him seated at God's right hand, distinguishing his heavenly priesthood from the repeated earthly ministry of Levitical priests.

context: now when these things were thus ordained, the priest went always into the first tabernacle, accomplishing the service of God
point_summary: Whereas the Levitical priests entered only the earthly holy place to perform their service, Christ's superior priesthood is marked by passing through the heavens into the very throne room of God.

context: But notice in verse number seven
point_summary: Hebrews 9:7 establishes the contrast: the Levitical high priest entered only an earthly Holy of Holies once yearly, whereas Christ passed through the heavens into God's actual throne room.

context: but into the second went the high priest alone, once every year, not without blood, which he offered for himself and for the errors of the people.
point_summary: Unlike the Levitical high priest who entered the Holy of Holies once yearly offering blood for his own sins and the people's, Christ's sinlessness meant his sacrifice was entirely for others, not himself.

context: We notice here, again, in chapter number four and verse number 15 of Hebrews, where the Bible says,
point_summary: Christ's sinlessness, affirmed in Hebrews 4:15 ("yet without sin"), is presented as the qualification that made His offering for the world's sin sufficient—unlike Levitical priests who first atoned for their own.

context: for we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities, but was in all points tempted like as we are yet without sin.
point_summary: Christ's sinlessness—"without sin" per Hebrews 4:15—is cited as the reason he needed no atoning sacrifice for himself, unlike Levitical priests, making his offering solely for the world's sin.

context: when they would go in to make these sacrifices for sins, notice in verse number 3, and by reason hereof he ought as for the people, so also for himself to offer for sins.
point_summary: Unlike the sinless Christ, the Levitical high priest was required by Hebrews 5:3 to offer sacrifice for his own sins before atoning for the people's, disqualifying him from being a universal Savior.

context: where the Bible says that he could only go into the Holy of Holies once every year,
point_summary: Unlike the Levitical high priest who entered the Holy of Holies once yearly offering blood for his own sins and the people's, Christ needed no such self-offering because he was sinless.

context: thou art a priest forever, after the order of Melchizedek
point_summary: Christ's priesthood is superior in duration because, unlike the mortal Levitical priests, he is a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek and lives perpetually to intercede.

context: he became the author of eternal salvation
point_summary: Christ's superiority as High Priest is grounded in his becoming the author of eternal salvation, a permanence contrasted with the Levitical priests who died and could not continue.

context: wherefore he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him, seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them
point_summary: Christ's priesthood is superior in duration because, living forever, he saves to the uttermost and perpetually intercedes—unlike the Levitical priests who were cut off by death.

context: and they truly were many priests because they were not suffered to continue by reason of death.
point_summary: Unlike the Levitical priests, who were many precisely because death prevented any one of them from continuing, Christ's priesthood is superior in its permanence.

context: but this man, after he made one sacrifice for sin forever, sat down on the right hand of God
point_summary: Unlike the repeated annual sacrifices of the Levitical priesthood, Christ made one sacrifice for sin forever and then sat down—his finished, unrepeatable offering marking the superiority of his priesthood.

context: The Bible actually begins in chapter number 10 and verse number 1
point_summary: The repeated annual sacrifices of the Levitical law were only a shadow of good things to come, incapable of perfecting worshippers—contrasting the single, sufficient offering of Christ.

context: for the law having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things, can never with those sacrifices, notice this phrase, which they offered year by year continually, to make the comers thereunto perfect.
point_summary: The repeated, year-by-year Levitical sacrifices prove their own insufficiency, contrasting with Christ's single, permanent offering that accomplished what animal blood never could.

context: Because the blood of bulls and goats, notice, as we continue here in the passage of Scripture in verse number 4, it is not possible that the blood of bulls and goats should take away sins.
point_summary: The repeated animal sacrifices of the Levitical order were insufficient because the blood of bulls and goats cannot take away sins, contrasting the finality of Christ's single offering.

context: These individuals that are being addressed in Hebrews chapter 5, they had been saved long enough to go on to spiritual maturity.
point_summary: Hebrews 5:12–14 indicts believers who, having been saved long enough to teach others, remain spiritually immature, when God's design is progressive growth toward discernment and full maturity.

context: We find it's interesting in verse number 1 of chapter 6, he says, "...therefore leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ,"

context: ...but strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil.

context: strong meat, and it says that it belongeth to them that are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil
point_summary: Spiritual maturity, illustrated by the analogy of solid food belonging to the fully grown, is not reserved for a few but available to every believer who exercises their senses through use.

context: now the God of peace that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, that Great Shepherd of the sheep through the blood of the everlasting covenant

context: make you perfect in every good work to do His will, working in you that which is well-pleasing in His sight through Jesus Christ to whom be glory forever and ever, amen

context: those that are being addressed in Hebrews 5, that were said to be dull of hearing

context: the warning of Hebrews chapter number 5, verses 11 through 14

context: the warning of Hebrews chapter number 5, verses 11 through 14
point_summary: Spiritual maturity, grounded in the profitable word of God, enables believers to receive and benefit from the full weight of sound doctrine a spiritual leader desires to impart.

context: That we would be able to have that strong meat. That we would have the ability to discern between both good and evil.
point_summary: Spiritual maturity, marked by the capacity for strong meat and discernment between good and evil, is the goal toward which believers must press, in contrast to the immaturity that hinders receiving deeper truth.

context: We think about the truth of Hebrews four in verse number
point_summary: Confident, bold access to God's throne of grace is made possible by Christ our great High Priest, who ever lives to intercede—grounding prayer's confidence in him, not in the believer's eloquence.

context: let us therefore come boldly under the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in the time of need

context: this truth in verse 14, and this is the confidence
point_summary: The Greek word translated "confidence" in 1 John 5:14 and "boldly" in Hebrews 4:16 are the same term, grounding bold prayer in Christ's ongoing intercession as our great High Priest.

point_summary: Hebrews 12:2's declaration that Christ endured the cross "for the joy set before Him" is cited to show He willingly embraced Calvary, not prayed to avoid it.

context: He will never leave thee nor forsake thee.

context: The Bible says now faith is the substance of things hoped for the evidence of things not seen

context: For by it the elders obtained a good report through faith. context: We understood that in Hebrews 10 in verse number 22 that the recipients were instructed to let us draw near With a true heart remember this in full assurance of faith

context: Later in the chapter. They were instructed in verse number 37 excuse me 38 now the just shall live by faith

context: as we end the Chapter the 10th chapter and you come to the latter part of verse number 39 It says but we are of them that believe to the saving of the soul We're not of them who draw back unto perdition

context: now faith is And he's going to give us this great description of what faith is

context: we have a Responsibility as the people of God to live by faith that reference that we found at the end of Hebrews chapter number 10

context: faith is explained. He says now faith is notice this word substance faith is the
point_summary: Hebrews 11:1 is treated as a foundational definition correcting widespread misunderstanding of faith, with "substance" explained as that which rests on fact, producing genuine confidence and assurance rather than mere theory.

reference_text: the 11th chapter the book of Hebrews
context: as we go throughout the book of the 11th chapter the book of Hebrews over the next several weeks
point_summary: Faith in Hebrews 11 is presented as active trust—not mere intellectual acknowledgment—defined simply as "taking God at his word," with the chapter's examples to be explored over coming weeks.

context: It's interesting that Hebrews 1039 when he says that we are of them that believe is that same word that we saw?
point_summary: Faith, as the Greek root underlying both "persuaded" (2 Timothy 1:12) and "believe" (Hebrews 10:39) reveals, is fundamentally being convinced or persuaded of what God has said.

context: for by it the elders Obtained a good report
point_summary: Faith exercised by the elders of Hebrews 11 resulted in a "good report"—meaning they received God's approval—because they lived by faith.

context: but without faith it is impossible to please Him. For he that cometh to God must believe that He is, and that He is a rewarder of them that diligently seek Him.

context: without it, it's impossible to please Him.
point_summary: Living by faith—not by sight—is the divinely appointed means by which God's people please Him, since without it pleasing Him is impossible.

context: the elders that are referenced here in verse number two
point_summary: Faith's connection to divine approval is established here: the elders of Hebrews 11 obtained God's commendation precisely because they lived by faith, and this pleasing of God through faith is presented as the universal desire and calling of believers.

context: The Bible says, through faith, what's that next word? We. Through faith, we understand that the worlds were framed by the Word of God so that things which are seen were not made of things which do appear.
point_summary: Amid a chapter naming individual heroes of faith, Hebrews 11:3 uniquely shifts to "we," drawing every believer into the shared, faith-grounded understanding that the visible creation was framed by God's Word.

context: By faith, Abel, in verse four.
point_summary: Abel is named as the first individual in Hebrews 11's catalog of faith, illustrating the pattern that living by faith brings God's approval.

context: By faith, Enoch, in verse number five.
point_summary: Enoch's mention in verse 5 illustrates the broader principle of Hebrews 11 that those who exercise faith receive God's approval and please Him.

context: because Jesus destroyed and what He did on the cross of Calvary in destroying the works of the enemy, that we find that He delivered us from the fear of death
point_summary: Christ's work on the cross destroyed the enemy's power and thereby delivered believers from the fear of death, grounding Christian confidence in the face of dying.

context: through faith we understand that the worlds were framed by the Word of God
point_summary: Faith grasps what reason cannot prove—that the worlds were framed by God's spoken Word—and the visible evidence of design throughout the universe confirms that Word's creative power.

context: faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen
point_summary: Faith, defined in Hebrews 11:1 as the substance of things hoped for and the evidence of things not seen, is the faculty by which believers take God at His Word regarding unseen realities, including creation.

context: so that things which are seen are not made of things which do appear
point_summary: Faith, not empirical observation, is the means by which believers understand that the visible universe was made from what does not appear—a truth the sermon connects to atomic physics and God's spoken, sustaining power.

context: we started studying this book back in Hebrews 1
point_summary: Faith, not empirical observation, is the means by which believers understand that God holds all things together by His power, as established earlier in the series from Hebrews 1.

context: But he says in verse number 3, through faith we understand that the worlds were framed by the Word of God so that things which are seen were not made of things which do appear.
point_summary: Faith's first exercise, according to Hebrews 11:3, is understanding creation—that the visible world was framed by God's Word—a truth the sermon presents as the indestructible biblical foundation under sustained modern attack.

context: Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen.
point_summary: Faith defined in Hebrews 11:1 as substance and evidence grounds the congregation's confidence that God created all things from nothing, a conviction held by faith rather than sight.

context: For by it the elders obtained a good report.
point_summary: Faith in God as Creator is the first and foundational example of how the elders obtained a good report, establishing that belief in creation belongs to the same trust that pleases God.

context: and that as we find it in the latter part of the verse, so that things which are seen were not made of things which do appear

context: As we study the rest of chapter 11, we're going to see how people exercised faith.

context: the significance of verse number 3 right at the front of all of Hebrews chapter 11, how important that it is that we understand through faith that He is the Creator.
point_summary: Recognizing God as Creator, as affirmed in Hebrews 11:3, is presented as the foundational conviction that gives believers confidence to trust God in every subsequent trial and circumstance.

context: as we begin to study the lives of those that are recorded in Hebrews 11 and were challenged by their testimony and their exercising of faith
point_summary: Faith in Hebrews 11 is presented as the pattern of God's people taking him at his word, with their recorded testimonies serving as a challenge and encouragement to present believers to live likewise.

reference_text: without faith it's impossible to please you

context: but beloved We are persuaded better things of you and things that accompany salvation Though we thus speak

context: that you be not slothful but followers of them who through faith and patience Inherit the promises

context: you notice in our text of Scripture here in Hebrews 5 verse number 11 Down through really the entirety of chapter number 6

context: Notice what the writer of Hebrews says to these believers that he's writing to. context: for God is not unrighteous to forget your work, notice, and labor of love, and labor of love, which ye have showed toward his name, and that ye have ministered to the saints, and do minister.

context: as we look at these two verses of Scripture in Hebrews 6 9 and 10 and we think about the evidence of conversion
point_summary: Hebrews 6:9–10 is read as a call to self-examination, asking whether visible evidences—works, service, labor of love—accompany one's conversion and confirm genuine new birth.

context: as we look at these two verses of Scripture in Hebrews 6 9 and 10 and we think about the evidence of conversion
point_summary: God's recognition of believers' labor of love serves as evidence of genuine conversion, prompting self-examination of whether such fruit accompanies one's own profession of faith.

context: the reality is that this exhortation in 2nd Corinthians 13 to examine themselves really I believe is an important exhortation in light of what we read here in Hebrews chapter number 6 is there evidence of conversion
point_summary: Hebrews 6:9–10 is read as a call to self-examination, with the works and labor of love mentioned there serving as evidences by which believers may assess the reality of their own conversion.

context: when the writer said of whom we have many things to say and hard to be uttered seeing your dull of hearing

context: the Bible says in verse 37, they were stoned.
point_summary: Faithful service to God, as seen in the stoning and sawing asunder of Hebrews 11's unnamed heroes, does not guarantee earthly blessing but calls believers to pursue God himself rather than his benefits.

context: without faith It is impossible to please him but he that cometh to God must believe that he is and a rewarder of them that diligently seek him

context: as we preached and taught through the book of Hebrews, we're mindful of that truth in Hebrews 7, in verse number 25, where the Bible says, wherefore He is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by Him, seeing He ever liveth to make intercession for them.

context: Notice in Hebrews chapter number 7, in verse number 25, the Bible is giving us truth here and we'll get to this passage here, Lord willing, in another month or so as we go through the truth, the teaching with regard to the priesthood of Christ and after the order of Melchizedek, not after that of Aaron. We find in verse 25, however, of Hebrews 7, the Bible says, wherefore He is able also

context: so Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many and unto them that look for Him shall He appear the second time without sin unto salvation
point_summary: Christ's second coming is certain and imminent—appearing "without sin unto salvation" to those who look for Him—leaving no time for last-minute preparation when He arrives.

context: for yet a little while and He that shall come will come and will not tarry
point_summary: Christ's return is certain and precisely timed—He will come and will not tarry—leaving no opportunity for last-minute preparation.

reference_text: He that shall come will come, and will not tarry
context: He that shall come will come, and will not tarry.
point_summary: The imminence of Christ's return, drawn from "He that shall come will come, and will not tarry," grounds an urgent call to redeem the time and press the Master's work with Acts-like urgency.

context: whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth
point_summary: Divine chastening through conviction is presented as a birthmark of genuine believers, confirming rather than threatening their salvation, because God disciplines those He loves.

context: And the Bible says that Enoch in verse number five, by faith Enoch was translated that he should not see death.
point_summary: Enoch's testimony in Hebrews 11:5 — that he pleased God — is held up as the attainable, defining mark of a life of faith, inviting believers to evaluate whether their own daily walk reflects the same.

context: the testimony of Enoch
point_summary: Enoch's testimony in Hebrews 11:5 is cited briefly to introduce verse 6's declaration that without faith it is impossible to please God, grounding the sermon's call to walk in the Spirit rather than the flesh.

context: without faith it is impossible to please Him, for they that believe, they that come to God must believe that He is and that He's a rewarder of them that diligently seek Him
point_summary: Faith is the indispensable condition for pleasing God, set in contrast with walking in the flesh, which Romans 8:8 declares makes pleasing Him impossible.

context: We find that in Hebrews chapter number 11, a verse that I mentioned a moment ago
point_summary: Hebrews 11:6 grounds the call to live by faith not in mystical experience but in the plain truth that without faith it is impossible to please God.

context: verse number 6, the Bible says, but without faith it is impossible to please Him.
point_summary: Living by faith is not a mystical concept but the practical act of taking God at His word, and Hebrews 11:6 grounds this by declaring that such trust is the very condition of pleasing God.

context: as Moses stood on the bank of the Red Sea and he believed God, that God said, raise up your arm with that staff and that rod, that He would part the Red Sea
point_summary: Faith is not mystical stepping into nothing, but the concrete trust illustrated throughout Hebrews 11—taking God at His word, as Moses did at the Red Sea, confident God will do what He says.

context: When Abraham went out, not knowing whether he went, but he went out believing God, God says that it was by faith that he went.
point_summary: Living by faith is not mystical but practical—trusting God at His word—as Abraham's departure into the unknown (Heb. 11:8) illustrates alongside other biblical examples of those who simply believed what God said He would do.

context: And the Bible says that without faith it is impossible to please Him, for he that cometh to God must believe that He is, and that He is a rewarder of them that diligently seek Him.
point_summary: Faith is impossible without believing both that God exists and that He rewards those who seek Him, a principle illustrated throughout Hebrews 11 by figures like Moses and Abraham who simply took God at His word.

context: God is pleased when we trust him
point_summary: Faith that pleases God grows through saturation in Scripture, because hearing God's Word—His character, attributes, and mighty acts—produces the belief that He exists and rewards those who seek Him.

context: by him, therefore, let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually. That is the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name.
point_summary: Praising God continually—offering the fruit of lips giving thanks to his name—pleases him and stands as the opposite of the complaining that displeases him.

context: How shall we escape if we neglect so great salvation we read in the book of Hebrews?
point_summary: Neglecting so great a salvation carries inescapable consequences, making the Lord's Supper a solemn call never to lose the awe of what Christ accomplished.

context: The Bible says that he ever liveth to make intercession for us.
point_summary: Christ's exalted, living intercession at the Father's right hand—drawn from Hebrews 7:25—grounds the Lord's Supper as an occasion for rejoicing, not merely solemn reflection.

context: Hebrews nine in verse 17 says, as is appointed unto man wants to die, but what? After this, the judgment.
point_summary: Every person encountered will face death and subsequent judgment, making the certainty of eternity a compelling motivation for proclaiming the gospel boldly.

context: for the law having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things, can never, with those sacrifices which they offered year by year continually, make the comers thereunto perfect.
point_summary: The Old Testament law, described in Hebrews 10:1 as "a shadow of good things to come," points forward to Christ, whose single sufficient sacrifice fulfilled what the law's repeated offerings could never accomplish.

context: but this man, speaking of the Lord Jesus Christ in verse number 12 of Hebrews 10, after he'd offered one sacrifice for sins forever, sat down on the right hand of God.
point_summary: Christ's single, permanent sacrifice—offered once and followed by his sitting down at God's right hand—stands as the substance to which the Old Testament's shadowy sacrificial system pointed, making legalistic observance obsolete.

context: In Hebrews chapter four, if you take a moment and turn over to the book of Hebrews, we'll actually look at a couple of passages in the book of Hebrews. In Hebrews chapter number four, it's a verse of scripture that many of us are familiar with where the Bible teaches us, instructs us with regard to our approach to the throne of grace. And Hebrews four and verse number 16, the Bible says, let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need.
point_summary: The command to pray without ceasing implies that God is always available, and Hebrews 4:16's invitation to come boldly to the throne of grace confirms the believer's unhindered access to God in every moment of need.

context: Having therefore, brethren, notice what it says, boldness to enter into the holiest, how? By the blood of Jesus, by a new and living way which he hath consecrated for us through the veil, that is to say, his flesh.
point_summary: Because Christ's blood rent the veil and opened the holy of holies, believers now possess bold, unhindered access to God's throne—access unavailable even to most Israelites under the old covenant.

context: and having a high priest over the house of God, let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith, having our hearts sprinkled
point_summary: Christ's role as great high priest over the house of God grounds the believer's bold, assured access to God in prayer—access unavailable under the Old Testament system.

reference_text: appointed unto man once to die, but after this, the judgment
context: Jesus is appointed unto man once to die, but after this, the judgment.
point_summary: Death and judgment as the universal consequence of sin establish why Christ's atoning death was the only acceptable payment — no human effort or corruptible thing could satisfy what mankind owes.

context: The opportunity that we have, as it says in Hebrews 4, that we can come boldly under the throne of grace
point_summary: Hebrews 4's "come boldly to the throne of grace" is cited as evidence that God's invitation to prayer is genuine and not merely a polite gesture.

context: In Hebrews chapter 11, we come to verse number 36. Notice what the Bible says, and others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings, yea, moreover of bonds and imprisonment.
point_summary: Hebrews 11:36–38 is cited to establish that severe, varied trials—mockings, scourgings, imprisonment, and death—are not unique to the psalmist but are the common experience of God's faithful people throughout history.

context: The Bible says they wandered about in sheepskins and goatskins being destitute, afflicted, tormented. It says of whom the world was not worthy, they wandered in deserts and in mountains and in dens and caves of the earth. These trials that are mentioned at the end of the great faith chapter in Hebrews chapter 11, there are some specifically that we can look to and we can piece things together and begin to understand who may be spoken of in these particular trials.
point_summary: Hebrews 11:36–38 is cited to establish that serious, varied trials are a documented reality for God's people, grounding the sermon's broader call to pour out the heart before God amid suffering.

context: wherefore in all things it behooved him to be made like unto his brethren, that he might be a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people
point_summary: Christ's incarnation and suffering qualify Him as a merciful, faithful High Priest who is able to give aid to the tempted, making Him the one before whom believers can safely pour out their hearts.

context: for in that he himself hath suffered, being tempted, he is able to succor them that are tempted.
point_summary: Because Christ himself suffered and was tempted, Hebrews 2:18 grounds the call to pour out one's heart to God in the assurance that he is able to give real aid to the tempted.

context: seeing then that we have a great high priest that is passed into the heavens, Jesus, the son of God, let us hold fast our profession
point_summary: Because Jesus, as great High Priest, has passed into the heavens yet was tempted in all points as we are, believers can come boldly to the throne of grace to pour out their hearts and receive help in time of need.

context: for we have not an high priest, which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities, but was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin.
point_summary: Because Christ was tempted in all points as we are yet without sin, he is a high priest genuinely touched by human infirmity and therefore approachable with complete transparency in prayer.

context: Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need.
point_summary: Because Christ is a high priest who was tempted in all points yet without sin, believers can come boldly to the throne of grace to obtain mercy and help when pouring out their hearts before God.

context: The Bible talks about in the book of Hebrews the deceitfulness of sin
point_summary: Sexual sin carries divine judgment, as Hebrews 13:4 declares that God himself will judge whoremongers and adulterers, a truth the sermon sets against Joseph's refusal of Potiphar's wife.

context: I want you to look at Hebrews 13, if you will
point_summary: Hebrews 13:4 is cited to establish that sexual immorality—specifically adultery and whoredom—falls under divine judgment, grounding Joseph's refusal in the objective moral standard that God himself will judge such sin.

context: In Hebrews chapter 13, so often we'll quote verse five
point_summary: God's certain judgment of sexual immorality—declared in Hebrews 13:4—frames verse 5's context, showing that Joseph's refusal reflects an awareness that adulterers do not escape divine reckoning.

context: marriage is honorable in all and the bed undefiled. But the Bible says that whoremongers and adulterers, God will judge.
point_summary: Hebrews 13:4 is read to establish that both fornication and adultery fall under divine judgment, underscoring the awfulness of sexual sin that Joseph refused to commit.

context: for as much then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood he also himself likewise took part of the same that through death he might destroy him that had the power of death That is the devil
point_summary: Christ's incarnation and death destroyed the devil's power over death, freeing believers from the lifelong bondage of fearing death—a fear overcome by the resurrection promise of 1 Corinthians 15.

context: and deliver them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage
point_summary: Christ's incarnation and death, by destroying the devil's power over death, delivers believers from the lifelong bondage of fearing death—a fear overcome by the resurrection promise of 1 Corinthians 15.

context: we understand in Hebrews chapter number nine that the Bible teaches us, as it appointed unto man once to die, and after this, the judgment
point_summary: Death is universal and certain by divine appointment, but the spiritual second death holds no power over those who have been born again.

context: therefore leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ let us go on unto perfection
point_summary: Hebrews 6:1's call to "go on unto perfection" is cited as the positive counterpart to the warning against drawing back, framing the Christian life as a forward-pressing course to be finished in faith.

context: shall live by faith but if any man draw back my soul shall have no pleasure in him
point_summary: The warning of Hebrews 10:38 — that God has no pleasure in those who draw back — anchors the broader Hebrews call to persevering faith rather than retreating to what was left behind.

context: but we are not of them who draw back unto perdition but of them that believe to the saving of the soul
point_summary: Faith that does not draw back unto perdition but believes to the saving of the soul is the defining mark of those who press forward rather than return.

context: we read through Hebrews chapter number 11 that great hall of faith chapter
point_summary: Hebrews 11's hall of faith serves as the cloud of witnesses whose finished courses ground the charge in chapter 12 to lay aside every weight and run with endurance.

context: he begins chapter number 12 by saying now let us lay aside every weight and the sin which doth so easily beset us and let us run with patience the race that is before us
point_summary: The cloud of faithful witnesses from Hebrews 11 grounds the charge of Hebrews 12:1 to lay aside every weight and run with patience the present race, linking Paul's finished course to the believer's ongoing conduct.

context: But without faith, it is impossible to please Him. For he that cometh to God must believe that He is,
point_summary: Living by faith is an opportunity unique to earthly life, since Hebrews 11:6 declares that faith pleases God, and once mortality gives way to immortality, sight replaces faith entirely.

context: and that He is a rewarder of them that diligently seek Him.
point_summary: Faith pleases God uniquely in this present life, because once mortality gives way to immortality, the opportunity to trust God without sight is forever gone.

context: Hebrews 9.27 is an important verse of scripture. It goes out of the book of Romans, but Hebrews 9.27, as it is appointed unto man, wants to die, but after this, the judgment.
point_summary: Physical death as the universal human appointment establishes the certainty of divine judgment to follow, grounding the penalty of sin in what every person already knows they will face.

context: ...it's 9.27. Make sure you're looking at that verse...
point_summary: Appointed death and subsequent judgment (Heb. 9:27) establishes that sin's penalty is not merely physical death but eternal separation from God, shown directly from Scripture so the listener sees its divine authority.

context: as it's appointed unto man, wants to die after this, the judgment.
point_summary: Hebrews 9:27 is offered as a brief, quotable verse on death and coming judgment that can be shared even when no extended gospel conversation is possible.

context: If you were to read the book of Hebrews in the second chapter, you will find that he came and he made himself, took upon himself the form of a man, the seed of Abraham.
point_summary: Hebrews 2 is cited to establish that Christ took on human flesh—the seed of Abraham—specifically so he could die and thereby redeem mankind.

context: wherefore He is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by Him, seeing He ever liveth to make intercession for them
point_summary: Christ's saving work is total and ongoing — He is able to save to the uttermost because He ever lives to make intercession, grounding present salvation in His perpetual priestly ministry.

context: ...Turn over to Hebrews 5, if you will. Hebrews 5, and really, this is an interesting passage...
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11–12 is cited to show that God's will for the saved is spiritual growth, as Paul rebukes believers who, despite time enough to be teachers, remain dull of hearing.

context: ...Well, notice with me in Hebrews 5 in verse number 11. He says, of whom we have many things to say...
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity is rebuked in Hebrews 5:11 because believers who have been saved long enough to teach others remain "dull of hearing," failing to grow as God wills.

context: that ye have need of milk and not of strong meat
point_summary: Hebrews 5:12 is used to illustrate that believers who have been saved long enough to be teachers still need basic instruction, signaling arrested spiritual growth rather than maturity.

context: for everyone that useth milk is unskillful in the word of righteousness, for he is a babe
point_summary: Spiritual maturity is not determined by how long one has been saved, but by diligent use of God's Word, which trains the senses to discern good and evil.

context: But strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil
point_summary: Spiritual maturity is not determined by length of time as a believer but by the exercise of one's senses to discern good and evil through consistent engagement with God's Word.

context: I'm a stranger. Hebrews chapter 3, 11
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the fear of death that the book of Hebrews speaks of

context: the hall of faith chapter, they believed God.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I thank you that you're able to save to the uttermost.

reference_text: without faith, it's impossible to please him

point_summary: Faith comes by hearing God's Word, and God used that Word—through Gideon's faith builders—to increase Gideon's faith so he could fulfill his assigned task, since without faith it is impossible to please God.

context: In fact, the same Greek word is used in Hebrews 9 and translated as mercy seat.

context: Notice in Hebrews 7 and verse number 25, a precious truth of God's Word is contained within this single verse of Scripture, and I'm thankful that he's our intercessor

context: wherefore, he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him. I'm thankful for that salvation, but he says, seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them.

context: he ever liveth to make intercession for us
point_summary: Christ's present intercession ("he ever liveth to make intercession for us") and his active preparation of a heavenly place together confirm that the believer's cup overflows with blessings extending beyond this world.

context: And the reality is that we look at the book of Hebrews in the 11th chapter, he talks about we're pilgrims and we're strangers.

context: as Paul is writing to the believers in the book of Hebrews, he tells us for in the time that you ought to be teachers. Why does he use that phraseology in Hebrews five in verse number 12?
point_summary: Hebrews 5:12 is cited as evidence that spiritual growth is expected of believers, since those who had sufficient time to become teachers still required elementary instruction.

context: The Bible says that without faith it is Impossible to please God in Hebrews 11 in verse number 6 for he that cometh to God must believe that he is and that he is a Rewarder of them that diligently seek him
point_summary: Faith is the indispensable foundation of pleasing God, requiring both belief in His existence and trust that He rewards those who diligently seek Him.

context: individuals that are mentioned in Hebrews chapter 11 Believed what God said
point_summary: Hebrews 11 is cited to demonstrate that faith is active obedience—each figure listed believed God's word and stepped out accordingly, illustrating that genuine faith takes God at his word.

context: Looking Unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Looking unto Jesus as "the author and finisher of our faith" (Heb. 12:2) served as the congregation's guiding theme through 2020, providing an anchor during the disruption of the coronavirus pandemic.

context: Even last year as we studied through the book of Hebrews and we mentioned in Hebrews 13, those 10 things that we ought to be thankful for
point_summary: Hebrews 13's list of reasons for thanksgiving includes faithful spiritual leaders who proclaim God's Word without compromise, making such ministers a gift to be deeply valued.

context: "...looking unto Jesus, the Author and Finisher of our faith."
point_summary: Hebrews 12's call to look unto Jesus is cited to contrast the right posture—gazing at the Savior rather than at circumstances—with the common tendency to do the opposite in times of trial.

context: we have now this new and living way
point_summary: The "new and living way" revealed in Hebrews 10 is cited as the fulfillment of what Old Testament prophets could only anticipate, contrasting the old covenant's letter with the now-arrived grace of the gospel.

context: But strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil.
point_summary: Spiritual maturity is measured by progression from the milk of the Word to its strong meat, with seasoned believers developing discernment through sustained engagement with Scripture.

context: God said, without faith, it is impossible to please him. For he that cometh to God must believe that he is, and that he's a rewarder of them that diligently seek him, in Hebrews 11, in verse number six
point_summary: Faith in God's word—including its most contested passages—is framed as a non-negotiable spiritual diet, grounded in Hebrews 11:6's declaration that approaching God requires believing He is and that He rewards those who seek Him.

reference_text: tempted in all points like as we are, yet without sin
context: I'm thankful that our God was tempted in all points like as we are such as we are yet without sin
point_summary: Christ's willingness to be tempted in all points as humans are, yet without sin, is presented as the theological ground for confidence that God understands and limits the temptations his people face.

point_summary: Hebrews 11:17's phrase "when he was tried" is cited as the proper interpretive lens for Genesis 22:1's word "tempt," confirming the event was a divine test, not a solicitation to sin.

context: By faith Abraham, when he was tried, offered up Isaac and he that had received the promises offered up his only begotten son
point_summary: Abraham's confident declaration that both he and Isaac would return (Gen. 22:5) is explained by Hebrews 11:19 as faith that God was able to raise Isaac from the dead.

context: Accounting that God, what does the next word say? Was able.
point_summary: Abraham's declaration that "we will come again" reveals a faith grounded in the conviction, confirmed in Hebrews 11:19, that God was able to raise Isaac from the dead.

context: according to what Hebrews 11 tells us, that he accounted that God was able to raise him from the dead.
point_summary: Abraham's faith that God could raise Isaac from the dead was extraordinary precisely because he had no biblical precedent or prior witness of resurrection to draw upon.

context: He went to that cross willingly for the joy that was set before Him.
point_summary: Christ's willing endurance of the cross—motivated by "the joy set before Him"—is presented as the fulfillment of what Isaac's near-sacrifice only foreshadowed: a complete, voluntary payment for sin.

context: dealing with Hebrews chapter number seven and looking at Melchizedek
point_summary: God's divine orchestration of Melchizedek's prior meeting with Abram—before the king of Sodom arrived—is illuminated by Hebrews 7, which provides interpretive insight into Melchizedek's identity and significance.

context: turn over to the book of Hebrews, if you will, I just want to make a few statements from Hebrews chapter number seven
point_summary: Hebrews 7 is cited to establish Melchizedek as an Old Testament type of Christ whose eternal, non-Levitical priesthood demonstrates the superiority of Christ's high-priestly office.

context: Notice in verse number one of Hebrews seven, the Bible says, for this Melchizedek
point_summary: Melchizedek's titles — king of righteousness and king of peace — and his lack of recorded genealogy make him an Old Testament type pointing to Christ's superior, eternal priesthood.

context: First being by interpretation, notice, king of righteousness, and after that also king of Salem, which is king of peace, without father, without mother, without descent, having neither beginning of days nor end of life, but made like unto the Son of God, abideth a priest continually.
point_summary: Melchizedek's titles—king of righteousness and king of Salem (peace)—together with his unrecorded origin and perpetual priesthood mark him as an Old Testament type of Christ's superior, eternal priesthood.

context: We find that in verse 4 of Hebrews 7, it says, now consider how great this man was unto whom even the patriarch Abraham gave the tenth of his spoils.
point_summary: Melchizedek's greatness is demonstrated by the fact that even Abraham—the revered patriarch—paid him tithes, establishing Melchizedek's superiority over the Levitical priesthood that descended from Abraham.

context: we systematically studied through the book of Hebrews
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He said, I'll never leave thee nor forsake thee.
point_summary: God's promise never to leave nor forsake His people (Hebrews 13:5) is the theological ground for fearlessness, yet even believers may experience fear that should drive them to seek God.

context: we're not gonna fear what man can do unto us.
point_summary: Fear, even among God's people, is a legitimate human response to crisis, but it is meant to drive believers toward seeking God rather than paralyzing them.

context: as it appointed unto man, once to die, and after this the judgment
point_summary: Hebrews 9:27's declaration that death is followed by judgment grounds the sermon's urgent call to evangelism: every person we encounter will one day stand before God.

context: The Bible tells us that we're to come boldly into the throne of grace.
point_summary: Bold access into God's presence is possible not by personal merit but solely through Christ, the great high priest who made a way and ever lives to intercede.

context: He ever liveth to make intercession for us.
point_summary: Christ's role as the ever-living intercessor and great high priest is what grants believers access to God's presence—not personal merit.

context: how this man Esau is described in Hebrews chapter number 12 in verse number 16
point_summary: Hebrews 12:16 is cited to authenticate the Genesis account and to supply God's own characterization of Esau as a "profane person" who traded his birthright for a single meal.

context: for ye know how that afterward, when he would have inherited the blessing, he was rejected, for he found no place of repentance, though he sought it carefully with tears.
point_summary: Hebrews 12:17 clarifies that Esau's tears were not repentance but grief over losing the blessing, illustrating how fleshly people mourn consequences rather than sin itself.

reference_text: He is the same yesterday, and today, and forever

point_summary: God's immutability — that He is the same yesterday, today, and forever — is cited as the foundational reason believers will never lack cause for thanksgiving or praise.

context: He ever liveth to make intercession for us.

reference_text: whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth

point_summary: God's chastening of believers is framed as an expression of his love that warrants gratitude, not reluctance.

context: As it is appointed unto man once to die, but after this, the judgment

context: for verily he took not on him the nature of angels, but he took on him the seed of Abraham

context: the certainty of heaven, and as was pointed out, man wants to die,

context: He told us that we're to come boldly unto the throne of grace.

context: This man, according to Hebrews chapter number 10, made one sacrifice for sin forever.

context: The book of Hebrews speaks about, in chapter number 12, I think I might have said Hebrews 13 earlier, but in Hebrews chapter number 12, when the Bible speaks about this event, it speaks about Esau seeking it,
point_summary: Hebrews 12:17 clarifies that Esau's tearful seeking was not repentance but a pursuit of the blessing itself — a distinction the sermon draws directly from the text.

context: Without faith, it is impossible to please him. For he that cometh to God must believe that he is and a rewarder of them that diligently seek him.
point_summary: This present life is the only opportunity believers have to live by faith, since faith gives way to sight in heaven, making now the singular window for both trusting God and witnessing to others.

context: in fact in hebrews chapter number 10 the word of god teaches us this truth with regard to going forward to making progress
point_summary: Hebrews 10:37–39 is invoked to reinforce the command to go forward, showing that God's people must not retreat but press on in anticipation of Christ's imminent coming.

context: for yet a little while and he that shall come will come and will not tarry
point_summary: Hebrews 10:37–39 is read as a divine call to press forward without retreat, contrasting the condemned posture of drawing back with the faithful posture of going forward by faith.

context: now the just shall live by faith
point_summary: Living by faith means pressing forward without drawing back, since God takes no pleasure in retreat and His people are those who believe unto the saving of the soul.

context: If any man draw back my soul shall have no pleasure in him
point_summary: Living by faith means pressing forward in obedience rather than drawing back, since God takes no pleasure in those who retreat from the life of faith.

context: But we are not of them who draw back unto perdition but to them that believe to the saving of the soul
point_summary: Hebrews 10:39 anchors the sermon's call to "go forward" by establishing that the people of God are not those who draw back unto perdition but those who believe unto the saving of the soul.

context: But without faith it is impossible to please him For he that cometh to god must believe that he is and that he is a rewarder to them that diligently Seek him
point_summary: Faith without forward movement is impossible pleasing to God, as Hebrews 11:6 is cited to establish that going forward is the only posture consistent with genuine, God-pleasing faith.

context: we believe that the Bible tells us in Hebrews chapter 7 in verse number 25 that he is able to save them to the uttermost
point_summary: Christ's ability to save "to the uttermost" (Heb. 7:25) grounds the call to go forward with the unchanged gospel, trusting God's power rather than devising new methodologies.

context: let us come boldly unto the throne of grace
point_summary: Hebrews 4:16 is cited as one of several memorized "prayer promises" illustrating the believer's privilege of bold access to God—a privilege the sermon argues Christians know yet fail to practice as their first response.

context: as we read the book of Hebrews in chapter number nine
point_summary: Hebrews 9 is cited to show that Christ's role as the one mediator of 1 Timothy 2:5 was made possible through His substitutionary atonement, entering the holy place by His own blood.

context: we'll also mention a verse over in the 12th chapter

context: but Christ being come and high priest of good things to come by a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands, that is to say, not of this building
point_summary: Christ's role as mediator of the New Covenant is grounded in His own blood shed once for all, securing eternal redemption where animal sacrifices under the old covenant could not.

context: neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by His own blood, He entered in once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption for us
point_summary: Christ's mediatorial role was made possible through His substitutionary atonement, as Hebrews 9:12 shows He entered the holy place once by His own blood, obtaining eternal redemption.

context: for if the blood of bulls and of goats and the ashes of an heifer, sprinkling the unclean sanctified through the purifying of the flesh
point_summary: If animal blood could purify the flesh under the old covenant, the blood of Christ—offered without spot through the eternal Spirit—accomplishes the far greater work of purging the conscience to serve the living God.

context: how much more shall the blood of Christ who through the eternal spirit offered himself without spot to God, purge your conscience from dead works to serve the living God
point_summary: Christ's mediatorial role is grounded in His substitutionary atonement — His blood, offered without spot through the eternal Spirit, purges the conscience and secures eternal redemption as the New Testament's mediator.

context: And for this cause, He is the mediator of the New Testament that by means of death, for the redemption of the transgressions
point_summary: Christ's role as mediator of the New Covenant was made possible specifically through His substitutionary death, by which those called receive the promise of eternal inheritance.

context: that were under the first Testament, they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance for where a Testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator.
point_summary: Christ's qualification as mediator of the New Testament rests on His own blood securing eternal redemption, surpassing the animal sacrifices of the old covenant that could only purify the flesh.

context: We find that in chapter number 12 in verse number 24, the Bible says this with regard to the subject matter of the Lord Jesus Christ being the mediator. The Bible says unto Jesus, the mediator of the new covenant and to the blood of sprinkling that speaketh better things than that of Abel.
point_summary: Christ's blood as mediator of the new covenant—cited from Hebrews 12:24—grounds the assurance that believers may approach God at any time through Jesus, the one mediator between God and man.

context: for the joy that was set before him, according to Hebrews chapter number 12.

context: ...he ever liveth to make intercession for us...
point_summary: Christ's ascension to the Father's right hand is not an absence but an active, ongoing ministry—he ever lives to make intercession for his people until the moment he returns.

context: Hebrews chapter number 7 and verse number 25, it's a precious verse of Scripture, and we find that there, we think about the truth of our Lord, and it says this, wherefore, he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him, seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them.
point_summary: Christ's ability to save "to the uttermost" means no sinner is too far gone, because he ever lives to intercede for those who come to God through him.

context: The Bible says, and as it is appointed unto man, once to die, after this the judgment.
point_summary: Every person faces a certain appointment with death followed by judgment, making the present moment urgent for trusting Christ as Savior.

context: ...as it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment...
point_summary: Death is appointed once for all men, and Christ's second appearing—unlike His first—will not address sin again, because He already bore it completely at Calvary.

context: ...so Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many, and unto them that look for Him shall He appear the second time without sin unto salvation.
point_summary: Christ's second coming will require no further atoning work, because sin was fully dealt with at His first coming, making His return one of salvation for those who look for Him.

context: In chapter 10 of the book of Hebrews, in verse 37, notice what the Bible says. For yet a little while, and He that shall come will come, and will not tarry.
point_summary: Christ's second coming will occur precisely on God's schedule, just as His first coming fulfilled the appointed fullness of time—He will come and will not tarry.

context: therefore we ought to give the more earnest heed to the things which we have heard, lest at any time we should let them slip.
point_summary: Neglecting what has been heard is the danger Hebrews 2:1 warns against, calling believers to give earnest, deliberate attention to God's word lest it slip away.

context: God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets
point_summary: God's progressive revelation—from prophets in past ages to His Son in these last days—establishes the superior weight of the message that demands earnest heed in Hebrews 2:1.

context: hath in these last days spoken unto us by His Son, whom He hath appointed heir of all things, by whom also He made the worlds
point_summary: God's climactic speech through His Son in these last days—surpassing all prior prophetic revelation—is the theological ground for the urgent call to heed in Hebrews 2:1.

context: who being the brightness of His glory, and the express image of His person, and upholding all things by the word of His power, when He had by Himself purged our sins, sat down on the right hand of the majesty on high
point_summary: Christ's identity as the radiant expression of God's glory, the sustainer of creation, and the purifier of sins—established in Hebrews 1:1–3—grounds the "therefore" of Hebrews 2:1 and explains why neglecting His word is so grave.

context: therefore we ought to give the more earnest heed to the things which we have heard, lest at any time we should let them slip
point_summary: Drifting from God's word is not merely a memory failure but a failure of submission—a willful refusal to hearken to divine authority.

context: According to Hebrews 2, He's delivered us from that fear.
point_summary: Hebrews 2 is cited as the basis for why salvation removes the fear of death, since the believer knows that to be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord.

context: the Bible would tell us in Hebrews chapter number 10 that the Old Testament and the law and all of those offerings and sacrifices that they were a shadow of good things to come
point_summary: Hebrews 10's description of Old Testament law and sacrifices as "a shadow of good things to come" grounds the typological link between the Passover blood and the saving blood of Christ.

point_summary: Sin in the life of a believer results in chastisement, which Scripture presents as evidence of God's love and a distinguishing mark of genuine sonship.

context: Now the Bible tells us whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth. In Hebrews chapter number 12, verses 5 through 7, you can read of chastisement there.
point_summary: Divine chastisement of sinning believers is framed as an act of love and a distinguishing mark of genuine sonship, with its absence a reason to question one's salvation.

context: Now the Bible tells us whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth. In Hebrews chapter number 12, verses 5 through 7, you can read of chastisement there.
point_summary: Chastisement is a birthmark of genuine salvation, confirming that God deals with believers as beloved children whom He disciplines because He loves them.

context: Hebrews chapter number 12 deals with that very specifically in verses 5 through 7.
point_summary: Chastisement is a mark of genuine sonship, given by a loving God to His children precisely because sin in the believer's life hinders fellowship with Him.

context: Hebrews chapter number 12 deals with that very specifically in verses 5 through 7.
point_summary: Chastisement is a mark of genuine sonship, evidence that God loves His children enough to discipline them when they sin.

context: Not according to the covenant that I made with their fathers in the day when I took them by the hand to lead them out of the land of Egypt; because they continued not in my covenant, and I regarded them not, saith the Lord.

context: Notice back in chapter number seven, this was given to us in chapter seven and verse number 22 about this better testament.

context: Keep your place here in Hebrews chapter number 8, but turn to the book of Jeremiah.

context: And so it's important that we understand the prophecy in Jeremiah, the truth in Romans chapter 11, as we take and unfold that which is given to us here in Hebrews chapter number 8.

context: for this is the covenant that I will make with the house of Israel after those days, saith the Lord. Again, this is a quote from what we see in Jeremiah 31. Notice it says, I will put my laws into their mind and write them in their hearts.

context: they shall not teach every man his neighbor and every man his brother, saying, Know the Lord, for all shall know me from the least to the greatest.

context: for I will be merciful to their unrighteousness, and their sins and their iniquities will I remember no more.

context: for the law having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things, can never, with those sacrifices which they offered year by year continually, make the comers thereunto perfect.

context: But Christ being come and a high priest of good things to come, by a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands, that is to say, not of this building.

context: Then verily the first covenant had also ordinances of divine service, and a worldly sanctuary.

context: And every priest standeth daily ministering and offering oftentimes the same sacrifices which could never take away sins
point_summary: Hebrews 10:11 is cited to establish that the repeated Old Testament sacrifices could never permanently remove sin, contrasting the insufficiency of the old covenant with Christ's once-for-all atonement.

context: they died without mercy under two or three witnesses
point_summary: Hebrews 10:28's record of merciless death under the Mosaic law serves as the lesser-to-greater baseline for the far more severe judgment awaiting those who reject Christ's atoning blood.

context: how much sore punishment, suppose ye, shall he be thought worthy who hath trodden underfoot the Son of God
point_summary: Rejecting Christ's atoning blood after full exposure to the gospel—treating it as common—incurs a far greater judgment than death under the Mosaic law.

context: ...And took joyfully the spoiling of your goods...Knowing in yourselves that ye have in heaven a better and an enduring substance.
point_summary: Joyful acceptance of the spoiling of earthly goods was possible because these believers knew they possessed a better, enduring substance in heaven—making heavenly treasure the ground of earthly contentment.

context: we see that here in verse number 35. Cast not away therefore your confidence, which hath great recompense of reward.
point_summary: Confidence in God's promises must not be abandoned under hardship, because it carries a great recompense of reward for those who hold fast without wavering.

context: we looked at verse 23. Let us hold fast the profession of our faith. And remember it says without wavering.
point_summary: Holding fast the profession of faith without wavering is grounded not in optimism but in the revealed faithfulness of the God who promised.

context: Cast not away therefore your confidence, which hath great recompense of reward
point_summary: Confidence in God's promises—anchored here in the certainty of Christ's imminent, untarrying return—is presented as the ground of the believer's enduring encouragement.

context: for ye have need of patience, that after ye have done the will of God, ye might receive the promise
point_summary: Endurance through suffering is sustained by the promise of Christ's imminent return, which Scripture guarantees will not be delayed.

context: for yet a little while, and he that shall come will come and will not tarry
point_summary: Hebrews 10:37's declaration that Christ "will not tarry" refutes any suggestion of delay in His return, grounding the believer's daily encouragement in the certain promise of Christ's coming.

reference_text: now the just shall live by faith. But if any man draw back, notice my soul shall have no pleasure in him.
context: Here, he says, now the just shall live by faith. But if any man draw back, notice my soul shall have no pleasure in him.
point_summary: Hebrews 10:38, drawing on Habakkuk 2:4, calls the justified to press forward in faith without drawing back, since God takes no pleasure in retreat.

context: Without faith, it is impossible to please Him. For he that cometh to God must believe that He is a rewarder of them that diligently seek Him.
point_summary: Living by faith is the only way to please God, as Hebrews 11:6 declares, and that faith-driven life shapes behavior because what one believes will inevitably reveal itself in how one lives.

context: I believe that it's a great introduction to the content that we find in Hebrews chapter 11, right? The great hall of faith.

context: As we wrap up Hebrews chapter number 10 and really wrap up this entire section that's covered from chapter five
point_summary: Hebrews 10 closes the sermon's extended argument (chapters 5–10) that Christ's priesthood, sanctuary, and once-for-all self-offering are superior to the entire Levitical system, setting the stage for the life of faith in chapter 11.

context: If we have the knowledge that I'm seeking a better city. Amen? We see that in Hebrews 11.
point_summary: Hebrews 11 is invoked as the scriptural ground for enduring hardships, reproaches, and afflictions joyfully, because the believer's hope is fixed on a better city yet to come.

context: A little while. He that shall come will come and will not tarry.

context: Hope you're encouraged by the principle that the just shall live by faith.
point_summary: Living by faith means pressing forward through hardship and affliction, not drawing back, because the just shall live by faith and this world is not the believer's home.

context: We are not of them that draw back.
point_summary: Believers are not those who draw back, but those who press forward in faith, enduring hardships and afflictions with joy because this world is not their home.

context: ...our eyes on Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith...

context: ...that he that shall come will come...

context: Wherefore, seeing we also are compassed about, with so great a cloud of witnesses.

context: for the joy that was set before Him He endured the cross, despising the shame.
point_summary: The joy set before Christ motivated his endurance of the cross and its shame, confirming that the finished work was purposeful and certain, not abandoned.

context: Wherefore, seeing also we also are encompassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses, notice, let us, let us lay aside every weight

context: and the sin which doth so easily beset us and let us run with patience the race that is set before us. Four times the word us is used in verse number 1 of Hebrews 12.
point_summary: Though the cloud of witnesses in Hebrews 12:1 is real, the fourfold repetition of "us" shifts the passage's emphasis onto living believers who must still run their own race.

context: You know what he said in Hebrews 12 in verse 1? We've referenced it a couple of times already, but in verse number 2, looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith, we read at the end of verse number 1, let us run with patience the race that is set before us.
point_summary: Keeping eyes on Jesus—not on people who will disappoint—is the means by which a believer runs with patience the race set before him and fulfills his ministry.

context: You know what he said in Hebrews 12 in verse 1? We've referenced it a couple of times already, but in verse number 2, looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith, we read at the end of verse number 1, let us run with patience the race that is set before us.

context: I need to keep my eyes on Jesus, because he is the author and finisher of our faith.
point_summary: Keeping one's eyes on Jesus as "the author and finisher of our faith" is presented as the essential focus required to fulfill one's ministry.

context: your word is quick and powerful and sharper than a two-edged sword
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He's the same yesterday, today, and forever.

context: Hebrews chapter number six this morning, Hebrews chapter number six. And as you're turning there, I'm going to read, I'm going to read a passage of scripture to you.

context: therefore leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ notice, let us go on unto perfection

context: In verse 13, it says, for when God made promise to Abraham, because he could swear by no greater, he swear by himself.

context: The Bible says that because he could swear by no greater, he swear by himself, saying, verse 14, surely blessing I will bless thee, and multiplying I will multiply thee.
point_summary: Because God could swear by no one greater than himself, his oath to Abraham is the highest possible confirmation of his promises, grounded in his own unchangeable nature.

context: The Bible says that because he could swear by no greater, he swear by himself, saying, verse 14, surely blessing I will bless thee, and multiplying I will multiply thee.
point_summary: Because God could swear by no one greater, He swore by Himself, making His promise to Abraham—and by extension to believers—doubly certain through an oath that cannot fail.

context: And so after he had patiently endured, he obtained the promise.

context: For man verily swear by the greater, and an oath for confirmation is to them an end of all strife.

context: Wherein God, willing more abundantly to show unto the heirs of promise the immutability of his counsel, confirmed it by an oath,

context: that by two immutable things in which it was impossible for God to lie. Aren't you thankful for that?

context: It says that we might have a strong consolation, who have fled for refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us, which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and steadfast, and which entereth into that within the veil,

context: And we find here that in chapter number seven and eight, he's going to continue to expound upon those things

context: as we note here in Hebrews chapter number 6, that as he refers back to Genesis chapter 22

context: And so as we come to Hebrews chapter number 6 and we see, for when God made promise to Abraham, and we think about the truth of that which is being given to us here,

context: Wherein God, willing more abundantly to show unto the, notice this, the heirs of promise, the immutability of his counsel, confirmed it by an oath.

context: by two immutable things, in which it was impossible for God to lie

context: which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and steadfast, in which entereth into that within the veil

context: seeing then that we have a great high priest that is passed into the heavens, Jesus, the son of God, let us hold fast our profession.

context: For we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities, but was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin.

context: the anchor of our soul, that it's sure and steadfast, and that He is in heaven.

context: but this man,

context: whither the forerunner is for us entered, even Jesus, made an high priest forever after the order of Melchizedek
point_summary: Jesus as "forerunner" means he has entered heaven in advance as the place where his people are to follow, securing their hope as an eternal high priest after the order of Melchizedek.

context: in which entereth into that within the veil at the end of verse number 19

context: We understand the truth of Hebrews chapter number 2, that we no longer fear death.

context: without faith, it is impossible to please him for he that cometh to God must believe that he is

context: and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him.
point_summary: God himself — not merely his gifts — is the reward promised to those who diligently seek him, as Abram's refusal of Sodom's offer illustrates.

context: In fact, in the book of Hebrews, in the 11th chapter, a passage of scripture that we've often referred to as being the hall of faith chapter, we see how God lists throughout that portion of scripture, by faith this individual did this, and by faith this individual did this.
point_summary: Hebrews 11, the "hall of faith" chapter, is invoked to show that faith means taking God at His word and acting in obedience, trusting He will do what He said.

context: without faith it is impossible to please Him
point_summary: Pleasing God requires active faith—believing both that He exists and that He rewards those who seek Him—making faith not optional but the essential foundation of a life lived to please God.

context: Look over quickly, if you will, at Hebrews chapter 12. And so often we focus on the first couple of verses

context: looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him, what does it say in your Bible? Endured the cross. Endured the cross. Notice, despising the shame and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God.

context: But notice the Bible says that he endured the cross. Why? For the joy that was set before him.

context: In verse number three, it says, for consider him that endured, we see the word again, such contradiction of sinners against himself, lest ye be wearied and faint in your own minds.

context: We see it illustrated here in Hebrews 12 again

context: to consider him that endured such contradiction
point_summary: Christ's endurance of suffering without retaliation is held up as the supreme example enabling believers to stand firm in hardness.

context: and what shall I more say? For the time would fail me to tell of Gideon, of Barak, of Samson, of Jephthah, of David, also in Samuel, and of the prophets, who through faith subdued kingdoms, wrought righteousness, obtained promises, stopped the mouths of lions,

context: quenched the violence of fire, escaped the edge of the sword. Out of weakness were made strong, waxed valiant in fight, turned to flight the armies of the aliens.
