context: Women received their dead, raised to life again, and others, notice, before we get to the others that are mentioned halfway through verse 35, those are great triumphs that we read in verses 32 through 34, and even the first phrase of verse 35, we say, man, look at what was accomplished through faith, look at these great triumphs through faith.

context: and others had trial of cruel mockings and scourgings, yea, moreover, of bonds and imprisonment.

context: They were stoned, they were sawn asunder, were tempted, were slain with the sword.

context: keep our eyes on Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith

context: for the law having a shadow of good things to come and not the very image of the things, all right, can never with these sacrifices which they offered year by year continually make the comers thereunto perfect for then would they not have ceased to be offered.

context: ...By Him, therefore, let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually.

context: looking diligently, lest any man fail of the grace of God, lest any root of bitterness springing up trouble you

context: In Hebrews 10.24, and let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works, not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together as the manner of some is, but exhorting one another and so much the more as you see the day approaching.

context: In Hebrews three in verse 13, the Bible speaks about the deceitfulness of sin.

context: Remember that Hebrews 7 in verse number 14, as it's speaking about the priesthood of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ, that we have a great high priest.

context: for whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth

context: for as much then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, He also Himself likewise took part of the same, that through death He might destroy him that had the power of death.

context: It tells us this in Hebrews 11 and verse 22. By faith Joseph, when he died, made mention of the departing of the children of Israel

context: The Bible tells us in this chapter, in Hebrews chapter 11 and verse number 6, without faith it is impossible to please Him. For he that cometh to God must believe that He is, and that He is a rewarder of them that diligently seek Him.

context: He says that we can come boldly unto the throne of grace.

context: the very truth for us to understand this morning is that the reason, listen, when God corrects us, we talk about chastisement in the life of a believer, you know that that's because he loves us. Whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth, he correcteth.

context: so that we may boldly say, the Lord is my helper and I will not fear what man shall do unto me

context: Hebrews chapter 12 says that for the joy that was set before him, he despised the shame

context: How shall we escape if we neglect so great salvation? Hebrews chapter number 2.

context: The Bible tells us in Hebrews 11, in verse number six, that but without faith it is impossible to please him. For he that cometh to God must believe that he is,

context: I'm wearing a polo shirt, and it has Hebrews chapter 6 on there that Jesus is the anchor of my soul.

context: God also bearing them witness both with signs and wonders and with diverse miracles and gifts of the Holy Ghost according to his own will.

context: for the law, having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things, can never, with those sacrifices which they offered year by year continually, make the comers thereunto perfect

context: And verse number 12 says

context: we find the fulfillment in Hebrews five, the reference to that

context: he said the things that he had to utter, that they were hard to utter, seeing you're dull of hearing

context: chapter number seven of the book of Hebrews, he deals with that of Melchizedek

context: The Bible begins by saying this in verse number one, as I read already, for the law, having a shadow of good things to come.

context: And we find that it says that it was a shadow of good things to come. And then notice this phrase, and not the very image of the things.

context: as we've studied the ninth chapter of the book of Hebrews

context: And we find that the Bible continues in verse number four again, for it is not possible that the blood of bulls and of goats should take away sins.

context: Reading through Hebrews 11 and other portions of Scripture

context: We understand Hebrews 11, six, without faith, it's impossible to please Him.

context: Hebrews tells us this, in Hebrews 2, in verse number 14, speaking about the victory of our Lord Jesus Christ, He says this, for as much then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, He also Himself likewise took part of the same, that through death He might destroy him that had the power of death, that is the devil.

context: the redemption of the transgressions that were under the first testament, they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance.

context: Because without the shedding of blood there's no remission.

point_summary: God works within believers to produce what is well-pleasing in His sight through Christ, grounding the call to use one's spiritual gift in divine enablement rather than self-effort.

context: he'll never leave us nor forsake us
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he'll never leave us nor forsake us
point_summary: God's promise never to leave nor forsake believers (Hebrews 13:5) is distinguished here from the deeper, conditional blessing of sweet fellowship with God, which can be hindered by sin.

context: he tasted death for every man
point_summary: Hebrews 2 grounds the necessity of the incarnation: Christ took on human flesh rather than angelic form because only a man could taste death and pay for humanity's sin.

context: Jesus Christ, the same yesterday, today, and forever
point_summary: God's immutability — that He is the same yesterday, today, and forever — is cited as the biblical ground for trusting Him as the unchanging source of every good and perfect gift.

context: He is able to save to the uttermost
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the law is a shadow of good things to come
point_summary: The transitional generation that lived between the old covenant's sacrificial system and its fulfillment in Christ uniquely experienced the law as a fading shadow, with Hebrews 10:1 grounding that tension historically.

context: for the time that you ought to be teachers
point_summary: Spiritual maturity, not mere spiritual age, is the standard James holds before believers — those long in the faith ought by now to be teachers, not still infants.

context: He's the same yesterday and today and forever
point_summary: Christ's immutability — "the same yesterday and today and forever" — grounds the sermon's insistence that the gospel message Paul delivered to Corinth remains unchanged and settled.

context: For the joy that was set before him, according to Hebrews chapter number 12
point_summary: Christ's endurance of the cross is grounded in the joy set before him, as Hebrews 12 is cited to explain why he did not come down despite having the power to do so.

context: Look, if you will, at Hebrews chapter 13
point_summary: Hebrews 13 is cited to show that God's ongoing sanctifying work in believers—their "perfection" and spiritual maturity—is something to embrace and respond to, not resist.

context: now, the God of peace that brought again from the dead, our Lord Jesus, that great shepherd of the sheep through the blood of the everlasting covenant. Notice what he says, make you perfect in every good work to do his will, working in you that which is well pleasing in his sight through Jesus Christ to whom be glory forever and ever. Amen.
point_summary: Spiritual maturity and perfection are God's own work within the believer, accomplished through the resurrected Christ and grounded in the blood of the everlasting covenant.

context: it says to make you perfect in every good work, to do his will. Notice, working in you
point_summary: God's sanctifying work in believers—making them perfect in every good work to do His will—is directed not toward self-fulfillment but toward His glory, as the doxology of Hebrews 13:21 makes plain.

context: for when the time ye ought to be teachers, ye have need that one teach you again, which be the first principles of the oracles of God and it becomes such as have need of milk and not of strong meat
point_summary: Prolonged spiritual immaturity — needing milk when one should be teaching others — illustrates the failure to grow through God's Word that Paul laments in the Corinthian church.

context: for everyone that useth milk is unskillful in the word of righteousness, for he is a babe
point_summary: Remaining on milk rather than advancing to solid doctrinal teaching marks a believer as spiritually immature and unskillful in the word of righteousness, the opposite of the perfection Paul desires for the Corinthians.

context: But strong meat belongeth to them that are of full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil
point_summary: Spiritual maturity is marked by the capacity to receive solid doctrinal teaching — "strong meat" — which belongs to those whose senses are exercised through use of God's Word to discern good and evil.

context: Hebrews chapter number six this morning Hebrews chapter number six and if you're taking notes will be in verses 1 through 8
point_summary: Hebrews 6:1 opens the positive challenge within the third warning passage: recipients previously confronted with spiritual immaturity are now called to move forward toward spiritual maturity.

context: Hebrews chapter number 5 verses 11 through 14
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11 opens the third warning passage in Hebrews, confronting readers with a spiritual immaturity directly caused by their dullness of hearing.

context: It begins in chapter 5 verse number 11 and it goes through the end of chapter number 6
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11–6:end constitutes the third warning passage in Hebrews, and spiritual immaturity caused by dullness of hearing marks its opening confrontation to the original recipients.

context: as we move into the sixth chapter of the book of Hebrews and continue looking at this Warning passage
point_summary: Hebrews 6:1 is read as a direct challenge to spiritually immature believers—those previously confronted for dullness of hearing—to press forward into spiritual maturity.

context: leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ Let us go on unto perfection not laying again the foundation of repentance from dead works and of faith toward God
point_summary: Hebrews 6:1 calls believers to press beyond foundational doctrines toward spiritual maturity, moving from confrontation with immaturity to active progress in their walk with Christ.

context: of the doctrine of baptisms of laying on of hands and of resurrection of the dead And of eternal judgment
point_summary: Hebrews 6:2's list of foundational doctrines—baptisms, laying on of hands, resurrection, and eternal judgment—represents the already-laid groundwork believers must move beyond in pressing toward spiritual maturity.

context: this will we do if God permit
point_summary: God's permission is the necessary condition for spiritual progress, underscoring that growth toward maturity is not self-generated but dependent on divine enablement.

context: that spiritual discernment that we could discern both good and evil
point_summary: Spiritual maturity, as described in Hebrews 5:14, involves developing the discernment to distinguish between good and evil—the very capacity the Hebrew Christians were being challenged to pursue.

context: verses 11 through 14 And then I'll tell you the pronouns that are used here Notice what it says of whom we have many things to say and hard to be uttered seeing ye Are dull of hearing
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11 introduces the warning passage by identifying believers who are "dull of hearing" and therefore failing to progress toward maturity—a diagnosis confirmed by the second-person pronouns throughout the section.

context: for when the time? For for when for the time ye ought to be teachers ye have need that one teach you again Which be the first principles of the oracles of God
point_summary: Hebrews 5:12 identifies believers who, though they should have become teachers by now, remain spiritually stunted—still needing milk rather than solid food—because they are dull of hearing.

context: and are become such as have need of milk and not of strong me
point_summary: Remaining on spiritual milk rather than solid food marks a believer as unskillful in the word of righteousness — evidence of stunted discipleship, not unbelief.

context: For every one that useth milk is unskillful in the word of righteousness for he is a babe But strong meat belongeth to them that are a full age Even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern Both good and evil
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity is identified as inability to handle the word of righteousness, marking the believer addressed here as a babe still requiring milk rather than solid doctrinal teaching.

context: we come to verse number one of chapter six and it says, therefore
point_summary: The pronoun "us" in Hebrews 6:1 identifies the call to press on toward maturity as addressed to believers, continuing the same audience established by the pronouns in the preceding chapter.

context: leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ, let us go on unto perfection, not laying again the foundation of repentance from dead works and of faith toward God
point_summary: The "let us go on" of Hebrews 6:1 is addressed to believers, calling them to advance beyond foundational doctrines without abandoning those essentials entirely.

context: of the doctrine of baptism, of laying on of hands, and of resurrection of the dead, and of eternal judgment
point_summary: Hebrews 6:1–2 lists foundational doctrines—baptisms, laying on of hands, resurrection, eternal judgment—that believers must be grounded in before advancing to deeper maturity, not abandoned but built upon.

context: as we mature physically our diet no longer exclusively will consist of milk, but we will begin to have other things, and obviously the passage deals with milk and meat
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity is marked by an exclusive diet of milk — elementary doctrine — when believers ought to have progressed to the solid food that marks full-grown, discerning faith.

context: we notice in chapter 6, verses 1 through 3, the pronouns us and we are used
point_summary: Spiritual maturity requires pressing beyond foundational doctrines—not abandoning them, as a maturing diet retains milk while adding meat—but advancing further into Christian growth.

context: we find that in chapter 5, verses 11 through 14, in chapter 6, verses 1 through 3
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity—being "dull of hearing"—is presented as a condition afflicting genuine believers who have failed to progress beyond foundational doctrine toward deeper maturity.

context: believers that are dull of hearing
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11–14 addresses believers specifically, identified by consistent first-person plural pronouns, as those confronted with spiritual immaturity and challenged to progress toward maturity.

context: for it is impossible for those who were once enlightened and have tasted of the heavenly gift and were made partakers of the Holy Ghost and have tasted the good Word of God and the powers of the world to come, if they shall fall away to renew them again unto repentance, seeing they crucify to themselves the Son of God afresh and put Him to an open shame
point_summary: The shift from first-person pronouns in verses 1–3 to third-person pronouns ("those," "they," "them") in verses 4–6 signals that the author is addressing a different group of people than the believers previously in view.

context: but beloved, we are persuaded better things of you and things that accompany salvation, though we thus speak
point_summary: The pronoun shift back to "beloved" and "you" in Hebrews 6:9 signals the author's return to addressing believers, distinguished from the unbelievers referenced in verses 4–6.

context: Hebrews 6 verses 4 through 6
point_summary: Hebrews 6:4–6 is treated here not as teaching the loss of salvation but as a passage misread in isolation, corrected by clearer texts affirming the believer's eternal security.

context: Hebrews 5, verses 1 through 10
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: verse number 9, that it's a powerful verse of scripture that teaches the eternal security of the believer
point_summary: Hebrews 5:9's declaration that Christ became "the author of eternal salvation" is cited as textual evidence for the eternal security of the believer, making any subsequent loss of that salvation internally contradictory within the same letter.

context: in verse number 9, being made perfect, he became the author of eternal salvation unto all them that obey him
point_summary: Hebrews 5:9's declaration that Christ became "the author of eternal salvation" functions as a contextual bookend that guards against reading the difficult passage of Hebrews 6 as teaching the loss of salvation.

context: look in chapter number 6 and verse number 19
point_summary: Hebrews 6:19's image of hope as a sure and steadfast anchor of the soul forms one bookend—alongside Hebrews 5:9—framing the difficult passage and confirming eternal security.

context: which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and steadfast, in which entereth into that within the veil
point_summary: Hebrews 6:19's image of hope as "an anchor of the soul, both sure and steadfast" is presented as a bookend verse framing the difficult passage and confirming the doctrine of eternal security.

context: for it is impossible for those
point_summary: Hebrews 6:4's description of those "once enlightened" is read as characterizing professing but unregenerate individuals, not genuinely saved believers who could forfeit eternal salvation.

context: they have tasted the good word of God in verse number 5. And then the last description that is given at the end of verse number 5, it says in the powers of the world to come
point_summary: Being called a "partaker" of the Holy Ghost is distinguished from the indwelling of the Spirit at salvation, suggesting these individuals participated with the Spirit without being genuinely regenerate.

context: if they shall fall away, to renew them again unto repentance, seeing they crucify to themselves the Son of God afresh, and put him to an open shame
point_summary: Hebrews 6:6's declaration that renewal to repentance is "impossible" rules out any loss-and-recovery-of-salvation reading, since that interpretation would logically forbid ever being saved again.

context: from Hebrews 6 and looking at this example from the first century
point_summary: Judas, outwardly indistinguishable from the eleven even to his fellow disciples, serves as the first-century illustration of the apostate described in Hebrews 6 who was among the covenant community yet ultimately betrayed Christ.

context: Hebrews 6 verses 4 through 6, and we speak about enlightened, and taste of the heavenly gift
point_summary: Enlightenment in Hebrews 6:4 is grounded in John 1:9's declaration that Christ is the true light who illumines every man through creation, conscience, and Scripture.

context: the powers of the world to come
point_summary: Tasting "the powers of the world to come" describes a real but ultimately non-saving exposure to supernatural reality, illustrated by those in Matthew 7 who performed works in Christ's name yet were never truly His.

context: there's an illustration that is given in verses 7 and 8
point_summary: The illustration of earth that drinks in rain yet yields herbs versus thorns pictures those who receive spiritual blessing and respond with fruitful growth, contrasting those whose lives prove they were never truly regenerate.

context: For the earth, which drinketh in the rain, that cometh oft upon it, and bringeth forth herbs meat for them by whom it is dressed, receiveth blessing from God
point_summary: Hebrews 6:7–8 functions as an illustration of verses 4–6, showing that the same gospel exposure can produce genuine fruit in true believers or thorns in those who were never truly saved.

context: But that which beareth thorns and briars is rejected, and is nigh unto cursing, whose end is to be burned
point_summary: Land that receives rain yet yields only thorns and briars illustrates those exposed to gospel truth who never truly believed, whose end is rejection and burning.

context: the warning that is being given here in Hebrews 5.11 through 6.20
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11–6:20 is read as a unified warning against spiritual immaturity, not as a proof text for losing salvation, which the sermon frames as a manipulative misreading.

context: He says in verse number nine, the matter of which we'll study in our next lesson, but beloved, we are persuaded better things of you, things that accompany salvation
point_summary: Verse 9 introduces a turn toward assurance—"better things…that accompany salvation"—previewed here as the basis for distinguishing genuine saving faith from works-based security, to be expounded in the next lesson.

context: I will never leave thee nor forsake thee
point_summary: God's promised presence — "I will never leave thee nor forsake thee" — grounds the call to intentionally seek fellowship with him rather than rushing past him in daily busyness.

context: not forsaking the assembling of the manner of some is, but exhorting one another. It says this, and so much the more as you see the day approaching
point_summary: Forsaking regular church assembly—cited from Hebrews 10:25—is identified as a symptom of being too busy for Jesus, with more gathered worship urged as the remedy for a spiritually weakened church.

context: and let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works, not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together as the manner of some is, but exhorting one another in so much the more as you see the day approaching
point_summary: Hebrews 10:24–25 is cited to show that mutual encouragement is a universal Christian duty, distinct from—and not negated by—the specific spiritual gift of exhortation.

context: In Hebrews 10 in verse 25
point_summary: Assembling together is a non-negotiable command, not a preference, because certain acts of mutual exhortation and edification are simply impossible to fulfill apart from physical gathering.

context: let us consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works
point_summary: Assembling together is presented as a prerequisite for obeying commands like Hebrews 10:24's call to "consider one another to provoke unto love and to good works," which cannot be fulfilled apart from gathered worship.

context: look over at the book of Hebrews
point_summary: The earthly tabernacle was built after the pattern of the heavenly one, establishing that the altar John sees in Revelation 6 corresponds to a real altar in the heavenly sanctuary.

context: The day of atonement is in clear view in chapter number nine
point_summary: The earthly tabernacle was constructed after the pattern of the heavenly one, establishing that the altar John sees beneath in Revelation corresponds to a real heavenly sanctuary, not a symbol.

context: you begin to see in chapter 10, the law having a shadow of good things to come
point_summary: Hebrews 10's description of the law as "a shadow of good things to come" establishes that the earthly tabernacle was patterned after the true heavenly sanctuary, grounding the altar imagery of Revelation 6 in that heavenly reality.

context: now of these things, now of the things which we have spoken, this is the sum
point_summary: The heavenly tabernacle, of which the earthly was a copy, is the true sanctuary where Christ now ministers as High Priest — establishing that the altar John sees beneath is a real, heavenly structure.

context: We have such an high priest who is set on the right hand of the throne of the majesty in the heavens, a minister of the sanctuary, and of those that says the true tabernacle, which the Lord pitched and not man
point_summary: The heavenly tabernacle is the true original, of which Moses's earthly structure was only a copy, establishing that the altar John sees "under" in Revelation 6 corresponds to a real heavenly sanctuary.

context: for every high priest is ordained to offer gifts and sacrifices
point_summary: The earthly tabernacle, including its altar, was built as a shadow and pattern of the true heavenly sanctuary, establishing that the altar John sees in Revelation 6 has a real, heavenly counterpart.

context: Wherefore, it is of necessity that this man have somewhat also to offer, for if he were on earth, he should not be a priest, seeing that there are priests
point_summary: The earthly tabernacle was built as a shadow and pattern of the true heavenly tabernacle, establishing that a real altar exists in heaven beneath which the martyred souls of Revelation 6 are located.

context: He that cometh to God must believe that he is and that he's
point_summary: Specific, faith-filled prayer demonstrates genuine belief that God both exists and actively rewards those who diligently seek Him, distinguishing real faith from generic, unanswerable requests.

context: a rewarder of them that diligently seek him. It says before, I quoted only the latter part, without faith it is impossible to please him
point_summary: Specific, faith-filled prayer pleases God because, as Hebrews 11:6 states, he rewards those who diligently seek him—and vague prayers cannot demonstrate or test that believing dependence.

context: come boldly before the throne of grace
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the deceitfulness of sin
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: chastisement is a sign that you're a believer whom the Lord loveth, He chasteneth
point_summary: Chastisement is presented as evidence of God's love for believers, a merciful escalation after unheeded conviction, before discipline reaches its severest form.

context: appointed unto man wants to die after this the judgment
point_summary: The urgency of gospel ministry rests on the certainty that every person will die and face judgment, making reconciliation work the most consequential labor on earth.

context: Without shedding of blood, there is no remission
point_summary: The scarlet thread Rahab bound in her window is read as a type of Christ's atoning blood, grounding the typology in Hebrews 9:22's declaration that without the shedding of blood there is no remission.

context: we've looked at Hebrews 11 and verse 6, you don't need to turn there, but the Bible says, but without faith it is impossible to please him. For he that cometh to God must believe that he is and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him
point_summary: Hebrews 11:6 grounds the sermon's third point: God rewards those who diligently seek Him, so that faith expressed in obedience—including giving—invites God's blessing.

context: He's the same yesterday and today and forever.
point_summary: God's immutability — that He is the same yesterday, today, and forever — grounds the sermon's argument that He cannot be redefined, only accepted or rejected on His own revealed terms.

context: in Hebrews chapter number 10, in verse number 37, the Lord, we have this truth, this promise, for yet a little while, and he that shall come will come and will not tarry
point_summary: Christ's return, promised in Hebrews 10:37 as certain and imminent, marks the definitive finish line of the Christian race—the moment when, at the trumpet's sound, the running ends.

context: we can look at Hebrews chapter number 12, and we understand that we're to run this race
point_summary: Hebrews 12's call to lay aside every weight and run with patience, fixing eyes on Jesus, frames the Christian life as a race demanding endurance until its appointed finish.

context: as we consider the truth of Hebrews chapter number 12, when the Bible tells us, Wherefore, seeing we also are compassed about with so
point_summary: Hebrews 12:1–2 frames the Christian life as a race requiring endurance, with every weight and besetting sin laid aside, and with the eyes fixed on Jesus as both its author and finisher.

context: a cloud of witnesses, let us lay aside every weight, and the sin which doth so easily beset us
point_summary: Hebrews 12:1's image of running with patience, laying aside every weight, and looking to Jesus frames the sermon's call to live each day as though it were the final leg of the race.

context: let us run with patience the race that is set before us
point_summary: Hebrews 12:1's image of running with patience, laying aside every weight, and fixing eyes on Jesus frames the sermon's call to live each day as though it were the final leg of the race.

context: looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Hebrews 12:2 grounds the race metaphor by identifying Jesus as both the author and finisher of faith, the one toward whom believers run with patience until the race ends.

context: He will not tarry
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: it is appointed unto man once to die. But after this, the judgment
point_summary: Death's appointed certainty—"it is appointed unto man once to die, but after this the judgment"—grounds the sermon's urgent call to live each day as though it could be the last, finishing well for Christ.

context: if you look at Hebrews chapter 11, we refer to it as the great hall of faith chapter
point_summary: Hebrews 11, the "hall of faith" chapter, is invoked to show that genuine belief always produces action — faith and behavior are inseparable.

context: the one who is the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Christ's own endurance of the cross—enduring its shame for the joy set before him—is presented as the supreme example of the steadfast patience James calls believers to exercise through trials.

context: who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God
point_summary: Christ's endurance of the cross "for the joy that was set before him" is cited as the supreme example of the patience James calls believers to imitate through their own trials.

context: the law, as it says in the book of Hebrews, is a shadow of good things to come
point_summary: The law's sacrifices, repeated daily and annually, served only as a shadow and reminder—never able to remove sin—in contrast to Christ's single, sufficient sacrifice that ended the system entirely.

context: those sacrifices that they offered daily, every day coming back, repeating, annually going in and all that they did, it could never take away sin
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He lives to make intercession for us
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The book of Hebrews talks about the deceitfulness of sin in the second chapter
point_summary: Hebrews 2 is cited for its warning about sin's deceitfulness—its power to minimize wrongdoing and prevent the sinner from seeing it as the grave offense against God it truly is.

context: remember I said Hebrews 2 talks
point_summary: Hebrews 2:1 is invoked to underscore sin's deceitive power—specifically the lie that personal sin affects only the individual, when in reality it spreads and harms the whole.

context: As an appointed unto man wants to die, but after this, the judgment
point_summary: The universal appointment of death and subsequent judgment grounds the necessity of Christ's atoning blood as the only sufficient payment for sin.

context: Wherefore, speaking of the Lord, he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him, seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them.
point_summary: Christ's ability to save "to the uttermost" is grounded in his perpetual intercession, offering comfort that no sinner lies beyond the reach of his saving work.

context: He said, I'll never leave thee nor forsake thee.
point_summary: God's promise never to leave nor forsake His people (Hebrews 13:5) is the sustaining assurance that enables believers to maintain resolve and complete their God-given tasks amid ongoing difficulty.

context: Look at Hebrews chapter 12, if you will, just very quickly
point_summary: Hebrews 12 is invoked as a call to resolve and finish well, set against the enemy's strategy of distraction, with God's promised presence sustaining believers through repeated opposition.

context: wherefore, seeing we also are compassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses, let us lay aside every weight and the sin which doth so easily beset us and let us run with patience the race that is set before us, looking unto Jesus, the author and, what, finisher of our faith, who for the joy that was set before Him endured the cross, despising the shame and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God
point_summary: Hebrews 12:1 is invoked to press the call to finish the race—laying aside every weight and fixing eyes on Jesus, the finisher of faith, as the model and power for enduring opposition to the end.

context: For consider Him that endured such contradiction of sinners against Himself, lest ye be wearied and faint in your minds
point_summary: Considering Christ's endurance of sinners' contradiction guards against weariness and lost resolve, sustaining the believer's commitment to finish the race set before them.

context: the Bible says that it's appointed unto man once to die, and after this the judgment
point_summary: Death is the universal, unavoidable appointment every person faces, making the eternal consequence of one's spiritual condition before God an urgent present reality.

context: there's no forgiveness without the remission or there's no without the shedding of blood there's no remission
point_summary: Without the shedding of blood there is no remission — sinners are brought near to God solely through the blood of Christ shed at the cross.

context: he's never gonna leave me nor forsake me
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Without faith it is impossible to please him, for he that cometh to God must believe that he is, and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him
point_summary: Hebrews 11:6 is cited to establish that Stephen's defining characteristic of being "full of faith" is the indispensable foundation for pleasing God and the source of his evident power.

context: the Bible is quick, it's alive, it's powerful, it's sharper than a two-edged sword
point_summary: God's Word carries inherent, living power—able to pierce hearts and produce conviction even when hearers refuse to receive it through any other means.

context: He's the same yesterday, today, and forever
point_summary: God's unchanging character — "the same yesterday, today, and forever" — grounds the assurance that the Lord who heard the poor and needy in Isaiah's day still hears and acts today.

context: I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee
point_summary: God's faithfulness to never leave or forsake His people is illustrated through an elderly woman whose failing memory retained only one verse: "I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee."

context: not forsaking the assembling of ourselves as the manner of some is, but exhorting one another and so much the more as you see the day approaching
point_summary: As the signs of Christ's return multiply, the command not to forsake assembling together intensifies — making faithful church attendance all the more urgent, not optional.

context: Hebrews talks about how he's greater than everything, but he takes some time to talk about he's greater than the angels
point_summary: Christ's superiority over angels, affirmed in Hebrews, provides the doctrinal backdrop for distinguishing the worship due Him alone from the created, servant role of the angelic beings described in Revelation 4.

context: Here's what Hebrews 13 says
point_summary: Hebrews 13:15's call to offer the sacrifice of praise *continually* is held up as the standard motivating believers to verbal worship now, before death ends every earthly opportunity.

context: by him, therefore, let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually
point_summary: Hebrews 13:15's command to offer "the sacrifice of praise continually" — the fruit of lips giving thanks — challenges believers to examine whether their speech consistently and accurately reflects God's character.

context: we're talking about Hebrews chapter number 2 or 12. Look over there if you will in Hebrews chapter number 12 this evening
point_summary: Hebrews 12 is applied here as a call to eliminate spiritual weights and sins that hinder the believer's race, using the athletic analogy to frame revival readiness.

context: wherefore, seeing verse number 1, we also are compassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses, let us lay aside every weight. And the sin which doth so easily beset us, let us run with it. And with patience the race that is set before us.
point_summary: Hebrews 12:1 is read to distinguish between weights and sins, stressing that weights—not always sinful—distract believers from running the race of revival readiness.

context: If we want to run the race with patience, we've got to lay aside every weight
point_summary: Hebrews 12:1's call to "lay aside every weight" is applied here to mean eliminating all distractions and encumbrances—not merely most—as a prerequisite for running the Christian race with patience.

context: Lay aside every weight and the sin which doth so easily beset us
point_summary: Eliminating spiritual weights — laying aside every weight and besetting sin — is presented as a necessary step of preparation for personal revival.

reference_text: without faith, it's impossible to please him
context: without faith, it's impossible to please him
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: how shall we escape if we neglect so great salvation
point_summary: Neglecting so great a salvation is presented as inexcusable precisely because Christ gave everything—leaving all—so that sinners could be saved.

context: a root of bitterness
point_summary: A root of bitterness, left unaddressed for decades, embeds itself into a person's very being and colors every perspective they hold.

context: Hebrews chapter 13 is where we're going to start
point_summary: Hebrews 13:15 grounds the sermon's central question—what God receives from us—by commanding a continual, Christ-mediated sacrifice of praise offered back to God.

context: By Him, therefore, let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually. That is the fruit of our lips giving thanks to His name.
point_summary: Praise offered to God through the fruit of our lips must be *continual*—not contingent on favorable circumstances—because He is worthy to receive it regardless of the troubles we face.

context: By Him, therefore, let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually
point_summary: Hebrews 13:15 calls believers to offer the sacrifice of praise *continually*—not only in favorable circumstances but in adversity, because God's goodness never ceases.

context: as Hebrews 13 tells us, to offer praise to Your name
point_summary: Hebrews 13 is cited as a standing command to continually offer praise to God's name, grounding the sermon's call for ongoing, deliberate worship.

context: the Bible tells us that he'll never leave us nor forsake us
point_summary: God's promise never to leave nor forsake His people (Hebrews 13:5) is cited as the assurance underlying Jehoshaphat's walk with God, grounding divine presence in covenant faithfulness.

context: as it appointed unto man wants to die But after this the judgment
point_summary: Appointed death for every person leads inevitably to divine judgment, here distinguished as either the Bema seat for the redeemed or the great white throne for the lost, whose only verdict is the lake of fire.

context: He said this in Hebrews chapter 13, that he will never leave us, nor forsake us.
point_summary: Christ's promise in Hebrews 13 never to leave or forsake his people grounds the assurance that the same risen Savior who drew near to the Emmaus disciples still ministers to his people today.

context: come boldly to the throne of grace, why? So that we can obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need
point_summary: God's intimate knowledge of every believer's hidden sorrow is the ground for approaching the throne of grace boldly to obtain mercy and help in time of need.

context: he ever liveth to make intercession for us
point_summary: Christ's resurrection life grounds his ongoing, compassionate nearness to his people, as the living Savior who "ever liveth to make intercession" actively draws near to those who are overwhelmed and grieving.

context: Hebrews chapter number 12, looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Fixing one's gaze on Jesus, the author and finisher of faith, is the corrective to doubt, which enters when obstacles rather than the Lord become the focus of attention.

context: look unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Doubt creeps in when believers shift their gaze from Jesus—the author and finisher of faith—to their obstacles, making the circumstances appear greater than the Savior.

context: The same yesterday, today, and forever
point_summary: God's immutability — that He is "the same yesterday, today, and forever" — is the antidote to doubt that creeps in when believers fix their eyes on circumstances rather than on Christ.

context: He said that it's impossible to please Him without faith
point_summary: Doubt and unbelief are identified as what limits God's work in a believer's life, grounding the warning in the declaration that without faith it is impossible to please Him.

context: the law having a shadow of good things to come but the law was insufficient
point_summary: The animal sacrifice required to make the coats of skins is identified as the beginning of Old Testament typology, with Hebrews 10:1 confirming that the law's sacrificial system was only a shadow pointing forward to Christ's sufficient atonement.

context: as you study the book of Hebrews you'll find that the theme throughout the book of Hebrews is that Christ is better
point_summary: Hebrews' pervasive theme that Christ is better than all alternatives illuminates why God's provided way of salvation surpasses even the tree of life as a remedy for fallen humanity.

context: We're told in Hebrews in chapter two and verse number nine, the Lord Jesus Christ, that he tasted death for every man
point_summary: Christ's tasting death for every man (Heb. 2:9) is set alongside Isaiah 53 and Mark 15 to confirm that the substitutionary atonement pictured in Genesis 3's coats of skins finds its fulfillment in Jesus Christ.

context: without faith, it's impossible to please Him
point_summary: Unbelief, not divine limitation, is what derailed Israel's direct path from Egypt to Canaan, making Hebrews 11:6 the hinge on which the entire wilderness failure turns.

context: Turn to the book of Hebrews, if you will.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets hath in these last days spoken unto us by his son, whom he hath appointed heir of all things by whom also he made the world to being the brightness of his glory and the express image of his person
point_summary: God's progressive revelation—speaking through the prophets in times past and ultimately through His Son—is presented as the foundation for why ignoring divine instruction carries such grave consequence.

context: upholding all things by the word of his power when he had by himself purged our sins, sat down on the right hand of the majesty on high
point_summary: Christ's superiority over all things, established in Hebrews 1, grounds the urgent call in chapter 2 to give earnest heed to what has been heard lest it slip away.

context: God has spoken, he says in verse number one in various manners by through the prophets
point_summary: God's progressive revelation — spoken through prophets and now supremely through his Son — establishes the authority and superiority of Christ's word as the ground for earnest, obedient attention to Scripture.

context: verse number two about speaking in these last days by his son
point_summary: God's climactic speech through his Son in these last days establishes the superior revelation that Hebrews 2 then warns must not be neglected or allowed to slip away.

context: chapter two in verse number one, therefore, we ought to give the more earnest heed to the things which we have, what does it say? Heard, lest at any time we should let them slip
point_summary: Because God has spoken supremely through his Son—superior to all prior revelation—the "therefore" of Hebrews 2:1 demands a correspondingly greater, more earnest attention to what has been heard, lest it slip away.

context: in verse number one of chapter two, therefore, we ought to give the more, notice he says the more earnest heed to the things which we have heard, lest at any time we should let them slip
point_summary: Because Christ is superior to all that preceded him, the exhortation of Hebrews 2:1 to give "more earnest heed" to what has been heard carries proportionally greater weight than any prior word spoken through angels or prophets.

context: Verse two, for if the word spoken by angels was steadfast and every transgression and disobedience received a just recompense of reward, how shall we escape if we neglect so great salvation, which at the first began to be spoken by the Lord and was confirmed unto us by them that heard him
point_summary: Every transgression under the angel-mediated Mosaic law received just recompense, establishing the lesser-to-greater argument that neglecting the superior salvation spoken by Christ carries even graver consequence.

context: If you look at verse number four, you see that and how he confirmed the word
point_summary: God's confirmation of the gospel through signs and miracles (v. 4) is tied directly to the apostolic witness that "heard" Christ, establishing the chain by which salvation was validated.

context: chapter number 12 of the book of Hebrews
point_summary: Hebrews 12:25 is pressed as a warning that refusing the heavenly voice of Christ carries greater inescapable consequence than Israel's refusal of the earthly word, applied to personal disobedience that forfeits God's promised good.

context: see that ye refuse not him that speaketh. For if they escape not who refused him that spake on earth, much more shall not we escape if we turn away from him that speaketh from heaven
point_summary: Refusing to obey God's spoken instruction is not mere disobedience by softer names—it is refusing him that speaketh, carrying the same inescapable weight as Israel's rejection of God on earth.

context: You don't need to turn there but in Hebrews

context: for the Word of God is quick and powerful and sharper than a two-edged sword, piercing even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit and of the joints and marrow, is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart
point_summary: God's Word is described as "probing" — using Hebrews 4:12's image of Scripture as a discerner of thoughts and intents to explain why God questioned Adam and Eve, not from ignorance, but to bring them to recognition of their sin.

context: turn to the book of Hebrews, if you will, in the 11th chapter
point_summary: Jacob's lifelong limp, carried through his reunion with Esau and into Egypt before Pharaoh, is framed as a perpetual physical reminder of his encounter with God, with Hebrews 11 introduced to establish the years he lived after that night.

context: by faith Jacob, when he was a dying, this is when he's in Egypt, he blessed both the sons of Joseph and worshiped. Notice what the Bible says, leaning upon the top of his staff
point_summary: Jacob's lifelong limp, memorialized in the staff he leaned on at death, is presented as a physical reminder of his transformative night-encounter with God at Peniel.

context: Bible with you this evening, Hebrews chapter number 11
point_summary: Hebrews 11's gallery of faith-driven lives is being examined as evidence that God still works when unbelief is set aside, with Abraham and Sarah as the current focus.

context: Through faith also Sarah herself received strength to conceive seed and was delivered of a child when she was past age. Because she judged him faithful who had promised.
point_summary: Sarah's miraculous conception is grounded in her judgment that God is faithful to His promises, making trust in divine faithfulness the theological foundation of her faith.

context: what God is revealing to us here in Hebrews 11 about her faith
point_summary: Sarah's faith in Hebrews 11:11 is grounded in her judgment that God is faithful to His promises, even when her own condition—barrenness—made fulfillment humanly impossible.

context: through faith also Sarah
point_summary: Sarah's laughter at God's promise raises the question of when her doubt turned to faith, since Hebrews 11:11 credits her with the very faith she initially seemed to lack.

context: the writer of Hebrews says in Hebrews 11, in verse number 11, the latter part of the verse where the Bible says, because she judged him faithful who had promised. So it says, through faith also Sarah herself received strength to conceive seed and was delivered of a child when she was past age
point_summary: Sarah's laughter turned to faith when she judged God faithful who had promised, trusting that his declarative "I will" made his word trustworthy and certain of fulfillment.

context: Sarah. Why? Because she judged him faithful who had promised.
point_summary: Sarah's faith in Hebrews 11:11 is grounded in her judgment that God is faithful to His promises, presented here as the model response when God's Word conflicts with human logic or apparent impossibility.

context: when Hebrews 11 is being written, the Bible says that through faith also Sarah.
point_summary: Sarah's doubt turning to faith upon hearing God's spoken word is what secured her place in Hebrews 11's roll call as one who believed.

context: she judged him faithful who had promised
point_summary: Sarah's inclusion in the hall of faith rests on a single decisive act: she judged God faithful to the promise he had spoken, and her doubt gave way to trust.

context: He had this testimony before his translation that he pleased the Lord
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: If you read Hebrews chapter 11, you'll notice that all of those that are mentioned in God's hall of faith
point_summary: Hebrews 11's "hall of faith" demonstrates that biblical faith is never abstract but always expressed through concrete, obedient action — as each entry begins "by faith" and then records what that person did.

context: I want you to hold your place here and turn to the book of Hebrews in chapter 11
point_summary: Hebrews 11's "hall of faith" is cited to demonstrate that genuine faith is always evidenced by concrete action, as every figure listed is commended for what they did in response to believing God.

context: here in Hebrews 11 and verse number 7, the Bible says this, by faith Noah
point_summary: Faith, not yet validated by visible evidence, moved Noah to obedient action—building the ark in response to God's warning about an unseen flood—demonstrating that genuine faith always produces corresponding works.

context: by faith Noah, being warned of God of things, not seen as yet. Moved. He moved with fear. And here's what he did. He prepared an ark for the saving of his house
point_summary: Faith, not as abstract belief but as visible action, is the point drawn from Hebrews 11:7—Noah's being "moved" to prepare the ark demonstrates that genuine faith in God's unseen warning produces concrete, obedient response.

context: He that says He's gonna come, He's gonna come. He's not gonna tarry.
point_summary: God's promise that Christ will come and will not tarry stands regardless of human belief or philosophical skepticism.

context: Noah, he was warned of God of things that had not even been seen. He just believed God because God told him
point_summary: Faith's response to God's word — believing and obeying it even before the unseen becomes visible — is held up as the pattern Noah modeled and believers today must follow through Scripture, the more sure word of prophecy.

context: it's appointed unto man once to die
point_summary: Appointed death is the universal human reality, cited from Hebrews 9:27, framing why Hezekiah's divine death sentence—though sobering—is not the worst outcome, since absence from the body means presence with the Lord.

context: according to Hebrews 11 and verse number 3, that it is imperative that we accept the record by faith
point_summary: Accepting the Genesis creation account requires faith, as Hebrews 11:3 teaches, because the record of creation's week is received by faith rather than independent human reasoning.

context: God who at sundry times and in diverse manners spake in time past under the fathers by the prophets
point_summary: God's progressive revelation—speaking through prophets in former times and then finally through His Son—culminates in the Son as heir of all things and creator and sustainer of the world.

context: in these last days spoken unto us by his son
point_summary: Christ's role as Creator established in Hebrews 1:2 is inseparable from His role as Redeemer—to surrender the former is to undermine the latter.

context: whom he hath appointed heir of all things by whom also he made the world
point_summary: Christ's role as Creator established in Hebrews 1:2 is inseparable from His role as Redeemer—to surrender the former is to undermine the latter.

context: who being the brightness of his glory and express the image of his person and upholding all things by the word of his power
point_summary: Christ's role as Creator and Redeemer in Hebrews 1:3 are inseparable—surrendering the Genesis creation account undermines the very foundation of his redemptive work.

context: when he had by himself purged our sins sat down on the right hand of the majesty on high
point_summary: Christ's role as Creator and Redeemer are inseparable truths in Hebrews 1, so that surrendering the Genesis creation account opens the door to denying his redemptive work as well.

context: being made so much better than the angels as he hath by inheritance obtained a more excellent name than they

context: in Hebrews 1 1 through 4 that he speaks about Jesus Christ as the Creator
point_summary: Christ's identity as both Creator—upholding all things by the word of his power—and Redeemer is inseparable, so surrendering Genesis undermines the very basis for his atoning work.

context: by whom also he made the worlds in verse number 2
point_summary: Christ's identity as Creator in Hebrews 1:2 is inseparable from his role as Redeemer, so surrendering the Genesis creation account undermines the very foundation of redemption.

context: he's upholding all things by the word of his power
point_summary: Christ's identity as both Creator—upholding all things by the word of his power—and Redeemer is inseparable, so surrendering Genesis undermines the very basis for his atoning work.

context: go to Hebrews chapter 10 and you'll have a great time in the Word of God
point_summary: Christ's completed work of atonement — "one sacrifice for sins forever" — is presented as the direct counterpart to his completed work of creation, both finished and unrepeatable.

context: The law was a shadow of good things to come according to Hebrews chapter number 10.
point_summary: The Old Testament Feast of Tabernacles, with its priestly water-pouring ritual, was a shadow of good things to come that Christ fulfilled when He stood and declared Himself the source of living water.

context: I'm reminded of what Hebrews chapter number 11 and verse number 13 tells me that I'm a stranger, I'm a pilgrim, and I'm a stranger. This world is not my home, I'm just passing through
point_summary: Living in booths during the Feast of Tabernacles becomes a personal reminder that believers, like the patriarchs, are strangers and pilgrims whose provision and sustenance come entirely from God.

context: Hebrews 9 says, as it appointed unto man once to die, but after this, the judgment.
point_summary: Hebrews 9:27 is cited to establish that death and judgment are universal human appointments, underscoring why Christ's atoning gift—not human effort—is the only answer to that inescapable verdict.

context: the Bible speaks in Hebrews chapter number 10 about those priests that stood daily ministering
point_summary: The repeated Old Testament sacrifices described in Hebrews 10:1 could never remove sin but served as a picture pointing forward to the Messiah, illustrated here by Noah's burnt offerings on the altar.

context: Look over, if you will, at Hebrews chapter number 10 and I want you to notice in verse number 12 what the Word of God tells us
point_summary: Noah's burnt offerings—which enabled an unholy people to approach a holy God—were only a picture; Christ's single, final sacrifice for sins, after which He sat down, fulfilled and forever replaced what those repeated offerings could never perfect.

context: But we find in Hebrews 10 and verse number 12
point_summary: Noah's burnt offerings, which allowed an unholy people to approach a holy God, were only a shadow; Christ's single, finished sacrifice for sins—unlike the priests who never sat down—is complete and final.

context: but this man after he'd offered one sacrifice for sins forever sat down on the right hand of God
point_summary: Unlike the repeated animal sacrifices that could never perfect the worshipper, Christ's single sacrifice for sins was so complete that he sat down — a finished work requiring no priestly chair in the tabernacle.

context: Hebrews chapter number 13 in verse number 15
point_summary: Genuine worship that pleases God is defined here as a continual sacrifice of praise—the fruit of lips giving thanks—offered through Christ, not merely whatever activity people label as worship.

context: Verse 15 of Hebrews 13 By him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God Continually that is the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name
point_summary: Genuine praise offered continually through lips giving thanks constitutes a sacrifice that pleases God, just as Noah's offerings produced a sweet savor before the Lord.

context: the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name
point_summary: Continual praise offered through the lips is called a sacrifice that pleases God, just as Noah's burnt offerings after the ark produced a sweet-smelling worship that pleased the Lord.

context: without faith, it is impossible to please him
point_summary: Faith—not human ability—is what pleases God and moves Him to act, as Joshua and Caleb's confidence rested not in their own strength but in whether God delighted in them.

context: for he that cometh to God must believe that he is and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him
point_summary: Faith—not human ability—is what pleases God and secures His action, as Hebrews 11:6 is invoked to explain why Joshua and Caleb anchored their confidence in divine delight rather than their own merit.

context: looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith
point_summary: Fixing the eyes on Jesus, as the "author and finisher of our faith," is presented as a deliberate choice that conquers doubt by redirecting focus from overwhelming problems to the Lord.

context: He will never leave us nor forsake us
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Notice in Hebrews two in verse number nine, the Bible says, but we see Jesus,
point_summary: Christ's incarnation and death are inseparable purposes: he became lower than the angels specifically to taste death in the place of every person, paying sin's penalty he himself did not owe.

context: who was made a little lower than the angels
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for the suffering of death, crowned with glory and honor that he by the grace of God should taste death for every man
point_summary: Christ's sinless substitution — tasting death "for every man" — is presented as the basis by which he conquered death and destroyed the devil's power over it.

context: for as much then as the children are partakers of flesh and blood, he also himself likewise took part of the same, that through death, notice this, he might destroy him that had the power of death
point_summary: Christ's incarnation and death are shown to be the very means by which He destroyed the devil's power over death, liberating those who lived in bondage to its fear.

context: and deliver them who through fear of death were all their lifetime
point_summary: Christ's victory over death liberates believers from lifelong bondage to the fear of death, the very fear the preacher identifies as his own greatest dread before salvation.

context: he delivered them who through fear of death were all their lifetime subject to bondage
point_summary: Christ's resurrection conquest of death liberates believers from lifelong bondage to the fear of death, replacing that fear with confident hope in their own future resurrection.

context: Look back in Hebrews, we were in Hebrews 2 a moment ago. I want you to go back to Hebrews 2 and I want you to see, we talked about the fact that he conquered death and what a joy that is to know, but continue reading with me in Hebrews 2.
point_summary: Christ's incarnation as the seed of Abraham—not angels—was the necessary condition for him to pay for human sin, as confirmed by the singular "seed" of Galatians pointing to Christ.

context: For verily he took not on him the nature of angels, but he took on him the seed of Abraham
point_summary: Christ's incarnation as the seed of Abraham—not as an angel—was the necessary condition for atoning for human sin, since only a human could die as a merciful and faithful high priest to make reconciliation.

context: verse 17 of Hebrews two, wherefore in all things it behooved him to be made like unto his brethren that he might be, notice this, a merciful and faithful high priest in things pertaining to God, to make reconciliation for the sins of the people
point_summary: Christ's incarnation as the seed of Abraham—not as an angel—was the necessary qualification for Him to serve as a merciful and faithful high priest who could make reconciliation for the sins of the people.

context: Verse 18, for in that he himself hath suffered being tempted. Notice this, he is able to succor them that are tempted
point_summary: Because Christ himself suffered being tempted, he is able to succor—to help and come alongside—those who are tempted, making him a compassionate Savior who is our help in the time of need.

context: seeing then that we have a great high priest, aren't you thankful that he is our great high priest? It says this, that is passed into the heavens, Jesus, the son of God, let us hold fast our profession
point_summary: Christ's qualification as a sympathetic high priest—having been tempted in all points yet without sin—makes the throne of grace the believer's surest resource for mercy and help in times of suffering and need.

context: for we have not in high priest, which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities, but was in all points tempted like as we are yet without sin
point_summary: Christ's sinless experience of temptation qualifies him as a high priest who is genuinely touched by human suffering, making the throne of grace the believer's proper refuge in times of need.

context: Let us therefore come boldly under the throne of grace that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in the time of need
point_summary: Because Christ was tempted and suffered as a man, believers facing tribulation can approach the throne of grace with confidence, assured of receiving mercy and help precisely when they need it most.

context: for in the time of the ought to be teachers, you have need that one teach you

reference_text: strong meat, belonging to them who are of full age

point_summary: Spiritual maturity—moving from milk to strong meat—is the necessary defense against being led away by false teaching.

context: Look at Hebrews chapter 5, if you will. In Hebrews chapter 5, we think about this idea of developing spiritual goals.
point_summary: Spiritual drift results from lacking intentional goals, and Hebrews 5 is invoked as a call to examine whether one is genuinely growing closer to God over time.

reference_text: Hebrews chapter five, in verse number 11
context: We find in Hebrews chapter five, in verse number 11, think about this idea of having spiritual goals.
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11 is invoked as a warning against spiritual stagnation, illustrating that believers who lack growth goals remain perpetual beginners needing milk rather than advancing to maturity.

context: of whom we have many things to say and hard to be uttered, noticing you're dull of hearing. For when for the time you ought to be teachers, you have need that one teach you again, which be the first principles of the oracles of God. And it becomes such as have need of milk and not of strong meat. For everyone that useth milk is unskillful in the word of righteousness, free as a babe. Strong meat belonging to them that are full age, even those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil.
point_summary: Hebrews 5:11–14 is read as a warning against spiritual stagnation, measuring growth not by time elapsed but by deliberate progress from milk to strong meat.

context: And here we find that in Hebrews chapter five, the writer is saying here to these that he's writing to that the time was that they ought to be teachers
point_summary: Spiritual maturity is not automatic with time but requires intentional hunger for God's Word, as Hebrews 5 rebukes believers who remained immature when they should have become teachers.

context: not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together as the manner of some is, but exhorting one another and so much the more as you see the day approaching
point_summary: Gathering with believers is framed as a non-negotiable expression of putting God first, especially as the day of Christ's return draws nearer.

context: Without faith, it's impossible to please Him
point_summary: Faith that acts on God's word without visible precedent—illustrated by Moses striking the rock—is the kind that pleases God, since without it pleasing Him is impossible.

context: turn over to Hebrews 11 if you will

context: in the offering of Cain and Abel I want you to see what the New Testament says with regard to the offering that Abel brought here in Hebrews 11 and verse number 4

context: by faith Abel offered unto God a more excellent sacrifice than Cain by which he obtained witness that he was righteous God testifying of his gifts and by it he being dead yet speaketh
point_summary: Abel's offering is identified in Hebrews 11:4 as a faith offering, with that faith grounded in God's prior communication of the requirement — consistent with Romans 10:17 that faith comes by hearing the Word of God.

context: we would consider the testimony of the book of Hebrews with regard to the event that is recorded in Genesis chapter 4
point_summary: Hebrews 11:4 identifies Abel's offering as a faith offering, and linking that to Romans 10:17 establishes that God must have verbally communicated the requirement to Adam, who then passed it to his children.

context: the writer of Hebrews could say that his offering Abel's offering was by faith
point_summary: Abel's offering is called a faith offering in Hebrews 11:4 because God had verbally communicated the requirement for sacrifice to Adam, who then passed that word to his children, fulfilling Romans 10:17.

context: the law it had a shadow of good things to come according to Hebrews chapter number 10
point_summary: Abel's faith-based offering, accepted by God, is contrasted with the Levitical law's shadow of good things to come, fulfilled in Christ's one sacrifice for sin forever.

context: Jesus Christ made one sacrifice for sin forever according to Hebrews chapter number 10
point_summary: Abel's faith-driven offering is grounded in Hebrews 10's teaching that the Levitical law was a shadow of good things to come, fulfilled in Christ's one sacrifice for sin forever.

context: Hebrews 9 in verse 22 speaks about there's no remission without the shedding of blood
point_summary: Cain's bloodless offering was rejected because, as Hebrews 9:22 declares, there is no remission of sin without the shedding of blood, contrasted with Abel's accepted blood offering made in faith toward the coming Messiah.

context: by faith, Abel brought a more excellent sacrifice
point_summary: Abel's "more excellent sacrifice" is cited as proof that God sets the exclusive terms of acceptable worship — a blood offering made in faith — and that departing from those terms, as Cain did, reflects humanity's persistent tendency to approach God on its own terms.

context: How should we escape if we neglect so great salvation?
point_summary: Neglecting so great a salvation is the urgent warning invoked to press the congregation toward sharing, not hoarding, the one acceptable way of salvation through Christ.

context: if you study Psalm 101 and you look in Hebrews 7

context: Look at Hebrews chapter 13 just for a moment
point_summary: God's promise never to leave or forsake His people (Heb. 13:5) grounds the father's plea for help, since the one who cannot abandon us is the very helper being sought.

context: In Hebrews 13, in verse number five, the Bible says this, let your conversation be without covetousness and be content with such things as you have for he hath said, and this is the part that we often quote, I will never leave thee nor forsake thee.
point_summary: God's promise never to leave or forsake his people (Heb. 13:5a) grounds the declaration of verse 6 that "the Lord is my helper," linking his constant presence to his active availability in times of need.

context: verse six, may boldly say, the Lord is my helper. And I will not fear what man shall do unto me.

context: without faith it is impossible to please him, for he that cometh to God must believe that he is, and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him
point_summary: Faith is the indispensable condition for pleasing God and approaching Him, grounded in believing both that He exists and that He rewards those who diligently seek Him.

context: here in Hebrew 13 and verse number 4 notice what the Bible says marriage is honorable in all

context: by faith, Abraham, when he was tried, he offered up Isaac and he that had received the promises offered

context: without shedding of blood, there is no remission

context: We've referenced that verse in Hebrews 11 in verse number six several times. We find that the Bible tells us and teaches us the just shall live by faith.

context: Without faith, it's impossible to please him.
point_summary: Pleasing God requires faith, and Hebrews 11:6 is invoked repeatedly as the standard against which the disciples' failure—rooted in unbelief—is measured.

context: we need to have faith to please God
point_summary: Faith is the indispensable requirement for pleasing God, invoked here as the standard against which personal and corporate unbelief must be measured.

context: as it is appointed unto man, wants to die after this, the judgment
point_summary: Appointed death and subsequent judgment are presented as the certain, authoritative truth that drives the missionary urgency to warn every person before that day arrives.

context: I will never leave thee nor forsake thee
point_summary: God's promise never to leave or forsake His people grounds the bold confession "the Lord is my helper," freeing believers from fear of any circumstance or adversary.

context: the Lord is my helper. And I will not fear what man shall do unto me
point_summary: Hebrews 13:6 is cited as New Testament confirmation that God is our helper, grounding the believer's fearlessness in God's promised presence rather than in circumstances.

context: the deceitfulness of sin in the book of Hebrews
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth. Whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth
point_summary: Divine chastening, cited from Hebrews 12:6, is framed as an expression of God's love that disciplines believers toward obedience and trust rather than self-directed living.

context: We read the book of Hebrews and we find that he went out looking for a land. He didn't know where he was going, but God was bringing him
point_summary: Abram's obedient departure from Ur toward an unknown land illustrates that God's sovereign leading precedes full understanding, as the promise of inheritance was given before Abram possessed it.

context: Hebrews chapter 13 and verse number 15 talks about the sacrifice of our lips
